note: Records-lane overlay for the Erdős hub: bounded quantities, their known [L,U] state, and what a machine-verifiable WITNESS would be. Curated seed from the 2026-07-17 12-candidate triage (each number freshness-checked against OEIS + survey literature at triage time); extended by mining agents. Validate with tools/validate_gap_map.py. CANDIDATES, not targets: freshness-check the survey literature again before ANY claim (the #552 / f(39) lesson). [merged 208 mined entries 2026-07-18; entries below the 12-item curated seed are agent-mined, structurally validated, NOT yet independently number-re-verified]

entries.quantity: R(3,3,3,3) — 4-color Ramsey number of the triangle (= A003323(4))

lower.source: Chung lower-bound construction (per Radziszowski DS1)

upper.source: Fettes–Kramer–Radziszowski

entries.witness_object: a 4-coloring of E(K_n), n ≥ 51, with no monochromatic triangle (⇒ R ≥ n+1)
entries.witness_verifier: O(n^3): scan every triangle in each color class; dependency-free script
entries.notes: 50-year gap; even +1 on the lower bound would be the first movement since ~1971. DS1 rev.17 still lists 51–62; Wesley arXiv:2509.03784 (2025) improves other multicolor numbers, not this one. OEIS deleted the value in Aug 2021 'since not known'.

entries.quantity: A292528(6) — minimum vertices of a triangle-free graph with chromatic number 6

lower.source: Goedgebeur (J. Graph Theory 2020)

upper.source: Goedgebeur (J. Graph Theory 2020)

entries.witness_object: a triangle-free graph on ≤ 39 vertices with chromatic number 6
entries.witness_verifier: triangle-free in O(V·E); χ ≥ 6 via SAT-UNSAT certificate for 5-colorability; χ ≤ 6 via explicit coloring
entries.notes: [32,40] unmoved since 2017 against Goedgebeur's own world-class enumeration tooling (a(5)=22, Jensen–Royle 1995). Witness side is legitimate but expert-defeated.

entries.quantity: c_7 — largest line-free subset of {1,2,3}^7 (density Hales–Jewett, = A156989(7))

lower.source: Polymath1 (arXiv:1002.0374)

upper.source: Polymath1 (arXiv:1002.0374)

entries.witness_object: a subset of {1,2,3}^7 of size ≥ 1303 containing no combinatorial line
entries.witness_verifier: enumerate all 4^7 − 3^7 = 14197 line templates; check no line lies fully inside; trivial script
entries.notes: 16-year bracket; +1 on the lower bound would be the first movement since Polymath1. Exact value needs an optimality certificate that does not exist compactly.

entries.quantity: c'_7 — 7-dimensional Moser number (largest subset of [3]^7 with no geometric 3-in-line)
entries.witness_object: a geometric-line-free subset of [3]^7 (2187 cells)
entries.witness_verifier: enumerate the (5^7 − 3^7)/2 = 37969 geometric lines; check none lies inside the set; milliseconds
entries.notes: c'_6 = 353 (Polymath 2010); published c'_7 bounds NOT yet sourced here — a miner must cite current best lower/upper before any record attempt.

entries.quantity: N(10) — maximum number of mutually orthogonal Latin squares of order 10 (= A001438(10))

lower.source: Parker orthogonal pair

upper.source: classical bound (design-theory survey)

entries.witness_object: 3 mutually orthogonal Latin squares of order 10 (historic if found)
entries.witness_verifier: Latin property per square + pairwise orthogonality (all 100 ordered symbol pairs distinct); a few lines, dependency-free
entries.notes: Open 60+ years whether 3 MOLS(10) exist; 2022–2025 SAT campaigns eliminate relation types only (9 of the Dukes–Howard types remain).

entries.quantity: A394563(6) — least N such that every graph on N vertices has an induced regular subgraph of order ≥ 6

lower.source: Dyson–McKay (arXiv:2604.08215)

entries.witness_object: a graph on N ≥ 21 vertices with no induced regular subgraph of order ≥ 6 (⇒ a(6) ≥ N+1)
entries.witness_verifier: enumerate all vertex subsets of size ≥ 6; check none induces a degree-regular graph (~2^N subsets, cheap at N ≈ 21)
entries.notes: Witness verification is trivial; beating the bound means out-searching McKay's 2026 computation. Exact value needs a universal upper-bound proof — wall.

entries.quantity: A006560(7) — smallest starting prime of 7 consecutive primes in arithmetic progression (CPAP-7)

upper.source: Zimmermann existence witness (OEIS comments; primerecords.dk)

entries.witness_object: a CPAP-7 with a smaller start: 7 consecutive primes in arithmetic progression
entries.witness_verifier: primality of the 7 + consecutiveness (no intervening primes) + equal gaps (multiple of 210); milliseconds
entries.notes: Only one CPAP-7 known; any smaller one is a genuine record with trivial verification. The exact minimum needs an infeasible ~1e21 exhaustive sweep.

entries.quantity: A001661(9) — largest number not a sum of distinct positive 9th powers

lower.source: Wiener (OEIS comment)

entries.witness_object: a larger integer verified non-representable as a sum of distinct 9th powers
entries.witness_verifier: none cheap — non-representability requires a full subset-sum reachability DP (petabyte-scale at 9th powers)
entries.notes: Wiener estimates ~200,000 core-months to finish 9th powers. No cheap certificate on either side; excluded from record hunting.

entries.quantity: A362137(7) — smallest Paley tournament with property S_7 (7-paradoxical)

lower.value: > 1163 (= a(6); all smaller Paley primes fail)
lower.source: OEIS a(6), Bozóki/Fisher computation line

upper.source: Graham–Spencer criterion

entries.witness_object: a prime p ≡ 3 (mod 4) below the current best whose Paley tournament is 7-paradoxical
entries.witness_verifier: asymmetric: refuting a prime is cheap (exhibit one 7-subset with empty common-predecessor set), but CERTIFYING S_7 needs ~C(p,5) exact enumeration — as costly as the search itself
entries.notes: Extrapolated a(7) ≈ 4000–6000; brute exact check is days-to-weeks on one box. Needs a symmetry-reduced SAT/CP decision method before it is movable.

entries.quantity: A385316(6) — smallest N that is a sum of 3 prime cubes in exactly 6 ways

lower.source: this project, 2nd independent implementation (supervisor board > 1e12; OEIS author > 4.99e11)

entries.witness_object: n/a — exact-cell lane: a complete enumeration to a cap is a proof (every representation of V ≤ cap uses primes ≤ cap^(1/3))
entries.witness_verifier: sieve primes ≤ cap^(1/3); enumerate all unordered triples; histogram sums; validated by reproducing a(1..5) exactly
entries.notes: Naive all-in-RAM search is memory-bound (~20GB @ cap 2e12, ~100GB @ 1e13); needs a streaming chunked search.

entries.quantity: R_3(C_9) — 3-color Ramsey number of the 9-cycle (= A389335(9))

lower.source: known construction; equals the Bondy–Erdős conjectured value

entries.witness_object: n/a — the lower bound already meets the conjectured value; only the nonexistence (upper) side remains
entries.witness_verifier: n/a
entries.notes: 'The first currently open case' per the cycle-Ramsey survey. No compact CNF for exact-9-cycle-freeness (~2e12 clauses naive) — not even a drat-candidate today.

entries.quantity: A006585(9) — number of Egyptian-fraction solutions to 1 = 1/x_1 + … + 1/x_9
entries.witness_object: n/a — an exact count has no witnessable gap
entries.witness_verifier: none — a claimed count has no compact certificate; only a full replication run
entries.notes: a(8) = 151182379 (Dethridge 2004); a(9) expected ~1e12–1e13 solutions; 22 years stuck. Recorded so the lane knows to skip count-shaped problems.

entries.quantity: A276661(11) — least possible largest element of an 11-element set of positive integers whose 2^11 subset sums are pairwise distinct

lower.source: Dubroff–Fox–Xu, 'A note on the Erdős distinct subset sums problem', SIAM J. Discrete Math. 35(1):322–324 (2021), arXiv:2006.12988 — proves a_n >= C(n, floor(n/2)) for ALL n (verified by reading the paper PDF, p.1–2); C(11,5) = 462. Supersedes the hub deep-record lower of 310 (drop-largest from Dyson's a(10)=309).

upper.source: Conway–Guy construction: A005318(11) = 594; explicit witness {285,433,510,550,570,581,587,590,592,593,594} recorded in OEIS A276661 comments (M. S. Branicky, Jan 2026).

entries.witness_object: An 11-element set of positive integers with largest element <= 593 and all 2048 subset sums distinct
entries.witness_verifier: Enumerate all 2^11 = 2048 subset sums in exact integer arithmetic, sort, assert no duplicates. Milliseconds, dependency-free.
entries.notes: Exact terms a(0..10) = 0,1,2,4,7,13,24,44,84,161,309; a(10)=309 proven by P. W. Dyson (2025, exhaustive search, code on GitHub pwdyson/erdos_1). Conway–Guy matches a(n) exactly for all n<=10 and is conjectured optimal at n=11, which is why the upper-side witness is graded hard: recent OEIS activity (Popov Nov 2025) improved a(12)<=1157 and a(13)<=2249 but left 594 untouched. The live bracket is 462 <= a(11) <= 594. ACTION FOR INTEGRATOR: hub deep record atlas/problems.json#1 states lower 310 — update to 462 (DFX 2021). Exact closure of a(11) is a Dyson-style bounded backtracking campaign (no compact DRAT known; lower-bound claims need reproducible search transcripts). Headline $500 conjecture (N >> 2^n) is asymptotic and untouched by this cell. Jan 2026 arXiv:2601.07068 (greedy dissociated sets) checked: does not change these bounds.

entries.quantity: A003002(212) = r_3(212) — maximum size of a subset of {1,...,212} containing no 3-term arithmetic progression

lower.source: Monotone from a(211)=43 (Cariboni b-file b003002.txt, n=0..211, tail verified 2026-07-18); an explicit 43-element 3-AP-free subset of [1,212] is reported independently verified in arXiv:2606.04016 (Jun 2026).

upper.source: Trivial monotone step a(n+1) <= a(n)+1 with a(211)=43 (OEIS A003002 b-file).

entries.witness_object: A 44-element subset of {1..212} with no 3-term arithmetic progression (settles a(212)=44 and gives A065825(44) <= 212)
entries.witness_verifier: Bitset over [1,212]; for each pair x<y test whether 2y-x is in the set (or scan all C(44,3) triples). O(44^2) operations, dependency-free, microseconds.
entries.notes: The next-cell question is exactly whether A065825(44) <= 212 (A065825 has 43 terms, a(43)=209). Actively attacked: arXiv:2606.04016 (Jun 2026) runs a CP-SAT architecture on precisely this cell and reports a 'structural hard pocket' — integrator should check whether that paper is affiliated with our own farm before claiming the lane. Nonexistence side is a natural CNF; UNSAT+DRAT closes the cell. Sibling next cells for the same Erdős #3 family: A003003 (no 4-AP) known n<=142, A003004 (no 5-AP) n<=115, A003005 (no 6-AP) n<=147, all Cariboni b-files with extremal-set a-files — each first uncomputed cell is an analogous SAT/ILP target. The $5000 headline (divergent harmonic sum forces arbitrarily long APs) is asymptotic and untouched by any finite cell.

checked: OEIS A003002 (JSON) + b003002.txt tail (a(208..211) read directly)

entries.quantity: S = set of limit points of (p_{n+1}-p_n)/log n — conjecture that S = [0,infinity]
entries.witness_object: none — membership of a value C in S is a statement about infinitely many prime gaps; no finite object certifies a limit point
entries.witness_verifier: None exists: any finite prefix of the primes is consistent with both C in S and C not in S. erdosproblems.com marks this problem as not resolvable by finite computation.
entries.notes: Known partial results (paraphrased from erdosproblems.com/5 remarks, 2026-07-18): infinity is in S (Westzynthius 1931), 0 is in S (Goldston–Pintz–Yildirim 2009), S has positive Lebesgue measure (Erdős 1955; Ricci 1956), S contains arbitrarily large finite numbers (Hildebrand–Maier 1988), [0,c] subset of S for some c>0 (Pintz 2016), at least 1/3 of [0,infinity) lies in S and S has bounded gaps (Merikoski 2020), at least 12.5% (Banks–Freiberg–Maynard 2016). A001223 is the raw prime-gap sequence — fully computable, no uncomputed cell, no record quantity. Theorem-shaped frontier only; nothing for the records scout.

entries.quantity: Upper density of the set of odd numbers not representable as p + 2^a + 2^b (p prime) — is it positive?
entries.witness_object: none — individual non-representable odd numbers are cheap to verify (A006286 already has a 10000-term b-file) but no finite set of them moves the density question
entries.witness_verifier: A single term t is verifiable dependency-free: for every pair 2^a+2^b < t test t-2^a-2^b for compositeness. But the open content is the density exponent, which no finite witness certifies.
entries.notes: State of knowledge (paraphrased from erdosproblems.com/9, edited Apr 2026): Crocker (Pacific J. Math 36, 1971) proved infinitely many such odd integers, with >> log log N of them below N; Pan (arXiv:0905.3809) improved the count to >>_eps N^(1-eps) for every eps>0. The remaining gap — from exponent 1-eps to positive density — is theorem-shaped, not witness-shaped; Erdős believed covering-system arguments cannot settle it (representable numbers exist in every infinite AP). A006286 (terms 1,2,3,128,150,252,...; b-file to 10000 terms by Chai Wah Wu) is routinely extendable but extension is not a record. No bounded finite quantity with a gap.

entries.quantity: K* = least k (if it exists) such that every integer n >= 2 is the sum of a prime and at most k powers of 2; equivalently sup_n A387053(n)

lower.source: B. Grechuk's observation recorded on erdosproblems.com/10: 1117175146 is not the sum of a prime and at most 3 powers of 2. Recomputed and confirmed locally 2026-07-18 (all sums of <=3 powers of 2 below n, repeats allowed, n-s composite in every case; n itself composite).

entries.witness_object: An integer n with no representation n = p + (sum of at most 4 powers of 2) — would raise the lower bound to K* >= 5
entries.witness_verifier: Enumerate all sums s of at most 4 powers of 2 with s < n (about C(log2(n)+4,4) multisets — allow repeats to be convention-safe); deterministically primality-test n - s (BPSW/Miller–Rabin, proven deterministic below 3.3e24). All composite => verified. For n ~ 1e10 this is under 1e6 primality tests: seconds, dependency-free.
entries.notes: Existence of any finite K* is the open problem: Erdős called it 'probably unattackable'; Erdős–Graham (1980) lean toward no such k existing, so K* may be infinite (hence exact_feasibility=wall — only the lower bound is movable). Granville–Soundararajan (1998) conjecture 3 powers suffice for all odd n (hence 4 for even); Grechuk's even witness plus the abundance of A006286 suggests infinitely many even n need >= 4. Gallagher (1975): for any eps>0 some k(eps) covers a set of lower density >= 1-eps. A387053 (a(n) = least k for each n, offset 2; b-file to n=10001 by Corneth) gives the per-n table; recent related literature: Johnston–Trudgian, arXiv:2605.17825 (2026), on Linnik–Goldbach/Romanov constants. A k=4 lower-bound witness likely needs covering-congruence sieving over structured candidate families rather than brute scan — graded hard, not plausible.

checked: local recomputation of the 1117175146 non-representability (sympy isprime, repeats allowed)

entries.quantity: A377587(13) — smallest odd m such that m - 2^k is non-squarefree for every k = 1..13

lower.source: OEIS A377587 comment: no further terms below 2^50; terms nest (a condition holding for k<=13 holds for k<=12), so a(13) > 2^50. Consistent with Hercher, arXiv:2411.01964 (2024), who verified the underlying Erdős conjecture (every odd n>1 is squarefree + 2^k) for all odd n up to 2^50.

entries.witness_object: Any odd m > 2^50 together with a certificate list (k, p_k) for k=1..13 where p_k^2 divides m - 2^k (min-type quantity: any qualifying m upper-bounds a(13))
entries.witness_verifier: 13 modular divisibility checks p_k^2 | (m - 2^k) plus one parity check — instant, dependency-free, if the witness carries its square divisors. Without them: squarefree-test 13 numbers < m via trial division + Pollard rho (m ~ 1e15–1e18: seconds).
entries.notes: Erdős #11 asks if every odd n > 1 is squarefree + a power of 2; verified to 2^50 ~ 1.13e15 (Hercher 2024; earlier Odlyzko to 1e7). A377587 is the natural record ladder: a(1..12) = 11, 29, 533, 849, 434977, ..., 60690478781437; a(13) is the first uncomputed cell. Witnesses are found by covering systems on exponents (p^2 | m - 2^k propagates along k in arithmetic progressions of the order of 2 mod p^2), the same machinery that produced a(12) — hence plausible, not easy. Exact value = bounded upward sweep continuing Hercher's method (compute-heavy but cell-shaped). Deep connection: Granville–Soundararajan (1998) tie a negative answer to Wieferich primes (A001220: only 1093 and 3511 known; OEIS records no others below ~5e17 per PrimeGrid, search later suspended). A counterexample to the conjecture itself (odd m failing for ALL k with 2^k < m) would close the problem negatively and is fully machine-verifiable — but none is expected.

entries.quantity: Size of {1..N} minus B, where B = integers representable in exactly one way as a sum of two elements of an infinite set A — conjectured >>_eps N^(1/2-eps) for every A
entries.witness_object: none — the quantified object is an infinite set A and an all-large-N asymptotic; no finite witness bears on it
entries.witness_verifier: None exists: both the universal claim (over all infinite A) and the density exponent are asymptotic. erdosproblems.com marks the problem as not resolvable by finite computation.
entries.notes: Context (paraphrased from erdosproblems.com/14, edited Sep 2025): attributed to Erdős–Nathanson / Erdős–Sárközy–Szemerédi; Erdős claimed a construction with complement <<_eps N^(1/2+eps) yet with infinitely many N where the complement is >>_eps N^(1/3-eps). The only finite handle is the Erdős–Freud (1991) finite analogue: some A in {1..N} leaves fewer than 2^(3/2)*sqrt(N) integers not uniquely representable, with 2^(3/2) suggested optimal — an untabulated constant-optimization that could seed a future OEIS-style table, but no maintained table exists today. The stub's linked A143824 (max Sidon subset of {1..n}; b-file n=0..500 by Sloane; ILP formulation by Pratt in comments) is tagged only 'possible' relevance on the problem page; its next cell a(501) is a computable ILP target but belongs to the Erdős #30 Sidon lane, not #14.

entries.quantity: Are there infinitely many cluster primes (odd p such that every even number <= p-3 is a difference of two primes both <= p)?
entries.witness_object: none — a single additional cluster prime is poly-time checkable (sweep all prime differences below p) but moves no recorded bound; infinitude admits no finite witness
entries.witness_verifier: Membership check for one p: sieve primes <= p, mark all pairwise differences, verify every even number <= p-3 is marked — O(pi(p)^2) but trivial for any p a search could reach. No maintained largest-cluster-prime record exists to improve.
entries.notes: Blecksmith–Erdős–Selfridge (Amer. Math. Monthly 106 (1999) 43–48) proved cluster primes are sparse: pi_C(x) <<_A x/(log x)^A for every A, and the sum of reciprocals of cluster primes converges (direction confirmed via MathWorld ClusterPrime). Elsholtz (2003) improved to << x*exp(-c(log log x)^2) for every c < 1/8 (per erdosproblems.com/17). First non-cluster prime is 97; A038133 lists non-cluster primes (b-file 10000 terms, T. D. Noe). Tao has flagged the problem as difficult on the erdosproblems page. Pure existence question — no bounded quantity with a gap; do not board.

entries.quantity: h(m) for practical m (fewest distinct divisors of m that always suffice to represent every 1 <= n < m) — are there infinitely many practical m with h(m) < (log log m)^O(1)? Also h(n!) < n^o(1)?
entries.witness_object: none — the $250 question quantifies over infinitely many m (and all large n for the factorial variant); a single (m, h(m)) evaluation moves no bound
entries.witness_verifier: h(m) for one m is computable (worst-case min-cardinality subset-sum over the divisors of m) but expensive for interesting m like n!, and no OEIS table of h-values exists to extend. erdosproblems.com marks the problem as not resolvable by finite computation.
entries.notes: Known (paraphrased from erdosproblems.com/18, edited Apr 2026): Erdős showed h(n!) < n; Vose (1985) proved infinitely many practical m have h(m) << (log m)^(1/2); the $250 reward (Erdős 1981) is for h(m) < (log log m)^O(1) infinitely often; strengthenings h(n!) < n^o(1) or (log n)^O(1) also open. A005153 (practical numbers; b-file 10000 terms, Eldar/Noe) is definitionally computable with no uncomputed cell — the sequence itself carries no record. A tabulation of h(n!) for small n would be new but is uncharted (no defended record), heavy per term, and moves no stated bound. Related problems 304, 825 on the same page.

entries.quantity: A332077 cell Sun(3,4) — minimal number of distinct sets of cardinality <= 3 forcing a 4-sunflower (first uncomputed cell of the sunflower-number table, in OEIS antidiagonal reading order)

lower.source: Abbott–Exoo, 'On Set Systems Not Containing Delta Systems', Graphs and Combinatorics 8 (1992): a 38-triple family with no 4-sunflower, so Sun(3,4) >= 39; as audited in hub deep record atlas/problems.json#20 (2026-07-13; no 2023–2026 improvement found).

upper.source: Erdős–Rado sunflower lemma bound as recorded in the OEIS A332077 formula section: Sun(m,n) <= (n-1)^m * m! + 1; (4-1)^3 * 3! + 1 = 163.

entries.witness_object: A family of >= 39 distinct sets, each of cardinality <= 3, containing no 4 sets whose pairwise intersections are all equal (raises Sun(3,4) >= family size + 1)
entries.witness_verifier: For N sets enumerate all C(N,4) 4-subsets (N=39: 82251) and check whether the six pairwise intersections coincide; core-bucketing reduces this further. Exact, dependency-free, sub-second.
entries.notes: Table state (OEIS example section): row m=1 and columns n<=2 trivial; row m=2 closed form (Abbott–Hanson–Sauer); Sun(3,3)=21 proven (Erdős–Rado; 20-set extremal family in the OEIS a-file); data stops exactly at Sun(3,4). Sibling open cells with 30–50-year-old construction records: Sun(4,3) >= 55 (Abbott–Hanson–Sauer 1972, 54-set family) and Sun(3,5) >= 89 (Abbott–Hanson 1974, 88 triples), both per hub deep audit atlas/problems.json#20. Witness search is SA-seeding + ILP/MaxSAT over a swept ground set (deep atlas: hours to seed, days–weeks for fixed-g optima); records are sticky structured Exoo-class constructions — plausible, not easy. Exact value graded wall: the ground set is unbounded, so certifying the upper side needs a ground-set-size theorem, not a fixed-vertex DRAT closure. The $1000 headline (f(n,k) < c_k^n) is asymptotic and untouched; recent progress (Rao; Bell–Chueluecha–Warnke (Cs log k)^k) is all on the asymptotic upper side. Keyword 'hard,more' on the OEIS entry confirms open table status.

checked: OEIS A332077 (JSON: data, formula, example, keywords)
atlas/problems.json#20 (hub deep audit 2026-07-13, incl. AHS 1972 / Abbott–Exoo 1992 / Abbott–Hanson 1974 cell records and 2023–2026 no-change check)

entries.quantity: A389646(24) — maximum over triangle-free graphs on 24 vertices of the minimum number of edge deletions needed to make the graph bipartite (first uncomputed term; a(23)=20 is the last known value)

lower.source: a(23)=20 (B. McKay, OEIS A389646 comment, Oct 2025) plus monotonicity a(n) >= a(n-1) (isolated-vertex argument); also forced by a(25)=25 (Ferudun) with the Fuller recursion a(25) <= a(24)+5

upper.source: Fuller recursion a(n) <= a(n-1) + n/5 (OEIS A389646 comment / SeqFan Oct 2025): a(24) <= 20 + 24/5

entries.witness_object: A triangle-free graph G on 24 vertices with certified minimum bipartization >= 21. Candidate generators: C5 blow-ups with class sizes summing to 24 plus edge-deleted variants (the a(23)=20 extremal graph is a (4,5,4,5,5) blow-up plus 5 subgraphs, per McKay).
entries.witness_verifier: Dependency-free two-step check: (1) triangle-freeness via trace(A^3)=0 or an O(n^3) loop; (2) exact min bipartization = |E| - maxcut by brute force over 2^23 ~ 8.4M bipartitions x <=144 edges — seconds to minutes in plain C.
entries.notes: Headline: do n^2/25 deletions always suffice on 5n vertices (sharp via balanced C5 blow-up)? Best general upper: a(n) <= n^2/23.5 (Balogh-Clemen-Lidicky, arXiv:2103.14179). Ferudun (arXiv:2606.28041, Jun 2026) proved a(5k)=k^2 for all k<=40, i.e. the conjectured value holds at n=25,30,...,200 — so the live machine lane is non-multiples of 5, starting at a(24) in [20,24]. Caution: the (5,5,5,5,4) blow-up heuristic gives only 20, so a witness >20 may not exist if a(24)=20; the cell itself looks reachable by McKay-style enumeration (a(23) fell Oct 2025).

entries.quantity: A003022(29) — length of the optimal (shortest) 29-mark Golomb ruler; first unproven cell after OGR-28 (a(28)=585, proved Nov 2022)

lower.source: Strict monotonicity from a(28)=585, proved optimal by the distributed.net OGR-28 exhaustive search completed 2022-11-23 (OEIS A003022; Wikipedia Golomb-ruler optimal table)

upper.source: Best-known 29-mark ruler, projective-plane construction p=29, Atkinson-Hassenklover 1984 (J. B. Shearer, IBM table of shortest known rulers, web.archive.org snapshot 2018; no improvement found in 2024-2026 searches)

entries.witness_object: A 29-mark Golomb ruler of length < 623: integers 0 = m1 < ... < m29 = L < 623 with all C(29,2) = 406 pairwise differences distinct.
entries.witness_verifier: O(k^2): enumerate the 406 pairwise differences and assert distinctness — microseconds, no dependencies.
entries.notes: The $1000 headline (h(N) = sqrt(N) + O_eps(N^eps) for Sidon sets) is asymptotic and not finitely computable. Every proven-optimal ruler from 10 to 28 marks matched a Singer/Bose/affine-projective construction; Rokicki-Dogon offer $250 per beaten construction length (36+ marks) and report no beats — so beating 623 is construction-defeating territory. The nonexistence side (proving 623 or anything optimal) is a distributed.net-scale exhaustion with no compact certificate (OGR-28: 8.5 years on a global grid). Alternative cheap-witness lane from atlas deep record (atlas/problems.json#30): the rational-certificate constant b in h(N) <= sqrt(N) + b*N^{1/4} + O(1), currently 0.9435 (arXiv 2607.01169, Jul 2026; prior 0.97633 AlphaEvolve-assisted 2025, 0.98183 Carter-Hunter-O'Bryant 2025) — a finite convex-QP dual certificate checkable in exact arithmetic in seconds. Companion sequences A143824 (max Sidon subset of {1..n}, b-file to 500) and A227590.

entries.quantity: Erdős minimum-overlap constant mu = lim M(n)/n, where M(n) = A393584(n) is the minimax, over equal partitions {X,Y} of {1..2n}, of the maximum number of pairs (x,y) with x-y equal to a fixed difference

lower.source: White [Wh22] (Acta Arith. 208 (2023) per project registry; cited as the current lower record on erdosproblems.com/36)

upper.source: Exact-rational certificate for the Hyra n=1024 admissible step construction — this project's DOI'd v1.2 note (results_registry id erdos-min-overlap-upper-bound); tighter than the 0.380876 (TTT-Discover LLM [YKLBMWKCZGS26]) listed on erdosproblems.com/36 as of its 2026-01-23 edit

entries.witness_object: An equal partition {X,Y} of {1..2n} (equivalently an admissible step construction) whose exact-rational max difference-overlap divided by n beats 0.38085942... Subadditivity a(kn) <= k*a(n) (Dobbelaere, OEIS comment) makes any finite witness a valid upper bound on mu.
entries.witness_verifier: O(n^2) exact-integer difference count: for each difference d, count pairs x in X, y in Y with x-y=d; take the max; compare as exact rationals. In-repo: p42 min-overlap verifier v0.2.0 (any n <= 4096; 1.23 s at n=4096; 8/8 tests).
entries.notes: OEIS terms known to n=33 (a(33)=13; a(n)=floor(5n/13) for small n) — next uncomputed cell a(34). Bound history: trivial 1/4; Scherk 1-1/sqrt(2); Haugland 2016 upper 0.3809268534330870; AlphaEvolve [GGTW25] then TTT-Discover [YKLBMWKCZGS26] to 0.380876; project v1.2 exact-rational record 0.38085942 (tightest repaired variant 0.38085906 needs a sub-ULP admissibility repair, not admissible outright). The lower side (raising 0.379005) needs a proof, not a witness — asymmetric gap. mu itself is an analytic limit, flagged 'cannot be resolved with a finite computation' upstream. External submission of the project record is pending human review; upstream page not yet reflecting it.

checked: results_registry/results.json id erdos-min-overlap-upper-bound (DOI'd, verifier command present)

entries.quantity: Existence of an infinite set A of totient values such that n_a/a -> infinity, where n_a is the least n with phi(n) = a (no single bounded numeric quantity attached)
entries.witness_object: No finite witness decides the problem: any finite list of totient values a with large ratio n_a/a is exactly checkable via inverse-totient computation, but the claim quantifies over an infinite set — neither a witness nor a counterexample is finite. A 'champions of n_a/a' record table is computable (cf. A014197 b-file to 10000, Alekseyev's invphi.gp) but carries no decision content.
entries.witness_verifier: Per single value a: enumerate all n with phi(n)=a (invphi / sieve with a provable search bound) and confirm the minimum — exact and cheap; verifies finite data only.
entries.notes: Pure existence question; erdosproblems.com/51 explicitly marks it not resolvable by finite computation. Adjacent famous open: Carmichael's conjecture (A014197 contains no 1; Erdős proved one such t would force infinitely many). Both linked OEIS sequences are fully computed in their published ranges (A002202 b-file to 10000, A014197 b-file to 10000) — no uncomputed cell exists to attack.

entries.quantity: Integer sum-product exponent delta* = sup{delta : max(|A+A|,|AA|) >> |A|^{delta - o(1)} for all finite A subset of Z} (Erdős–Szemerédi conjecture: delta* = 2)

lower.source: Cushman [Cu25] (arXiv:2512.13849), current record per erdosproblems.com/52 (page edited 2026-05-28); supersedes Bloom's 1270/951

upper.source: Trivial max <= |A|^2; Erdős–Szemerédi 1983 give sets with max(|A+A|,|AA|) <= |A|^2 exp(-c log|A|/loglog|A|), so 2 is not attained with a constant — the conjecture is delta* = 2

entries.witness_object: No finite witness moves the exponent — the $250 question is asymptotic (upstream: not resolvable by finite computation); even the 2026 real-case refutation required an infinite construction family plus proof. The finite shadow A263996(n) (min |A+A ∪ AA| over n-sets of positive integers) is a genuine record table but contest-saturated (Al Zimmermann 'Sums and Products' 2015-16, 1639 entries) and analytically disconnected from delta*.
entries.witness_verifier: Finite shadow only: given an n-set, compute all pairwise sums and products (bignum), dedupe, count the union — O(n^2) exact. No computation verifies a delta* claim.
entries.notes: MAJOR 2026 development caught this pass: Bloom–Sawin–Schildkraut–Zhelezov [BSSZ26] (arXiv:2605.28781) DISPROVED the conjecture for A subset of R — arbitrarily large real sets with max(|A+A|,|AA|) <= |A|^{2-c} — and for small subsets of F_p and the higher-fold real variant. The integer case (this problem) remains open, and the best real/integer lower bound is Cushman's 1962/1469. Atlas deep record atlas/problems.json#52 is STALE on both counts (still cites Bloom 1270/951 and predates the refutation) — recommend an update task. A263996 exact/best-known terms with b-file to n=205.

entries.quantity: A006855(41) = ex(41; C4) — maximum edges in a C4-free graph on 41 vertices (first uncomputed term; exact values end at a(40)=127)

lower.source: B. McKay, OEIS A006855 comment (Mar 2022); Afzaly–McKay extremal tables list >=3 witness graphs at 41 vertices / 132 edges in sparse6

upper.source: M. Alekseyev, OEIS A006855 comment (Jan 2023), via a(n) <= floor(a(n-1)*n/(n-2)): floor(127*41/39) = 133

entries.witness_object: A C4-free graph on 41 vertices with 133 edges (closes the cell at 133). More accessible variants: beat McKay's best-known at n=46..49 where gaps are wide — a(46) in [157,165], a(47) in [163,171], a(48) in [168,176], a(49) in [174,182].
entries.witness_verifier: Compute B = A^2 and assert every off-diagonal entry <= 1 (no two vertices share two common neighbors = no C4) — O(n^3) integer matrix multiply, dependency-free, instant at n<=49.
entries.notes: Headline #60 is a supersaturation conjecture (any n-vertex graph with > ex(n;C4) edges contains >> sqrt(n) copies of C4; He–Ma–Yang proved it for n = q^2+q+1 with q even; upstream flags it not finitely computable) — the linked A006855 table is the machine lane, and ex(n;C4) proper is problem #765's subject. McKay marks his 41-45 lower bounds as 'good chance of being exact', so the 133-edge witness at n=41 may not exist; the wider 46-49 gaps are the honest hunting ground. Cell graded 'unknown' rather than drat-candidate: enumeration reached n=40, but the 1-edge gap at n=41 has stood 3+ years against McKay and Alekseyev, and a SAT nonexistence cert (820 edge vars, ~3*10^5 C4 clauses + cardinality 133, heavy symmetry) is unproven at this scale.

entries.quantity: Irrationality status of E = Sum_{n>=2} 1/(n!-1) = 1.25349875569995347... (decimal expansion = A331373)
entries.witness_object: No finite witness exists in either direction: irrationality is a yes/no property with no cheap certificate; the digits are computable to arbitrary precision (b-file: 10000 digits) and decide nothing. Even a record-shaped proxy (irrationality-measure or continued-fraction statistics) would require proofs, not computation.
entries.witness_verifier: None; the only checkable artifact is recomputation of digits from the rapidly converging series, which is trivial and non-decisive.
entries.notes: Confirmed still open as of 2026. Active adjacent literature makes this worth a literature-watch flag rather than a compute lane: Tao's Ahmes-series irrationality results (arXiv:2406.17593, 2024) and the 2026 resolution of an Erdős–Graham rapidly-converging-series irrationality question (arXiv:2601.21442) — neither covers Sum 1/(n!-1). The linked OEIS entry is a decimal expansion, so there is no uncomputed cell in the gap-map sense.

checked: web search: arXiv:2601.21442, arXiv:2406.17593, erdosproblems forum irrationality blog (2026-07-18)

entries.quantity: C = lim_{k->infinity} R(k)^{1/k}, the diagonal Ramsey growth base (the limit's existence is itself open; bounds below are for liminf/limsup)

lower.source: Erdős 1947 probabilistic bound R(k) >= 2^{k/2}, giving liminf >= sqrt(2) (per erdosproblems.com/77, page edited 2026-02-08)

upper.source: Gupta–Ndiaye–Norin–Wei [GNNW24], improving Campos–Griffiths–Morris–Sahasrabudhe's 4 - 1/128 [CGMS23] (per erdosproblems.com/77)

entries.witness_object: No finite object moves the base: a Ramsey-graph witness at any fixed order (e.g. a 43-vertex mono-K5-free 2-coloring giving R(5,5) >= 44) improves one A059442 cell and contributes zero digits to the limit. Feeder cell state: R(5,5) in [43 (Exoo 1989), 46 (Angeltveit–McKay 2024)]; this project's own R(5,5) witness hunt closed with no witness at 43 (DRAT asymmetry certs published).
entries.witness_verifier: Feeder cells only: verify a 2-colored K_n has no monochromatic K_k by enumerating C(n,k) subsets — polynomial for fixed k (the project's existing R(5,5) verifier). Nothing verifies a claim about C itself.
entries.notes: $250 for the value; the same page records Erdős's separate $100 offer for mere existence of the limit (and joke prizes for nonexistence). Erdős speculated the value might be 2 with 'no real evidence'. Simpler/multicolor 4-c proof: Balister et al. [BBCGHMST24]. Upstream explicitly: not resolvable by finite computation. Deep record atlas/problems.json#77 concurs (wall; our SAT+DRAT/MIS tooling mechanism-matches only the analytically disconnected feeder cells). Its '3.8' upper is consistent with the page's 3.7992.

checked: project registry / R(5,5) campaign memory (closed, no witness)

entries.quantity: Constructive exponential Ramsey lower bound: an explicit family certifying R(k) > C^k for some C > 1 — equivalently explicit n-vertex graphs with clique and independence number O(log n)
entries.witness_object: No finite witness: at any single k, 'constructive' is vacuous (any exhibited coloring is a construction), so the problem demands an explicit infinite family plus proof. Frontier objects are extractor-based explicit Ramsey graphs: Cohen 2015 achieved no clique/independent set of size >= 2^{(loglog n)^C}; Li [Li23b] improved this to (log n)^C — still polylogarithmic, versus the O(log n) required. This is a theoretical-CS derandomization frontier, not a search-and-verify lane.
entries.witness_verifier: None cheap: certifying clique/independence bounds for the relevant graph sizes is itself computationally hard, and no single graph decides the asymptotic claim.
entries.notes: $100 prize. Erdős's probabilistic R(k) >> k 2^{k/2} is nonconstructive; the ask is an explicit C^k family. Distinct from #77 (which asks the VALUE of lim R(k)^{1/k}) despite the shared A059442 link — verified both pages directly to disambiguate. Page last edited 2026-01-23; upstream flags it not resolvable by finite computation. No OEIS cell to attack (A059442's own frontier cells like R(5,5) belong to #77's feeder discussion and to the small-Ramsey lane already covered by project campaigns).

entries.quantity: A006672(39) = R(C4, K_{1,39}) — first literature-open cell of the C4-vs-star Ramsey table (the finite shadow of #85's min-degree function f(n); R(C4,K_{1,n}) = min{m : f(m) <= m-n})

lower.source: Wu-Sun-Radziszowski 2015 lower-bound coloring, listed OPEN 46-47 in Radziszowski DS1 rev.18; witness re-derived in-project 2026-07-17 (project_ramsey_f39 reconciliation after retraction of an '=46' claim)

upper.source: Parsons 1975 bound R(C4,K_{1,n}) <= n + floor(sqrt(n-1)) + 2 (39+6+2=47); bracket confirmed in DS1 rev.18 and consistent with Boza arXiv:2409.12770 (all n<=38 now exact)

entries.witness_object: A red/blue edge-coloring of K_46 with no red C4 and no blue K_{1,39}; equivalently a C4-free graph H on 46 vertices with min degree >= 7 (blue = complement). Existence pins R(C4,K_{1,39}) = 47.
entries.witness_verifier: Dependency-free: check H has 46 vertices, min degree >= 7, and no C4 (all pairs of vertices share <= 1 common neighbor; O(n^3) via adjacency matrix). Milliseconds.
entries.notes: Headline #85 (is f(n+1) >= f(n) for all large n?) is explicitly not finitely resolvable (erdosproblems.com/85); known f(n) = (1+o(1))sqrt(n). The gap-shaped surface is the shared A006672 table: OEIS is stale (11 terms) but literature is complete through n=38 (Boza 2024 arXiv:2409.12770 closed the last 8 values <= 38), so n=39 is the first open cell. CAUTION: this exact cell caused this project's 07-16 retraction; the reconciled state is OPEN {46,47}. Project SAT deciders on the (46, min-deg-7) instance were killed after 5.5h with no result (2026-07-17) — graded beyond current tools. Table also cross-linked to hub #552/#242 lane.

checked: DS1 rev.18 bracket via project reconciliation 2026-07-17 (project_ramsey_f39 memory)
WebSearch: Radziszowski small Ramsey numbers C4 star

entries.quantity: A245762(7) = ex(Q_7, C4) — maximum edges in a C4-free subgraph of the 7-dimensional hypercube (first uncomputed term; exact known through ex(Q_6,C4)=132)

lower.source: Minamoto, arXiv:2603.29127 (v4 May 2026): explicit constructions certified by exhaustive enumeration of all 672 four-cycles of Q7; 19866 distinct 304-edge solutions found

upper.source: DERIVED elementary layer-splitting bound: ex(Q7,C4) <= 2*ex(Q6,C4) + 2^6 = 2*132 + 64, using ex(Q6,C4)=132 (Harborth-Nienborg 1994; ILP re-proof in arXiv:2603.29127). No better published Q7 upper bound found; the paper's ILP did not close Q7

entries.witness_object: Edge list of a C4-free subgraph of Q7 with >= 305 edges (vertices = 7-bit strings, edges = Hamming-distance-1 pairs).
entries.witness_verifier: Enumerate the 672 fixed 4-cycles of Q7 (C(7,2)*2^5 = 672) and assert none has all 4 edges present; count edges >= 305. O(672) checks, microseconds, no dependencies.
entries.notes: $100 prize attaches only to the asymptotic Turan-density conjecture (density -> 1/2?), pinned in [1/2, 0.60318] (lower Brass-Harborth-Nienborg 1995; upper Baber flag algebras, per erdosproblems.com/86) — unclaimable by finite computation. Finite frontier: lower record 304 was just set (May 2026) by heavy two-phase simulated annealing with Aut(Q7) diversification, so beating it with the same tools has no edge (hub deep record grades wall-ish). The unattempted novel target is UNSAT+DRAT at 305 edges (448 edge vars, 672 four-cycle clauses, cardinality >= 305, lex-leader symmetry breaking over |Aut(Q7)|=645120) => first proven term past n=6; instance is small but hardness genuinely unknown (paper's ILP failed on Q7). Q8: lower 680 (same paper), computational evidence for equality, almost certainly a wall.

entries.quantity: inf{R(G) : chi(G)=k} relative to the diagonal Ramsey number R(k), as k -> infinity — whether R(G) > (1-eps)^k R(k) (or > c*R(k)) for every G of chromatic number k
entries.witness_object: None for the stated problem: it is universally quantified over all graphs of chromatic number k for all large k, so no finite example settles it. (A finite curiosity exists: a chi(G)=k graph with R(G) < R(k), like Faudree-McKay's pentagonal wheel with R=17 < R(4)=18, only refutes the long-dead strong form R(G) >= R(k).)
entries.witness_verifier: No cheap verifier: even the sub-record min{R(G) : chi(G)=k} requires computing a Ramsey number per candidate G (itself exponential) plus a chromatic-number certificate; not a commodity check.
entries.notes: erdosproblems.com/87 marks this not resolvable by finite computation. Known: original conjecture R(G) >= R(k) fails at k=4 (Faudree-McKay 1993, pentagonal wheel R=17 vs R(4)=18); trivial for eps >= 3/4 since R(k) <= 4^k; Wigderson observed R(G) >> 2^{k/2} for chi(G)=k, matching best-known diagonal lower bounds — so progress is tied to the (asymptotic) diagonal Ramsey problem itself (cf. Campos-Griffiths-Morris-Sahasrabudhe 2023 exponential improvement). OEIS link A059442 (the R(n,k) table, flagged 'possible' by the hub) is the wrong record surface for THIS problem: its famous open cells (e.g. R(5,5) in [43,46], Angeltveit-McKay) belong to hub #77/#78/#545/#986, and this project's R(5,5) witness hunt is already CLOSED-published.

entries.quantity: A131628(7) = g(7) — maximum number of points in a planar 7-distance set (first open cell; controls the first genuinely open cell A186704(17) of the min-distinct-distances table)

lower.source: Ahmed-Snevily, ElJC 20(4) #P33 (2013): explicit 16-point triangular-lattice 7-distance set (g(7) >= 16), verified by exhaustive search within lattice families; construction implicit in Erdos-Fishburn 1996

upper.source: Blokhuis-type bound for s-distance sets in R^d: |X| <= C(d+s, d) = C(9,2) = 36 for s=7, d=2

entries.witness_object: A set of >= 17 points in the plane with exactly 7 distinct pairwise distances (coordinates as exact rationals/algebraics; if in the triangular lattice, integer certificates via squared distance a^2+ab+b^2). Any such set beats the 16-point record and, if non-lattice-similar with 17+ points, refutes the Erdos-Fishburn lattice conjecture for k>=7.
entries.witness_verifier: Compute all C(n,2) squared distances in exact arithmetic and count distinct values == 7. O(n^2), dependency-free, exact.
entries.notes: Headline #89 ($500) is the asymptotic distinct-distances conjecture (>> n/sqrt(log n)); Guth-Katz 2015 proved >> n/log n — not finitely resolvable. Finite tables: g(k) known only for k<=6: 3,5,7,9,12,13 (Erdos-Fishburn 1996; g(6)=13 Wei 2012). Librarian note: A186704 (OEIS stops at a(13)=6) extends by pure literature synthesis — a(14)=a(15)=a(16)=7 (>= 7 since g(k)<=13 for k<=6; <= 7 via 14/15/16-point subsets of the 16-point 7-distance set); the first genuinely open cell is A186704(17), which equals 7 iff g(7) >= 17. Erdos-Fishburn conjecture: for k>=7 all maximum k-distance sets are lattice-similar; Ahmed-Snevily present data suggesting it may be false. Upper 36 is the generic s-distance bound; no sharper published planar bound for k=7 verified.

checked: Ahmed-Snevily ElJC 20(4)#P33 PDF (read 2026-07-18: g(7)>=16 figure + g(3..13) bounds)
Ahmed-Wildstrom preprint (read 2026-07-18: only six g values known)

entries.quantity: Number of pairwise non-similar n-point configurations achieving the minimum number of distinct distances (question: >= 2, and probably many, for all large n)
entries.witness_object: None settling the problem: the claim is for all sufficiently large n. A finite witness for one n (two non-similar optimal sets) needs the exact optimum A186704(n) plus an optimality certificate for both configurations — the certificate side is an open real-geometry enumeration, not a cheap check, and known only for small n.
entries.witness_verifier: No cheap verifier: verifying a configuration MINIMIZES distinct distances requires ruling out all other n-point configurations (real-algebraic search), unlike counting distances in a given set (which is easy but insufficient).
entries.notes: erdosproblems.com/91 marks this not resolvable by finite computation. Known small-n structure (paraphrased): n=3 equilateral triangle unique; n=4 has two non-similar minimizers (square; two glued equilateral triangles); n=5 regular pentagon unique — published proof by Kovacs 2024 (settling Erdos's 'colleague from Zagreb' anecdote); Erdos (1987) claimed >= 2 non-similar examples for 6 <= n <= 9. The page notes unincorporated comment claims of partial results/path to solution — worth re-checking on revisit. Shares the A186704 table with #89; see that entry for the live finite cell.

entries.quantity: A006065(21) — maximum number of lines containing exactly 4 points among 21 points in the plane (orchard problem, 4-per-row; first uncomputed cell, exact values known through a(20)=23)

lower.source: Monotonicity (adding a generic 21st point preserves all 4-point rows) from A006065(20)=23 exact (Zhao Hui Du exhaustive computation 2008-2010; construction Xianzu Lin 2005)

upper.source: DERIVED trivial pair-counting: each of the C(21,2)=210 pairs lies on at most one line with >=4 points, each exactly-4-point line consumes 6 pairs => <= floor(210/6) = 35. No sharper published n=21 bound verified

entries.witness_object: 21 points with rational (or exactly-certified algebraic) coordinates spanning >= 24 lines that each contain exactly 4 of the points (for #101 relevance also record whether no 5 are collinear). Known strong constructions are algebraic (cubic-curve group law).
entries.witness_verifier: Exact rational collinearity: sort directions per point or test all C(21,3)=1330 triples with 3x3 determinants; group collinear classes; count classes of size exactly 4 and assert none of size >= 5. O(n^3), exact, dependency-free.
entries.notes: The $100 headline (number of 4-point lines is o(n^2) given no 5 collinear) is asymptotic and cannot be settled by one configuration. Finite surface: exact optima stuck at n=20 since ~2010; a(22) >= 28 (Du, OEIS illustration; also Pegg 2020 construction) but no verified a(21) lower bound better than monotone 23 was found. Hub deep record grades the exact direction WALL: order-type enumeration plus existsR-realizability (stretchability) — not SAT/DRAT-shaped; and lower-bound nudges must out-produce algebraic cubic-curve constructions. Integer-coordinate variant A172992 (known to n=27, Kamenetsky 2012) is provably below the real optimum for several n.

checked: OEIS A006065 JSON (fetched 2026-07-18: data through a(20)=23, keyword hard/more)

entries.quantity: A003829(9) — maximum number of distinct unit circles each passing through >= 3 of 9 points in the plane (first uncomputed cell; exact known through a(8)=16, Harborth-Mengersen 1986)

lower.source: Monotonicity (add a 9th point far from all unit circles) from A003829(8)=16 (Harborth-Mengersen, Discrete Math 60 (1986) 193-197)

upper.source: DERIVED pair-counting (the Harborth-Mengersen correction of Erdos's double count): each pair of points lies on <= 2 unit circles, each counted circle contains >= 3 pairs => <= floor(2*C(9,2)/3) = 24; tight at n=4 (a(4)=4)

entries.witness_object: 9 points (coordinates as exact algebraic numbers or high-precision floats plus symbolic certificates) admitting >= 17 distinct unit circles each through at least 3 of the points. Note the exactly-3 vs at-least-3 reading: OEIS name says circles containing 3 points; erdosproblems.com/104 counts circles with at least three — record both counts for safety.
entries.witness_verifier: For each of the C(9,3)=84 triples compute the circumcircle exactly and test unit radius (algebraic identity |p-c|^2=1); deduplicate centers; count. Exact arithmetic (rational/quadratic fields) or certified interval arithmetic; no dependencies beyond bignum. Milliseconds.
entries.notes: $100 headline is asymptotic: is the number of rich unit circles o(n^2)? Known (per erdosproblems.com/104, paraphrased): >> n trivially (Erdos 1981), >> n^{3/2} construction (Elekes 1984, possibly the right order); upper O(n^2), refined to n(n-1)/3 (Harborth-Mengersen 1986); Erdos offered 100 GBP for deciding O(n^{3/2}) in general position. The finite cell a(9) appears untouched since 1986 (OEIS 'more', no post-1986 refs found) — an 18-dimensional continuous search plus exact certification is a genuine, low-competition scout target; exact determination of a(9) would need real-algebraic case analysis (graded unknown).

checked: OEIS A003829 JSON (fetched 2026-07-18: a(3..8)=1,4,4,8,12,16, keyword more)

entries.quantity: ES(7) = f(7) — least N such that every N points in general position (no 3 collinear) contain a convex 7-gon; first unknown Erdos-Szekeres value (f(3..6) = 3, 5, 9, 17; conjecture f(n) = 2^{n-2}+1 = A000051(n-2), so f(7) = 33)

lower.source: Erdos-Szekeres 1935: explicit 2^{n-2} = 32-point general-position configuration with no convex 7-gon (construction tight for all n <= 6)

upper.source: Toth-Valtr 2005: f(n) <= C(2n-5, n-2) + 1 = C(9,5)+1 = 127; bracket 33..127 re-verified current by hub deep record 2026-07-16 (incl. Bogdan arXiv 2512.24061 Dec 2025 partial SAT attack; SoCG 2025 Baek-Balko improvement is asymptotic only)

entries.witness_object: 33 points in general position (rational coordinates, no 3 collinear) containing no 7 points in convex position — would give f(7) >= 34 and refute the 90-year-old conjecture at n=7. Almost certainly nonexistent.
entries.witness_verifier: Exact rational checks: no-3-collinear via C(33,3) = 5456 determinants; no convex 7-gon by scanning C(33,7) ~ 4.3M 7-subsets (a k-set is convex iff all its 4-subsets are; or via order-type/signotope machinery). Seconds, dependency-free.
entries.notes: The Happy Ending problem — flagship $500 target; settling f(7) would be the first new exact value since f(6)=17 (Szekeres-Peters 2006; SAT-reproven in 8.53 CPU-sec by Heule-Scheucher 2024). The proof side f(7) <= 33 is DRAT-shaped in principle (signotope encoding + forbid convex 7-subsets) but currently intractable: Bogdan's Dec-2025 16.67M-clause attack closes only anchored subfamilies with heavy-tailed runtimes to 2.28M CPU-sec per configuration, and the encoding is owned by the world's leading SAT-geometry groups (Heule, Scheucher, Maric) — no edge for a 100-core farm (hub deep record verdict WALL). Falsification search targets an object believed nonexistent.

checked: hub deep record atlas/problems.json#107 (bounds + Dec-2025 SAT status, compiled 2026-07-16)

entries.quantity: A068063(101) — maximum size of a nondividing subset of {1,...,101} (no element divides the sum of any nonempty subset of the others); first cell beyond the b-file, which ends at a(100)=8

lower.source: Monotonicity from A068063(100) = 8 (OEIS b-file b068063.txt by Christian Sievers, re-fetched and read 2026-07-18)

upper.source: DERIVED deletion bound a(N+1) <= a(N)+1 (removing an element preserves the nondividing property) with a(100)=8; the explicit literature bound F(N) < 3*sqrt(N)+1 (Erdos-Lev-Rauzy-Sandor-Sarkozy 1999) gives only 31

entries.witness_object: A 9-element subset A of {1,...,101} such that for every a in A, no nonempty subset of A\{a} has sum divisible by a. Existence pins a(101)=9; exhaustive refutation pins a(101)=8.
entries.witness_verifier: For each a in A run a subset-sum DP over A\{a} modulo a and assert residue 0 is unreachable by a nonempty subset (or brute-force all 2^8 = 256 subsets per element). Microseconds, dependency-free.
entries.notes: Headline #131 asks the growth of F(N): Erdos's original question (F(N) > N^{1/2-o(1)}?) is answered NO — F(N) <= N^{1/4+o(1)} via the non-averaging-set link (Pham-Zakharov 2024), against F(N) >> N^{1/5} (Csaba construction, in ELRSS99) and Straus's exp((sqrt(2/log 2)+o(1)) sqrt(log N)); correct exponent open (asymptotic, not machine-movable). The finite table is the scout surface: branch-and-bound over {1..N} extends A068063 cheaply (jumps to size 8 at n=65; size-9 threshold unknown — locating min{N : F(N)=9} is a clean, ownable record). Cross-ref Erdos #13 (Erdos-Sarkozy) and #186 (non-averaging), already worked in-project.

checked: OEIS A068063 JSON + b-file to n=100 (fetched 2026-07-18)

entries.quantity: A005346(7) = W(2,7) — least N such that every 2-coloring of {1..N} contains a monochromatic 7-term arithmetic progression (first uncomputed van der Waerden number; W(2,3..6) = 9, 35, 178, 1132)

lower.source: Rabung-Lotts, ElJC 2012 (cyclic zipper method): valid 7-AP-free 2-coloring of {1..3703}; still the current record per 2022-2026 secondary sources and hub deep record (Monroe's DDFW SLS work and arXiv:2111.01099 target other cells/asymptotics, not diagonal k=7)

entries.witness_object: A 2-coloring of {1,...,N} with N >= 3704 containing no monochromatic 7-term AP (improves the lower bound to W(2,7) > N). Only tower-type general upper bounds exist (Gowers), so no meaningful upper cell.
entries.witness_verifier: Scan all APs: for each start a and difference d with a+6d <= N check the 7 terms are not monochromatic; ~N^2/12 = 1.1M checks at N=3704. O(N^2), milliseconds, dependency-free — but a commodity every record-holder already runs.
entries.notes: The $500 headline (W(k)^{1/k} -> infinity) needs an asymptotic theorem (cf. Green 2021 w(3,k) lower bounds; sharpened by arXiv:2111.01099) — unreachable by witness. Finite surfaces per hub deep record: (1) exact W(2,7) via UNSAT+DRAT at N ~ 10^3-10^4 is astronomically infeasible (W(2,6)=1132 already needed clusters+FPGAs, Kouril-Paul 2008) — R(5,5)-class wall; (2) the lower-bound record is a live SLS/SAT search with a trivial verifier but is held by purpose-tuned specialist solvers (cyclic zippers, Heule-style constructions, Monroe's DDFW linear-weight-transfer); 14 years unbeaten, no verifier moat. Related off-diagonal cells (W(2,8) >= 11495, W(2,9) >= 41265, W(2,10) >= 103474) are equally specialist-held.

entries.quantity: A003002(212) — r_3(212), size of the largest subset of {1..212} with no 3-term arithmetic progression (first uncomputed cell; exact table verified to n=211)

lower.source: monotonicity from A003002(211)=43, OEIS b-file computed by Fausto A. C. Cariboni

upper.source: one-step bound r_3(n+1) <= r_3(n)+1 applied to a(211)=43 (remove the element n+1 from any extremal set)

entries.witness_object: A subset of {1..212} of size 44 containing no 3-term AP (would settle a(212)=44); otherwise the record is an exhaustive/ILP optimality certificate that 43 is maximal.
entries.witness_verifier: Pure-python O(k^2) scan: for each pair a<b in the set check 2b-a is not in the set; assert |S|=44 and S within range (~1000 membership checks).
entries.notes: Run structure (a(209..211)=43,43,43; recent runs of length 3-10) suggests a(212)=43, so the likely record is the closure certificate, not a size-44 set. The named $10000 problem (asymptotic formula for r_k(N)) is a wall: best upper bounds Kelley-Meka 2023 (k=3), Green-Tao 2017 (k=4), Leng-Sah-Sawhney 2024 (k>=5), per erdosproblems.com/142 (page dated 2026-04-04). Companion next cells: A003003(143) [r_4, table to 142], A003004(116) [r_5, to 115], A003005(148) [r_6, to 147], all Cariboni 2018 with witness sets published on OEIS.

entries.witness_object: None — progress on this quantity is an analytic theorem about all large x, not a finite certificate; no finite computation moves the proven-alpha threshold.
entries.witness_verifier: No cheap witness exists: the conjectured statement (limit exists for every alpha >= 0) is not falsifiable or improvable by finite data.
entries.notes: Granville 1998: the full conjecture follows from the ABC conjecture. The linked A005117 is just the squarefree sequence itself — no frontier cell. Not a records-lane target; recorded for gap-map completeness. erdosproblems.com/145 page last edited 2025-10-19.

entries.quantity: A003022(29) — length of the optimal (shortest) 29-mark Golomb ruler; first unproven cell after distributed.net OGR-28 closed a(28)=585 in Nov 2022

lower.source: strict monotonicity above a(28)=585 (deleting an end mark of a 29-mark ruler yields a strictly shorter 28-mark ruler); a(28)=585 proven optimal by distributed.net OGR-28

entries.witness_object: A 29-mark Golomb ruler: integers 0=m_1<...<m_29=L with all C(29,2)=406 pairwise differences distinct; any certified example establishes a(29) <= L. (Best-known candidate lengths exist from Singer/Bose-Chowla construction tables — Shearer, Rokicki-Dogon — but I could not verify a specific record length in a citable source this pass, so upper is left null rather than guessed.)
entries.witness_verifier: Compute all 406 pairwise differences, assert all distinct and mark count = 29; <1ms in pure python.
entries.notes: Relevance to #155 (is F(N+k) <= F(N)+1 for large N, F = A143824 max Sidon subset size): F is determined by the optimal-ruler table — F jumps exactly at A227590(n)=a(n)+1 — so pinning a(29) extends the verified range of the F(N+k)<=F(N)+1 conjecture beyond N=586. Bloom remark: conjecture may even hold with k ~ eps*N^(1/2). No ruler with >16 marks has ever beaten the finite-field constructions (Rokicki-Dogon hypothesis A, with a $250 bounty for orders 36..40000); OGR-28 consumed ~8 years of distributed volunteer CPU and OGR-29 was never launched — exact closure is a wall for one-box compute.

checked: Wikipedia Golomb ruler (OGR-27 completed 2014-02-19, OGR-28 completed 2022-11-23, optimal 585)
MathWorld GolombRuler (confirms optimal unknown for n>28)
Rokicki & Dogon, Larger Golomb Rulers (G4G12 PDF, read pp.1-6)

entries.quantity: A382397(66) — minimum size of a maximal Sidon subset of {1..66} (first uncomputed cell; OEIS table computed through n=65, added 2025)
entries.witness_object: A maximal Sidon set S in {1..66} of size 6 (or, surprisingly, smaller): all pairwise differences of S distinct, and for every x in {1..66}\S the set S+{x} has a repeated difference; establishes a(66) <= |S|.
entries.witness_verifier: Check C(|S|,2) differences distinct; then for each of the <=65 non-members x verify S+{x} violates the Sidon condition. Under 10^4 integer operations, dependency-free.
entries.notes: Data: a(n)=6 for all computed 43<=n<=65; monotonicity is empirically clean but unproven, so no rigorous lower/upper carried. Exhaustive closure of a(66) (and pushing to n~100) is a bounded search (C(66,6)~9e7 candidates with heavy Sidon pruning) — hours on one box; OEIS comment explicitly notes there are too few terms to pin the growth pattern, so new cells have real value. Asymptotic open content (Erdős-Sárközy-Sós 1994): greedy gives >> N^(1/3); Ruzsa 1998 (A small maximal Sidon set, Ramanujan J.) constructed maximal Sidon sets << (N log N)^(1/3); closing the N^(1/3) vs (N log N)^(1/3) factor is the prize. See also #340.

checked: web search confirming Ruzsa 1998 Ramanujan J. paper

entries.quantity: R(3,10) = A000791(10) — the smallest open Ramsey number in the R(3,k) family (table exact through R(3,9)=36)

lower.source: G. Exoo, explicit triangle-free graph on 39 vertices with independence number 9 (per Goedgebeur-Radziszowski arXiv:1210.5826 and Radziszowski's DS1 survey)

upper.source: V. Angeltveit, R(3,10) <= 41, arXiv:2401.00392; published Electron. J. Combin. 32(4) #P30, Nov 2025

entries.witness_object: A triangle-free graph on 40 vertices with independence number <= 9 (adjacency matrix); its existence proves R(3,10)=41 and closes the cell.
entries.witness_verifier: Triangle check O(n^3) on 40 vertices; independence alpha(G)<10 via exact max-clique on the complement (seconds with a basic branch-and-bound; no dependencies beyond the solver).
entries.notes: Freshness-checked 2026-07-18: 40 <= R(3,10) <= 41 is current; Angeltveit's bound is now peer-reviewed (EJC Nov 2025). Deciding 40 vs 41 requires ruling out all (3,10)-graphs on 40 vertices — Angeltveit-scale gluing computation, beyond one-box scope. The named $250 problem is the asymptotic: R(3,k) = c*k^2/log k with c in [1/2, 1]; lower c>=1/2 Hefty-Horn-King-Pfender 2025 (chain: Kim 1995 1/162, Bohman-Keevash & Pontiveros-Griffiths-Morris 1/4, Campos-Jenssen-Michelen-Sahasrabudhe 1/3), upper (1+o(1)) Shearer 1983; both 2025 papers conjecture c=1/2. Not machine-movable on the asymptotic side; the finite cell is the only witness surface.

checked: OEIS A000791 (full entry incl. Angeltveit/Wechsler comments)
arXiv:2401.00392 + EJC 32(4)#P30 publication status via web search

entries.quantity: The Graham-Spencer-Witsenhausen limit: lim_n (largest weakly-triple-free subset of the first n 3-smooth numbers)/n = maximal density of {x,2x,3x}-free sets = 0.80096575500655898909... (A386439)
entries.witness_object: None — GSW 1977 proved the limit exists and equals (1/3)*sum_{k in K} 1/d_k over 3-smooth numbers d_k, so the constant is machine-computable to arbitrary precision (Eberhard: 35 digits in A386439; A004059 b-file to 3335 terms). No gap remains to move with an example.
entries.witness_verifier: n/a — recomputing digits of a convergent explicit series is verification of known work, not a record.
entries.notes: Remaining open content is qualitative: is the constant irrational (open per A386439 comment), plus density/upper-density analogues for infinite sets (erdosproblems.com/168, page dated 2026-03-23). The finite tables A057561/A094708/A004059 are generated by an efficient algorithm (Eberhard's SageMath script), so there is no expensive frontier cell. Not a records-lane target.

entries.quantity: f(3) — supremum of sum_{a in A} 1/a over all 3-AP-free sets A of positive integers (Erdős-Graham); finite because reciprocal-sum divergence forces 3-APs (Bloom-Sisask 2020)

lower.source: J. Wróblewski [Wr84], A nonaveraging set of integers with a large sum of reciprocals; still the record per erdosproblems.com/169 (page dated 2026-04-04)

entries.witness_object: An explicit 3-AP-free set with certified reciprocal sum > 3.00849. Walker [Wa25] proved Kempner sets (all integers whose base-b digits lie in a fixed S subset of {0..b-1}) suffice to approach f(k), so a witness is a pair (b,S) + an AP3-freeness argument for the digit construction + a rigorous numeric lower bound on the reciprocal sum.
entries.witness_verifier: Structural AP3-freeness check of the digit construction, then interval-arithmetic partial sums with a geometric tail bound for sum 1/n over the Kempner set (classical Baillie-style algorithm, dependency-free).
entries.notes: Record is 40+ years old, hence graded hard, but the attack surface just changed: Walker [Wa25] pushed f(4) >= 4.43975 via the Kempner reduction, and the (b,S) search space is machine-enumerable with cheap rigorous evaluation — a genuine hunt candidate. Context: Berlekamp 1968 f(k) >= (log2/2)k; Gerver 1977 f(k) >= (1-o(1))k*log k; trivially f(k)/log W(k) >= 1/2 and any constant > 1/2 is open — this is the link to the stub's OEIS A005346 = van der Waerden numbers W(2,n) (known through W(2,6)=1132, Kouril-Paul 2008; W(2,7) open, lower-bound colorings are a separate SAT-witness surface). No explicit numeric upper bound for f(3) is published (finiteness only), hence bounded_below_only.

checked: erdosproblems.com/169 (paraphrased, page dated 2026-04-04, accessed 2026-07-18; cites Be68, Ge77, Wr84, Wa25)
web search for Walker 2025 (no independent copy located; number carried with erdosproblems.com attribution)

entries.quantity: A046693(214) — minimal number of marks in a sparse ruler / restricted difference basis for {0..214} (first cell beyond Ed Pegg Jr's verified table n=0..213)

lower.source: counting bound: C(k,2) >= 214 forces k >= 22

upper.source: one-step bound a(n+1) <= a(n)+1 (OEIS A046693 comment) with a(213)=25 from Pegg's b-file

entries.witness_object: S subset of {0..214} with |S| <= 25 whose pairwise differences cover every value 1..214; establishes a(214) <= |S|. Expected truth is 25 or 26: the excess of a(n) over round(sqrt(3n+9/4)) is 0 or 1 for every verified n (round(sqrt(3*214+9/4)) = 25).
entries.witness_verifier: Compute all |S|^2 pairwise differences and check {1..214} is covered; <10^5 integer ops, pure python.
entries.notes: The named problem (Erdős-Gál 1948, answering Rédei) is the limit constant lim a(n)/sqrt(n), proven to exist and known to lie in [1.56, sqrt(3)=1.73205...]: lower Leech 1956, upper Wichmann 1963; Pegg's computations [Pe20] give evidence the Wichmann upper bound is the truth. Finite witnesses cannot move the limit's bounds — but each new exactly-closed cell extends the excess-conjecture evidence (cf. A308766 divergence list, A326499 best-known solutions). Beware non-monotonicity: a(137) < a(136) and a(212)=25 < a(211)=26 (b-file), so only the +1-step and counting bounds are rigorous. Exhaustive closure of one cell at n=214 (choosing ~25 of 215 positions) is within reach of the same desktop-class search that produced the 0..213 table.

entries.quantity: A231255(7) — least t such that every t-step North-East lattice path (steps (0,1)/(1,0)) contains 7 collinear points (Gerver–Ramsey NE variant tracked by the hub; the hub statement itself is the Z^3 S-walk question)

lower.source: Barnoff & Bright, 'North-East Lattice Paths Avoiding k Collinear Points via Satisfiability', arXiv:2511.23226 — explicit 327-step path avoiding 7 collinear points (previous record 260 steps, Shallit); recorded in OEIS A231255

entries.witness_object: A binary string of N/E steps of length >= 328 whose lattice path has no 7 collinear points (current record path: 327 steps)
entries.witness_verifier: Dependency-free: for each of the ~L^2/2 point pairs, count path points on their line via cross-product test; O(L^3) integer arithmetic (~3.6e7 ops at L≈330), pure Python in seconds. Reject if any line carries >= 7 path points.
entries.notes: a(1..6) = 0,1,4,9,29,97; Barnoff–Bright enumerated all avoiding paths for k<=6 by SAT, so the k=7 frontier is a live SAT lane (record set Nov 2025 — beating it needs comparable incremental-SAT effort, not hours). No numeric upper bound: von Brömssen (May 2026) gives a(7) <= A380991(6) but A380991 (polyomino version) is only computed to n=4. Exact a(7) = UNSAT of avoidance at the threshold length — clean DRAT target in principle, scale unknown.

entries.quantity: A005115(27) — smallest possible last term of an arithmetic progression of 27 primes

lower.source: Strict monotonicity from A005115(26)=12783396861134173 (any AP27 contains an AP26 with strictly smaller end); a(26) PROVEN minimal by J. Perrenet, 13 Feb 2026, per N. Luhn's Primes-in-AP records page (pzktupel.de/PAP), minimal-end table

upper.source: Only known AP27: 224584605939537911 + 81292139*23#*n, n=0..26 (R. Gahan / PrimeGrid, 23 Sep 2019). All 27 terms re-verified prime this session with 12-base deterministic Miller–Rabin; end term matches Luhn's minimal-end table

entries.witness_object: 27 primes in arithmetic progression whose last term is < 696112717486210091
entries.witness_verifier: Deterministic Miller–Rabin (bases 2..37, valid below 3.3e24) on the 27 terms plus AP-structure check; <1s pure Python (executed this session on the current record).
entries.notes: FRESH state: minimal-end table now proves a(1..26) all minimal (k=23 Perrenet 2023, k=24 Petukhov Nov 2025, k=25 and k=26 Perrenet Feb 2026), so the OEIS row is fully certified and a(27) is the genuine next cell with a 54x-wide two-sided window. Finding any smaller AP27 took PrimeGrid 3+ years of distributed GPU search to find even one — grade hard. Exact a(27) is structurally a bounded exhaustive search below 6.97e17 and the Perrenet minimality pipeline just reached k=26, but expect CPU-decades not GPU-hours. Erdős #200 itself (is longest prime-AP in [1,N] of length o(log N)?) is asymptotic; PNT gives <= (1+o(1))log N; the A005115 table is the machine-record content.

entries.quantity: A020753(19)/A020754(19) — next record gap between consecutive squarefree numbers (current record: gap 19, from 125781000834058567 to 125781000834058586)

lower.source: A020753(18)=19 at A020754(18)=125781000834058567 (record table fed by the Mossinghoff–Oliveira e Silva–Trudgian k-free computation, arXiv:1912.04972, squarefree numbers computed to 1e18); all 18 interior integers re-verified non-squarefree and both endpoints squarefree this session

entries.witness_object: Consecutive squarefree s < s' with s'-s >= 20 PLUS exhaustive-sieve coverage from the current record start to the new gap (records-table semantics: must be the earliest such gap)
entries.witness_verifier: Certificate: for each of the >= 19 interior integers a prime p with p^2 dividing it (19 modular divisions); endpoint squarefreeness by factorization (Pollard rho, milliseconds). The sieve-coverage claim is the expensive part, not the certificate.
entries.notes: Records occur out of order (first gap-12 precedes first gap-11 — McNew, OEIS A020754), so the table is 'successive increasing gaps'. Extending it needs exhaustive sieving beyond ~1.26e17 (and likely beyond the ~1e18 extent of MOST 2019) — big distributed compute, hence hard. CRT gives arbitrarily large gaps at astronomical heights, so a bare large gap without sieve coverage is NOT a record witness. Theory frontier (paraphrased from erdosproblems.com/208): Erdős 1951 lower (pi^2/6)(log s)/(loglog s) infinitely often — conjectured sharp; upper s^{1/5-c} (Pandey 2024, improving Filaseta–Trifonov 1992); ABC implies O(s^eps) (Granville 1998).

checked: local verification of the gap-19 record

entries.quantity: Densities/infinitude of consecutive-prime-gap patterns: {n : d_{n+1} >= d_n} and {n : d_{n+1} <= d_n} each conjectured density 1/2, plus infinitude of d_{n+1} = d_n (A064113)
entries.witness_object: none — density and infinitude claims; no finite example moves them
entries.witness_verifier: n/a — the problem page marks this as not resolvable by finite computation; A064113/A333230/A333231 are bookkeeping sequences (b-files 1000/10000 terms, keyword easy) with no record frontier
entries.notes: The run version (d_n = d_{n+1} = ... = d_{n+k} solvable for all k) is equivalent to k consecutive primes in AP — that record lane lives at hub #141 (already seeded, CPAP records). Banks (2023) gives a heuristic for the density-1/2 statement conditional on a quantitative prime-tuples conjecture (paraphrased from erdosproblems.com/218, page edited Jan 2026). Empirical density tracking is possible but bounds nothing.

entries.quantity: A357018(44) — next record run of consecutive integers not expressible as a sum of two squares (current record: 106 consecutive, following A297350(43) = 99951600293)

lower.source: A357018(43)=106 (H. Pfoertner, 2022) with start A297350(43)=99951600293 (C. R. Greathouse IV b-file); the full 106-run and both representable endpoints (99951600293, 99951600400) re-verified this session via factorization

entries.witness_object: >= 107 consecutive integers each non-representable as a sum of two squares, plus exhaustive scan coverage from 99951600293 to the new start (records semantics)
entries.witness_verifier: Per-integer certificate: exhibit a prime p = 3 (mod 4) dividing n to an odd power (sum-of-two-squares criterion); ~110 Pollard-rho factorizations of ~1e11–1e13 integers, seconds in pure Python. Endpoints checked representable the same way.
entries.notes: Both records sequences carry keyword 'more' and stop near 1e11 — a segmented sieve marking a^2+b^2 (or multiplicative sieve via primes 3 mod 4) to 1e12–1e13 on one GPU box is a days-scale record hunt; the most compute-accessible lane in this chunk. Finite records do NOT move the asymptotic frontier: limsup gap/log n_k >= 0.868... (Dietmann–Elsholtz–Kalmynin–Konyagin–Maynard, IMRN 2022, improving Richards 1982's 1/4), best upper O(n_k^{1/4}) (Bambah–Chowla 1947), Erdős 1951 lower log/sqrt(loglog) i.o. — numeric facts paraphrased from erdosproblems.com/222.

entries.quantity: Asymptotics of A074741 (partial sums of squared prime gaps): Heath-Brown's conjecture sum_{p_n <= x} (p_{n+1}-p_n)^2 ~ 2 x log x (stub's Lean form: first N gaps ~ N (log N)^2, equivalent via p_N ~ N log N)
entries.witness_object: none — an asymptotic growth conjecture; no finite computation bounds or certifies it
entries.witness_verifier: n/a — A074741 b-file (T. D. Noe, n<=10000) supports ratio plots only; extending the numeric partial sums is trivial but carries no record or bound content
entries.notes: Heath-Brown 1982 (Acta Arith. 41) ties the conjecture to pair correlation of zeta zeros; Wolf's variant 2 x^2/pi(x) is also on the OEIS entry. Cramér-class: even RH-conditional results are far from the conjectured asymptotic. No gap-map frontier; keep as librarian/context entry only.

entries.quantity: A109926(21) — least n whose number of representations n = p + 2^k (p prime, 2^k < n) is exactly 21; records-of-f lane for f = A109925, the quantity Erdős conjectured to be o(log n)

lower.source: D. Johnson comment on OEIS A109926: a(n) > 10^10 for n >= 21

entries.witness_object: An integer n > 1e10 with exactly 21 primes among {n - 2^k : 1 <= 2^k < n} (any such n upper-bounds a(21); note A109926 is not monotone — a(18) < a(17))
entries.witness_verifier: ~34 deterministic Miller–Rabin tests on n - 2^k and count == 21; milliseconds, dependency-free (this session verified a(20) = 2282745465 has exactly 20 representations)
entries.notes: Search accelerators from OEIS comments: known terms n>4 divisible by 15 (conjectured 30 | a(n) beyond); segmented prime sieve + 2^k shift counting reaches 1e11–1e12 on one box in days. Once any n0 with 21 reps is found, exact a(21) is a bounded exhaustive count over (1e10, n0] — hence cell. FRESHNESS caveat: the 1e10 floor is 2009-era; re-check for extensions before spending compute. Companion verified-range fact for the same hub problem: A039669 = {4,7,15,21,45,75,105} (m - 2^k prime for ALL k) has no further terms below 2^120 (M. Alekseyev 2011, OEIS) and Erdős conjectured the list complete (cf. hub #1142). Theory: Erdős 1950 proved f(n) >> loglog n infinitely often (paraphrased from erdosproblems.com/236).
