entries.quantity: Least counterexample n > 2 to the Erdős–Straus conjecture 4/n = 1/x + 1/y + 1/z (conjectured nonexistent); tracked as the verified-range frontier N with the conjecture confirmed for all n <= N

lower.source: Mihnea & Bogdan, arXiv:2509.00128 — cited as [MiDu25] on erdosproblems.com/242 (page edited 2026-05-07): verified for all n <= 10^18; suffices to check primes

entries.witness_object: Extended verification run past 10^18: for every prime p in the six unresolved residue classes {1,121,169,289,361,529} mod 840 (Mordell 1969 handles all others) up to a new frontier N', a decomposition 4/p = 1/x+1/y+1/z
entries.witness_verifier: Each per-prime triple (x,y,z) checks in O(1) exact integer arithmetic (cross-multiply). But a full range certificate has re-verification cost equal to generation cost — no compact certificate format is known, so range extensions are expensive to independently audit.
entries.notes: Falsifiable: a single counterexample n would close the problem, but conjecture is believed true, and truth cannot be established by finite computation. Partial results (per erdosproblems.com/242): Mordell 1969 reduces to 6 classes mod 840; Terzi 1971 to 198 classes mod 120120; Vaughan 1970: exceptions in [1,x] <= x*exp(-c(log x)^{2/3}); Elsholtz–Tao 2013: sum of solution counts over primes <= N is N(log N)^{2+o(1)}, f(p) <= p^{3/5+o(1)}. CAUTION: Bradford, arXiv:2602.11774 (Feb 2026, 4KB, v1) claims a complete solution — unreviewed, and erdosproblems.com still lists the problem OPEN as of its 2026-05-07 edit; do not treat as closed. A073101 counts solutions with x<y<z (b-file to n=1000).

entries.quantity: No bounded quantity — structural rigidity conjecture: any integer sequence with a_n/a_{n-1}^2 -> 1 and rational sum of reciprocals must eventually satisfy the Sylvester recurrence a_n = a_{n-1}^2 - a_{n-1} + 1
entries.witness_object: None — an affirmative resolution is a proof over all sequences; a counterexample would be an infinite object (a full sequence plus the rationality of its reciprocal sum), not finitely checkable
entries.witness_verifier: None exists: rationality of an infinite series cannot be certified by finite computation
entries.notes: erdosproblems.com/243 (edited 2026-01-21) lists: Erdős–Straus 1964 [ErSt64] proved a limsup criterion any non-recurrence rational-sum sequence must satisfy; Duverney 2001 [Du01] proved the variant where sum(a_{n+1}/a_n^2 - 1) converges: rational sum iff eventually Sylvester recurrence. A000058 = Sylvester's sequence 2,3,7,43,1807,... (greedy Egyptian expansion of 1); OEIS b-file has 13 terms (doubly exponential growth).

entries.quantity: No bounded quantity — is sum_{n>=1} phi(n)/2^n irrational? (constant = 1.36763080198502235... = A256936)
entries.witness_object: None — irrationality of a single explicit constant; no finite witness in either direction (a rationality witness p/q would require proving an infinite-series identity)
entries.witness_verifier: None: high-precision digit computation is easy (geometric-decay series) but digits certify nothing about irrationality
entries.notes: No partial results listed on erdosproblems.com/249 (edited 2025-09-28); one user flags it 'difficult'. OEIS gives alternative series forms (Eldar: equals 1/2 + sum mu(k)/(2^k-1)^2, Mar 2026 comment). Source: Erdős–Graham 1980 p.61.

entries.quantity: No bounded quantity — is sum_{n>=1} p_n/2^n irrational, p_n the n-th prime? (constant = 3.67464396601132877... = A098990)
entries.witness_object: None — irrationality of a single explicit constant; no finite witness exists in either direction
entries.witness_verifier: None: digits are cheaply computable from the primes but decide nothing
entries.notes: Closest known result (erdosproblems.com/251, edited 2025-09-28): Erdős 1958 [Er58b] proved sum p_n^k/n! is irrational for every k >= 1 — the factorial-denominator analogue; the 2^n version is open. Erdős [Er88c] further conjectured sum p_n^k/2^n irrational for all k, and irrationality of sum p_n/(g_1...g_n) whenever g_n >= 2, g_n = o(p_n) (g_n = p_n + 1 shows a growth condition is needed).

entries.quantity: No machine-bounded quantity — irrationality of sum_{n>=1} sigma_k(n)/n! for each fixed k >= 1; the proof frontier stands at k = 4, first open case k = 5
entries.witness_object: None — each case k is an irrationality theorem, not a computation; progress means a new proof (the k=5 case), which is not a cheaply verifiable witness
entries.witness_verifier: None: no finite certificate can decide irrationality of these constants (A227988 = 3.5270004718... for k=1, A227989 = 6.3400966688... for k=2)
entries.notes: Frontier (erdosproblems.com/252, edited 2026-01-22): unconditionally irrational for 1 <= k <= 4 — k=1,2 Erdős 1952 [Er52] (cf. Erdős–Kac Monthly Problem 4518, solution Breusch 1954, per OEIS A227988 comment); k=3 independently Schlage-Puchta 2006 [ScPu06] and Friedlander–Luca–Stoiciu 2007 [FLC07]; k=4 Pratt 2022 [Pr22]. Irrational for ALL k >= 1 conditional on Schinzel's hypothesis (Schlage-Puchta) or Dickson's conjecture (FLS). Guy UPINT problem B14.

entries.quantity: No bounded quantity — explicit description / growth rate of the greedy 3-AP-free (Stanley) sequence A(4) = S(0,4) = A005487 and its family A(n); conjectured dichotomy: growth k^{log_2 3} vs k^2/log k
entries.witness_object: None — computing further terms of A005487 is a trivial deterministic greedy computation (b-file already has 10000 terms) and settles nothing about the asymptotic question
entries.witness_verifier: Terms are trivially recomputable/checkable (greedy 3-AP-free extension), but term data is not a record — no bound moves
entries.notes: Per erdosproblems.com/271 (edited 2026-01-20): A(1), A(3^k), A(2*3^k) have explicit base-3 characterizations (Odlyzko–Stanley 1978); Odlyzko–Stanley conjectured every Stanley sequence eventually grows like k^{log_2 3} or k^2/log k; no sequence is proven to have the second rate; Lindhurst 1990 data suggests A(4) does. Moy 2011: a_k <= (1/2+eps)k^2 eventually; explicit version a_k <= (k-1)(k+2)/2 + n (van Doorn & Sothanaphan, site comments). Fresh: Sothanaphan arXiv:2512.11983 (Dec 2025) argues irregular Stanley sequences plausibly do NOT have Theta(k^2/log k) growth — the conjectured dichotomy itself is in doubt. Not formalized in Lean.

entries.quantity: No bounded quantity — writing H_n = a_n/L_n with L_n = lcm(1..n): do gcd(a_n, L_n) = 1 and gcd(a_n, L_n) > 1 each occur for infinitely many n? (gcd(a_n, L_n) = A110566(n))
entries.witness_object: None meaningful — individual n with gcd = 1 are cheap to find and verify but are plentiful (2641 of the first 10000 per the OEIS b-file, largest 9311 — independently re-verified this session by exact rational arithmetic), so a 'largest known instance' record carries no evidential weight for infinitude
entries.witness_verifier: For a given n: compute lcm(1..n) and the exact numerator of H_n, take gcd — pure integer arithmetic, seconds for n in the millions; but no finite set of instances decides the pure-existence question
entries.notes: Per erdosproblems.com/291 (edited 2026-01-12): the gcd>1 direction is effectively resolved — site-recorded observation (Steinerberger): if the leading base-p digit of n is p-1 then p | gcd; exact criterion: p | gcd iff p divides the numerator of H_k where k = leading digit of n base p. The gcd=1 direction is the open part; heuristic count ~ x/log x of such n <= x (Shiu preprint 2016 [Sh16]), so infinitely many expected but density zero. Wu–Yan 2022 [WuYa22]: conditional on Q-linear independence of {1/log p} (follows from Schanuel), the gcd>1 set has upper density 1. A110566 b-file to n=10000 (Heinz).

checked: session computation: independent gcd verification at n=9311

entries.quantity: A192881(17) — minimum number of terms in an Egyptian fraction decomposition of 1 with least denominator 17 (finite proxy table for k(N) of #295); a(1..16) known, a(16) = 30

lower.source: Trivial harmonic bound: k distinct denominators >= 17 give sum <= sum_{i=17}^{16+k} 1/i, which first reaches 1 at k = 29 — computed and exact-arithmetic-checked this session (sanity: same bound gives 27 at N=16 vs true a(16)=30)

entries.witness_object: A list of k distinct integers 17 = n_1 < n_2 < ... < n_k with sum 1/n_i = 1; establishes a(17) <= k. Closing the cell additionally needs an exhaustive-search certificate that k-1 terms are impossible.
entries.witness_verifier: Exact rational arithmetic (e.g. Python fractions): check distinctness, minimum = 17, sum == 1. O(k), dependency-free, milliseconds.
entries.notes: Problem #295 asks whether k(N) - (e-1)N -> infinity; Erdős–Straus 1971 [ErSt71b] proved -c < k(N) - (e-1)N << N/log N (per erdosproblems.com/295, edited 2025-10-01). (e-1)*17 ~ 29.2, consistent with the harmonic lower bound 29. Table is live: Alekseyev added a(11)-a(15) with explicit decompositions Oct 2025 (in OEIS Example section); a(16)=30; keyword 'hard,more'. Caveat: k(N) allows n_1 > N while A192881 fixes n_1 = n; they coincide in all known cases but this equivalence is not proven here — new cells sharpen the finite picture yet cannot settle the limit question (asymptotic side is a wall). Múgica's OEIS a-file has decompositions achieving the published terms.

entries.quantity: A390394(39) — maximum size of A ⊆ {1..39} with no solution 1/a = 1/b_1 + ... + 1/b_k over distinct elements of A; first uncomputed cell (a(1..38) known, a(38) = 29)

lower.source: Monotonicity a(39) >= a(38) = 29: any valid subset for n=38 remains valid for n=39 (OEIS A390394 data, retrieved 2026-07-18)

upper.source: a(n+1) <= a(n) + 1 (delete element 39 from an optimal subset) with a(38) = 29 (OEIS A390394 data, retrieved 2026-07-18)

entries.witness_object: A subset A ⊆ {1,...,39} with |A| = 30 such that no a in A equals a reciprocal-sum of a distinct subset of A \ {a}; exhibiting one pins a(39) = 30 (gap is exactly {29,30}); otherwise exhaustive search certifies a(39) = 29
entries.witness_verifier: For each a in A: subset-sum check over {1/b : b in A, b != a} for target 1/a in exact rationals (meet-in-the-middle over <= 2^15 partial sums, or lcm-scaled integer DP); seconds, dependency-free
entries.notes: Headline asymptotic gap (erdosproblems.com/301, edited 2026-01-16): N/2 <= f(N) — take A = (N/2, N] — and f(N) <= (25/28 + o(1))N ~ 0.8929N via an elementary disjoint-blocks argument S_a = {2a,3a,4a,6a,12a} credited to Wouter van Doorn (site-recorded observation, not refereed). Erdős conjectured f(N) = (1/2 + o(1))N. If b_i need not be distinct the threshold drops to N/2 exactly (Cambie–van Doorn, site-recorded). Sequence created Nov 2025 (Raza), 38 terms, keyword 'hard,more', published Python generator; each new exact cell is a record. Cf. #302, #327.

entries.quantity: A390395(732) — maximum size of S ⊆ {1..732} with no solution 1/a = 1/b + 1/c over distinct a,b,c in S; first cell beyond the b-file (a(1..731) known, a(731) = 606)

lower.source: Monotonicity a(732) >= a(731) = 606 (OEIS b390395.txt, terms to 731 by Kesarwani, first 658 by Chai Wah Wu; retrieved 2026-07-18)

upper.source: a(n+1) <= a(n) + 1 (delete element 732 from an optimal subset) with a(731) = 606 (OEIS b390395.txt, retrieved 2026-07-18)

entries.witness_object: A subset S ⊆ {1,...,732} with |S| = 607 containing no distinct triple with 1/a = 1/b + 1/c (pins a(732) = 607), or a search certificate that a(732) = 606
entries.witness_verifier: Enumerate all harmonic triples a < b < c <= 732 with 1/a = 1/b + 1/c (O(n^2) pair loop with exact rationals, ~270k pairs, milliseconds) and check none lies inside S
entries.notes: Headline asymptotic gap (erdosproblems.com/302, retrieved 2026-07-18): (5/8 + o(1))N <= f(N) <= (9/10 + o(1))N. Lower: Cambie's construction — odd integers <= N/4 plus all of (N/2, N] (site-recorded). Upper: van Doorn note (PDF in github.com/Woett/Mathematical-shorts, site-linked; not refereed). If sound, the 5/8 lower bound already answers the specific sub-question 'is f(N) = (1/2+o(1))N?' negatively — flag for the curator; both bounds are site-recorded observations. Colouring version is #303, solved by Brown–Rödl 1991. Sequence created Nov 2025 (Raza), no 'hard' keyword — extending the table is routine; the frontier record is the asymptotic constants, which need proofs not witnesses. Cf. #301, #327.

entries.quantity: A097849(106) — max over 1<=k<=106 of the minimal number of unit fractions needed to write k/106 (row-106 maximum of table A097847); finite proxy for N(b)=max_a N(a,b)
entries.witness_object: For the claimed row max m at numerator k*: a length-T(106,k) Egyptian representation for every k (upper bounds each cell), plus a bounded-DFS transcript showing k*/106 has no representation with m-1 distinct denominators (minimality)
entries.witness_verifier: Exact-arithmetic check that each listed 1/n_1+...+1/n_t sums to k/106 with distinct denominators; minimality re-checked by standard bounded backtracking (denominator bounds at each depth make the search finite) — pure integer arithmetic, no dependencies
entries.notes: b-file ends at n=105 (a(105)=4); recent activity by von Brömssen (May 2026) suggests the table is actively extendable. The open problem itself is asymptotic: Erdős 1950 gives N(b) >> loglog b, Vose 1985 gives N(b) << sqrt(log b) — that gap is a wall (erdosproblems.com/304, edited Dec 2025, still lists Vose as best; checked arXiv:2512.22083 (van Doorn–Tang 2025) — it concerns v(k) for decompositions of 1, related via #293 but does not improve N(b)). Extending A097849 feeds the empirical N(b)-vs-loglog(b) curve. Second OEIS ref: A097847.

entries.quantity: Count of known primary pseudoperfect numbers (N>1 with 1/N + sum_{p|N} 1/p = 1); each is the modulus m of a solution to sum 1/p_i = 1 - 1/m, so a new term is a new solution; infinitude is the open question

lower.source: OEIS A054377: 8 terms in data (2..5998279018951962402) plus 8490421583559688410706771261086 and 35979351189199316534587473905773572006 (Martins & Wang, Apr 2026; confirmed in Irvine comment May 2026); erdosproblems.com/313 still says 8 known (stale)

entries.witness_object: An 11th primary pseudoperfect number N, supplied together with its complete prime factorization p_1 < ... < p_k
entries.witness_verifier: Check each p_i is prime (BPSW/Miller-Rabin), the p_i are distinct, prod p_i = N (terms are squarefree), and 1 + sum_i N/p_i = N in exact integer arithmetic — milliseconds even for 40-digit N, dependency-free
entries.notes: Known terms have exactly n distinct prime factors for n<9; new terms come from structured searches (Wang, 'Port Fillings for Primary Pseudoperfect Numbers', arXiv:2605.21518, 2026), not bounded sweeps — the 9th/10th terms are 31 and 38 digits. The two 2026 terms are known terms but not certified as the in-order a(9)/a(10) (they were moved out of the data field), so the ordered next-cell is also open. Infinitude cannot fall to computation.

checked: OEIS A054377 (JSON: data, Irvine comment 2026-05-26, Butske-Jaje-Mayernik 1999 ref)

entries.quantity: A025418(35) — least integer that is a sum of 3 positive cubes in exactly 35 ways (first uncomputed cell; a(36)-a(39) and a(42) are known, so a(35), a(40), a(41) are the open cells)
entries.witness_object: An integer m with exactly 35 representations m = x^3+y^3+z^3 (x<=y<=z, positive), listed explicitly; leastness additionally requires an exhaustive cube-sum sweep below m
entries.witness_verifier: Representation count for a single m is a dependency-free double loop over x<=y<=(m/2)^(1/3) checking m-x^3-y^3 for cubeness (~10^8 ops at m~10^12); leastness needs a segmented sieve accumulating all i^3+j^3+k^3 <= m (~10^12 triple-sum updates — bounded, reproducible, but heavy)
entries.notes: b-file ends at a(34)=373248000000; Johnson's comment gives a(36)=496793088000, a(37)=980922617856, a(38)=209584584000, a(39)=644972544000, a(42)=970299000000 (sequence is not monotone, so these do not bound a(35)). The parent problem is asymptotic and a wall: Mahler 1936 disproved Hypothesis K for k=3 with r_3(n) >> n^(1/12) infinitely often; Erdős/Chowla independently proved r_k(n) >> n^(c/loglog n) for all k>=3; whether r_k(n) > n^c infinitely often for k>=4 is fully open (erdosproblems.com/322, edited Sep 2025). Record-count witnesses (integers with record r_3) also live in this table's orbit. Second OEIS ref: A025456.

checked: OEIS A025418 (JSON + b-file to n=34 + Johnson comments)
OEIS A025456 (JSON, Beregovsky Oct 2025 comments citing Mahler 1936, Chowla-Pillai 1936)

entries.quantity: A384927(5001) — max size of S in {1..5001} with a+b never dividing ab for distinct a,b in S; monotonicity forces the cell into {3528, 3529} (a(5000)=3528), so it is a single binary decision

lower.source: a(5000)=3528 from Kesarwani b-file (terms 1..5000) + monotonicity a(n+1)>=a(n)

upper.source: a(n+1) <= a(n)+1 (adding one element to the ground set adds at most one to the max)

entries.witness_object: Lower/closing: an explicit 3529-element subset of {1..5001} with a+b never dividing ab. Upper/closing: an UNSAT certificate (DRAT or ILP dual) that no 3529-element such set exists
entries.witness_verifier: Set check is O(k^2) ~ 6.2M integer divisibility tests ((a+b) | ab), dependency-free, sub-second; the UNSAT side is a max-independent-set decision on a 5001-vertex graph whose edges are exactly the pairs with a+b | ab — checkable with drat-trim or an LP-dual audit
entries.notes: Conjectured a(n)/n -> 1/2 (odd numbers are an independent set of density 1/2 since a+b even, ab odd). The open content is whether S can be 'substantially more' than the odds, and the a+b | 2ab variant; van Doorn showed density >= 25/28+o(1) forces a bad pair (via #301 discussion). Extending the table sharpens the empirical excess a(n)-ceil(n/2). Site notes 1 claimed proof and 11 comments not yet incorporated (accessed 2026-07-18) — scout should re-check before major effort. Asymptotic question itself is a wall; table cells are machine-decidable.

checked: OEIS A384927 (JSON + b-file to n=5000, tail verified a(5000)=3528)

entries.quantity: A062241(30) — smallest integer >= 2 that is not a sum of two positive 113-smooth numbers (prime(30)=113); first uncomputed cell of the finite proxy for f(n) = best smoothness bound with n = a+b

lower.source: Donovan Johnson, OEIS A062241 comment

upper.source: A062241(n) <= A045535(n-1) (Sungkawichai, OEIS formula + proof PDF at github.com/vzsky/OEIS-compute, Mar 2026); A045535(29) = 328878692999 (Dobbelaere b-file)

entries.witness_object: A specific integer m with 2570169839 < m < 328878692999 that is not a sum of two positive 113-smooth numbers (gives a(30) <= m); candidates likely come from quadratic-residue obstructions a la pseudosquares A045535
entries.witness_verifier: Enumerate all 113-smooth numbers s <= m by exponent recursion over the 30 primes (roughly 10^7-10^8 values at this scale), then confirm m-s is never 113-smooth via sorted-set membership — self-contained, minutes-to-hours on one box; no factoring of m needed
entries.notes: Exact determination of a(30) needs the full sweep from 2570169840 upward (each m usually certifies fast via a small smooth s with m-s smooth), bounded above by 3.29e11 — heavy but bounded. Parent asymptotic problem: Balog 1989 proved f(n) <<_eps n^(4/(9 sqrt e)+eps) (exponent ~0.2695), best verified bound per erdosproblems.com/334 (edited Apr 2026); conjecturally f(n) <= n^o(1). A search hit (Turku NT seminar) suggested a convolution-estimate improvement toward exponent 103/(256 sqrt e) ~ 0.244 — primary source NOT located, treat as unverified lead. Second OEIS ref: A045535 (fully computed to n=50, so upper bounds exist for cells through a(51)).

checked: OEIS A062241 (JSON + b-file to n=29 + Johnson 2010 comment + Sungkawichai formula Mar 2026)
OEIS A045535 (JSON, data through index 32; Dobbelaere b-file to n=50)

entries.quantity: Verification depth for '33 is never a difference of Mian-Chowla terms' (33 = A080200(1), the smallest integer not known to lie in A-A for the greedy Sidon sequence A005282)

lower.source: A. Reichert, OEIS A080200 comment: candidate non-differences (incl. 33) confirmed absent among all pairwise differences of the first 100000 Mian-Chowla terms; a(100000)=7135566931719

entries.witness_object: Either (a) indices i>j with a_i - a_j = 33, which settles membership positively and deletes A080200(1), or (b) a deeper verified frontier (first N > 100000 terms with 33 still absent)
entries.witness_verifier: The greedy sequence is deterministic: recompute A005282 to index i with a pairwise-sum bitset (2*a(i) bits; ~1.8 TB at i=100000, so segmented/compressed methods needed) and check the claimed pair — no compact certificate exists, verification = independent recomputation with published code
entries.notes: Erdős-Graham asked about the growth of A (trivially >> N^(1/3), conjectured N^(1/2-eps): wall) and whether A-A has positive density / contains every integer; 22 was confirmed in A-A (a_15 - a_14 = 204-182). If 33 truly appears, a finite computation closes the first cell; nonmembership is not finitely certifiable — hence exact_feasibility unknown. A080200 currently lists 50 conjectured non-differences. Memory, not CPU, is the binding constraint on deeper verification. Second OEIS ref: A005282 (Reichert 100000-term file, May 2026).

checked: OEIS A080200 (JSON: Voss 2003 origin, Pfoertner 2021 and Reichert 2026 verification comments)

entries.quantity: Computation frontier for the Ulam sequence u(1)=1, u(2)=2: largest X with all Ulam numbers <= X computed, supporting the empirical density ~0.073979 and quasi-period 21.6016 against Ulam's density-0 conjecture

lower.source: Gibbs & McCranie, 'The Ulam Numbers up to One Trillion' (2017): 73979274540 Ulam numbers below 10^12; separately McCranie computed the first 2.8e10 terms (2015-16) — still the frontier per OEIS A002858 links (checked 2026-07-18)

entries.witness_object: An extended frontier X' > 10^12 (all Ulam numbers <= X' with counts, density, wave statistics, and consecutive-pair census), computed with Gibbs's linear bitwise-convolution algorithm
entries.witness_verifier: No compact certificate: the sequence is deterministic, so verification = independent recomputation (linear-time bitset convolution, ~days at 10^12 scale); spot-audits of published counts (e.g. 73979274540 below 1e12, only x=1,2,3,47 with x,x+1 both Ulam in first 2.8e10 terms) guard transcription errors
entries.notes: Erdős/Ulam questions — density 0? infinitely many pairs differing by 2? eventually periodic differences? — are all asymptotic (wall); computation refutes density 0 empirically (density stable ~0.0740). Known structure: clumps with period 21.601584+, ratio to mean gap 13.52 close to 8/5; a(1e9)=13517664323. erdosproblems.com/342 flags claimed partial results in 18 unincorporated comments (accessed 2026-07-18) — scout should re-check before frontier work.

checked: OEIS A002858 (JSON: McCranie comments 2012-2016, Gibbs-McCranie 2017 link)
web search confirming no post-2017 frontier extension found

entries.quantity: A364132(23) — least n such that {1..n} contains an increasing 23-term sequence all of whose consecutive-segment sums are distinct (inverse of Erdős-Harzheim f(n); a(22)=52 is the last known term)

lower.source: a(22)=52, OEIS A364132 data (22 terms, keyword hard,more); a(23) >= a(22) by monotonicity (a valid 23-sequence contains a valid 22-sequence)

entries.witness_object: An increasing sequence a_1 < ... < a_23 <= n with all C(24,2)=276 segment sums sum_{u<=i<=v} a_i distinct (gives a(23) <= n); the exact cell additionally needs an exhaustive/UNSAT proof for n-1
entries.witness_verifier: Compute prefix sums and check all 276 pairwise differences distinct — trivial integer check; the nonexistence side is a small SAT/CP instance (choose 23 of ~55 values, alldifferent on segment sums), certifiable by DRAT
entries.notes: Open content: growth of f(n) — Weisenberg (via #874) gives f(n) >= (2+o(1)) sqrt(n); whether f(n) = o(n) is open; for the non-monotone variant g(n), Hegyvari 1986 proved (1/3+o(1))n <= g(n) and Coppersmith-Phillips (via #867) gives g(n) <= (2/3 - 1/512 + o(1))n — those asymptotics are walls (erdosproblems.com/357, edited Jan 2026). Companion cell: A364153(14) (non-monotone table, 13 terms known, a(13)=18) is an equally cheap SAT target. Erdős also asked whether sum 1/a_i converges for infinite versions.

entries.quantity: Density/growth of MacMahon's segmented numbers a_k (A002048): Andrews conjectures a_k ~ k log k / loglog k; no bounded quantity with a improvable record gap
entries.witness_object: None — the sequence is deterministic and cheaply computable to any index (b-file to 10000 terms exists; simple sieve excluding sums of consecutive earlier terms), so extending the table is not a record; the open content is purely asymptotic
entries.witness_verifier: No cheap witness exists for the asymptotic claims; table values themselves verify by direct recomputation (seconds), which is precisely why they carry no record value
entries.notes: Known partial results (erdosproblems.com/359, edited Dec 2025, paraphrased): Porubsky 1977 proved a_k < (log k)^eps * k log k/loglog k infinitely often, and limsup A(x)/pi(x) >= 1/log 2; Andrews 1975 studied the conjecture a_k ~ k log k/loglog k. Erdős asked (n=1 case) whether a_k/k -> infinity and a_k/k^(1+c) -> 0 for all c>0 — neither direction is finitely checkable. Empirical growth-curve extension is trivial (open-easy compute, no record semantics); flagged not-gap-shaped so the scout skips it.

checked: OEIS A002048 (JSON: Andrews 1975, Porubsky 1977 refs; Wu b-file to 10000)

entries.quantity: least n such that n-1, n, n+1 are all powerful (Erdos / Mollin-Walsh: conjectured nonexistent)

lower.source: no triple with least member below 7.38e28 — complete list of odd powerful pairs differing by 2 (OEIS A076445 main terms; erdosproblems.com/364 remarks)

entries.witness_object: a triple (n-1, n, n+1) with all three powerful (every prime factor to exponent >= 2); any such triple settles the problem affirmatively and gives the first upper bound
entries.witness_verifier: factor the three integers and check min prime-exponent >= 2 in each — one page of dependency-free code; quick pre-filter: middle element must be divisible by 4 (n-1, n+1 odd)
entries.notes: abc conjecture implies only finitely many triples; quadruples trivially impossible (one member is 2 mod 4). Chan (2025) and She (2025) rule out special triple shapes (middle a cube with wings p^3x^2 resp. p^2x^3). The movable rung is the verified bound, but raising 7.38e28 needs certified-complete enumeration of gap-2 powerful pairs: Alekseyev's extended A076445 table (33 terms) is explicitly conjectured-complete only (Pell families via A118894/Sentance), so even the range extension is expert-hard. Erdos also conjectured n_{k+2} - n_k > n_k^c for powerful n_k.

entries.quantity: A060355(40) — smallest k > 10^22 with k and k+1 both powerful (next consecutive-powerful pair; the counting function N(x) feeds the open (log x)^{O(1)} question)

lower.source: Donovan Johnson's complete enumeration below 10^22 (OEIS A060355 b-file, 39 terms)

upper.source: OEIS A060859 b-file term 31 (~2.31e22, powerful k^2-1 family; a valid A060355 term above 10^22)

entries.witness_object: a pair (k, k+1) both powerful with 1e22 < k < 2.3146e22 (tightens the a(40) bracket); the exact cell falls to an exhaustive powerful-number sweep of the bounded window
entries.witness_verifier: factor k and k+1, check all prime exponents >= 2 on both — milliseconds; for the exact cell, a sweep certificate: enumerate all a^2*b^3 (b squarefree) in the window segment-by-segment and scan for adjacent values differing by 1
entries.notes: The yes/no part (must one of n, n+1 be a square?) is already answered NO: Golomb 1970 (12167 = 23^3, 12168 = 2^3*3^2*13^2) and Walker 1976 (7^3 x^2 = 3^3 y^2 + 1 has infinitely many solutions). Open content: is N(x) = #pairs <= x bounded by (log x)^{O(1)}? Window contains ~1.1e11 powerful numbers (density 2.173*sqrt(x)); chunked c^2 d^3 enumeration + adjacency scan is hours-scale on one box and would add a new OEIS term (first extension since 2011). The same sweep advances #366's frontier. Of the 39 known pairs only 7 have neither member square (Schoenfield, Jun 2024).

entries.quantity: count of n with {n, n+1} both powerful and at least one member 3-full (Erdos-Graham: is (8,9) essentially the only one?) — known count = 2 below 10^22

lower.source: complete consecutive-powerful-pair list below 10^22 (Johnson, OEIS A060355 b-file); only (8,9) and (12167,12168) have a 3-full member (erdosproblems.com/366 remarks)

entries.witness_object: a third pair (n, n+1) both powerful with one member 3-full — in particular the first-ever example in the strict Lean orientation (n 2-full, n+1 3-full), which currently has ZERO known examples
entries.witness_verifier: factor n and n+1; check all exponents >= 2 on both and all exponents >= 3 on one member — trivial, dependency-free
entries.notes: Both known examples have the 3-full member FIRST (8 = 2^3 before 9 = 3^2; 12167 = 23^3 before 12168), so the exact Lean-stub orientation is even more open. abc implies finitely many such n (Turturean, per erdosproblems). Honest rung: extending the A060355 sweep 10^22 -> 10^23 (~7e11 powerful numbers, days-scale) raises the verified bound; constructive Pell-style witnesses look blocked because 3-fullness of a pair member is a much stronger Diophantine constraint. Weaker open sub-question (Er76d): any pair of consecutive 3-full integers at all. Page freshly edited 2026-07-16.

entries.quantity: limsup behavior of prod_{n <= m < n+k} B_2(m) / n^2 for fixed k >= 3, where B_2 = powerful part (A057521) — conjectured << n^{o(1)}
entries.witness_object: none that bounds the conjecture — it is an asymptotic << statement; individual n with record products are verifiable curios but no finite set decides
entries.witness_verifier: n/a for the conjecture itself; any single example (n, k) is checkable by factoring k integers and multiplying their powerful parts
entries.notes: van Doorn (per erdosproblems, page edited 2026-03-23): for k <= 2 trivially << n^2, but the clean <<_k n^2 version FAILS for all k >= 3 — prod over [n, n+2] is >> n^2 log n infinitely often. Equivalent up to constants to #935. Empirical sub-lane a scout could run: hunt record values of prod_{m in [n, n+3)} B_2(m)/n^2 via CRT constructions (large squares dividing three consecutive integers) — each example cheaply verifiable but moves no bound. r-full generalization (B_r, r >= 3) also open.

entries.quantity: growth of F(n) = largest prime factor of n(n+1) — conjectured F(n) >> (log n)^2 always, with F(n) < (log n)^{2+eps} infinitely often
entries.witness_object: none for the asymptotic conjecture; nearest checkable object is a large n with F(n) far below (log n)^2 (a very smooth pair n, n+1), each verifiable by factoring but deciding nothing
entries.witness_verifier: n/a for the conjecture; a single smooth-pair example is verified by factoring n and n+1 (milliseconds)
entries.notes: State of the art verified: Polya 1918 (F -> infinity), Mahler 1935 (>> loglog n), Schinzel 1967-68 (F(n) <= n^{O(1/logloglog n)} i.o.; liminf F(n)/loglog n >= 2/7 effective), and the current best lower bound Pasten 2024 (arXiv:2312.03566, Cor. 1.5): F(n) >> (loglog n)^2 / logloglog n. Empirical sub-lane: Stormer-Lehmer Pell enumeration of p-smooth n(n+1) pairs (largest-n-per-prime table = A002072) supplies certified record smooth pairs; a miner must source the current largest completed smoothness bound before any record claim — not sourced here.

entries.quantity: asymptotic density of {n : P(n) < P(n+1)} (conjectured 1/2); unconditional bracket via lower/upper density

lower.source: Lu & Wang, lower density of the set (and of its complement); cited [LuWa25] at erdosproblems #371, preprint hal-01797939 (2018)

upper.source: complement of the Lu-Wang lower bound for the complementary set (upper density)

entries.witness_object: none — density bounds move only by proof; no finite computation certifies an asymptotic density
entries.witness_verifier: n/a; empirical counts are trivially computable (largest-prime-factor sieve) but carry no bound
entries.notes: Nearly theorem-shaped: Teravainen 2018 proved the LOGARITHMIC density is exactly 1/2; Tao-Teravainen 2019: asymptotic density 1/2 at almost all scales; Wang 2021: full conjecture conditional on Elliott-Halberstam for friable integers. Empirics to 10^9: 499992458 terms (~x/2, within 0.5 sigma of binomial p=1/2 model; Greathouse, OEIS). Progression of lower bounds: 0.0099 (Erdos-Pomerance 1978), 0.05544 (de la Breteche-Pomerance-Tenenbaum 2005), 0.1063 (Wang 2017), 0.1356 (Wang 2018), 0.2017 (Lu-Wang). Low value for a machine scout — no witness lane.

entries.quantity: solutions of n! = a_1! a_2! ... a_k! with n-1 > a_1 >= ... >= a_k >= 2; largest known n = 16, Hickerson list conjectured complete

lower.source: exhaustive check: no solutions with 16 < n <= 10^5 (Jud McCranie, OEIS A003135 comment)

entries.witness_object: either a new solution n > 16 (would extend the Hickerson list {9, 10, 10, 16} and be a genuine event) or an extension of the exhaustive no-solution verification beyond the soft 10^5 frontier
entries.witness_verifier: prime-valuation comparison: v_p(n!) = sum floor(n/p^i) must equal sum_j v_p(a_j!) for every prime p <= n — pure integer arithmetic, milliseconds even for very large n; no big factorial products needed
entries.notes: Hickerson list: 9! = 7!3!3!2!, 10! = 7!6! = 7!5!3!, 16! = 14!5!2!. The k=2 case is verified to n <= 10^3000 (Habsieger 2019, after Caldwell 1994) and Erdos 1993 proved a_1 >= n - 5 loglog n (Bhat-Ramachandra: constant 1/log2). Luca 2007: finiteness follows from abc; also would follow from P(n(n+1))/log n -> infinity (#368). The general-k exhaustive frontier is still McCranie's 2005 check at 10^5 — softest verified bound in this chunk; candidates require n, n-1 composite plus a prime-free tail interval, so pushing to ~10^8 is an hours-scale sieve+recursion. A new SOLUTION is believed nonexistent; the honest machine rung is the verified range.

entries.quantity: order of growth of |D_k intersect [n]| for k = 3..6, where F(m) = least k >= 2 admitting a_1 < ... < a_k = m with a_1!...a_k! a perfect square (e.g., is |D_6 intersect [n]| >> n?)
entries.witness_object: none — a growth-rate/density question; individual memberships are cheaply certifiable (exhibit the tuple and check squareness) but move no bound
entries.witness_verifier: membership check for one m: exhibit a_1 < ... < a_k = m and verify a_1!...a_k! is a square via parity of prime valuations — milliseconds
entries.notes: Known structure (Erdos-Graham 1976 + 2025-26 OEIS work): D_2 = {squares > 1}; no D_k contains a prime; least element of D_6 = 527; D_k empty for k > 6; |D_3 cap [n]| = o(|D_4 cap [n]|); conjectured |F_3 cap [n]| ~ c sqrt(n) with c = 3.70975... (A389117). Active enumeration lanes (Tao, Wu, Corneth, Pfoertner, Sep 2025-Apr 2026): A388851 (F_3 members, b-file 5685 terms), A387184 (F_4 members, 10000 terms), A389148 (composites e > 6 with no 5-tuple, 10000 terms; first non-semiprime term 16523 = 13*31*41). See Tao arXiv:2603.27990 sect. 1.3. Extending these enumerations is easy machine work but there is no bounded record quantity for the stated problem.

entries.quantity: A030979(1375) — least k > 10^70 with binomial(2k,k) coprime to 105 (equivalently: all base-3 digits <= 1, base-5 digits <= 2, base-7 digits <= 3)

lower.source: Christopher E. Thompson's complete enumeration to 10^70 (OEIS A030979 b-file, 1374 terms; extends Alekseyev's first 62 terms)

entries.witness_object: any k > 10^70 with base-3 digits in {0,1}, base-5 digits in {0,1,2}, base-7 digits in {0,1,2,3} (Kummer/Lucas: no carries in k+k in each base) — first new term since 2015, upper-bounds a(1375)
entries.witness_verifier: three base conversions + digit-range checks with any bignum library — microseconds; no factorial or binomial computation needed
entries.notes: Graham's $1000 prize question is INFINITUDE of the sequence — that side is a wall (EGRS 1975 settles any two odd primes; three primes open; Bloom-Croot [BlCr25]: almost-all-digits version for large primes). Pomerance heuristic: ~x^0.02595 terms up to x, so new terms beyond 10^70 exist but are extremely sparse; naive digit-DFS explodes (2^147 base-3 strings to 10^70) — needs Thompson-style multi-base constraint propagation, hence plausible-not-easy, and exact-cell status beyond 10^70 is unproven at realistic scale. b-file unchanged as of OEIS rev 2026-06-28. Also note Graham expected 3160 to be the last term coprime to 3*5*7*11.

checked: OEIS A030979 (rev 2026-06-28) + b-file header (1374 terms, complete to 10^70)

entries.quantity: A388850(9) — least start u of a maximal run of exactly 10 consecutive integers whose product has its largest prime factor appearing with exponent >= 2 ('bad interval' of width 9)

upper.source: candidate interval found by Touch Sungkawichai (OEIS A388850 comment, Jan 2026)

entries.witness_object: a maximal bad interval [u, u+9] with u < 4928180396 (largest prime of the product to exponent >= 2; neither one-step extension keeps the property); exact value via exhaustive scan below the candidate
entries.witness_verifier: factor the <= 12 integers involved (~5e9 scale — trivial), take the largest prime p dividing the product, check its total exponent >= 2, and check both one-step extensions [u-1, u+9] and [u, u+10] break the property — dependency-free, milliseconds per interval
entries.notes: a(0..8) = 4, 8, 48, 1680, 76725, 332925, 7474752, 4541154, 75047565 known exact (von Bromssen, Sep 2025; note non-monotone, so no clean lower bracket published — exhaustive frontier is at least ~7.5e7 implied by exactness of a(8), exact frontier unstated). Open candidates: a(9) <= 4928180396, a(10) <= 40533366231, a(11) <= 111460496439, a(12) <= 436502026483 (Sungkawichai, Jan 2026), a(13) <= 17952448643078 (StijnC, per OEIS). A segmented largest-prime-factor sieve to 4.93e9 + sliding-window check settles a(9) in hours on one box, and the same infrastructure attacks a(10)-a(12). Each newly confirmed larger width is also a direct witness for Erdos's 'can v-u be arbitrarily large?' (Cambie heuristic: yes; Cramer would force v-u = v^{o(1)}; Ramachandra gives v-u <= v^{1/2+o(1)}). Best actionable cell in this chunk alongside #365.

entries.quantity: Largest member of A322293 = {k : A322292(k) <= k}, where A322292(k) = max over composites c < k of (c + lpf(c)) — the exceptional k where no composite c < k has c + lpf(c) > k; the set is conjectured finite (Erdős–Eggleton–Selfridge)

lower.source: OEIS A322293 b-file (Michel Marcus / Robert Israel; 100 terms, exhaustive below 10^8)

entries.witness_object: An integer k > 267680 (necessarily with k−1 prime) such that every composite c < k satisfies c + lpf(c) <= k
entries.witness_verifier: Only c in (k−sqrt(k), k) matter since lpf(c) <= sqrt(c): for each j = 2..ceil(sqrt(k)) with k−j composite, trial-divide k−j by primes <= j and require a factor; confirm k−1 prime (BPSW). O(sqrt(k)·pi(sqrt(k))) work — seconds at k ~ 1e12. Dependency-free.
entries.notes: A322292(k) is trivially <= k + sqrt(k); Erdős–Eggleton–Selfridge suggest exceptional k are finite and that the max may be >= k + (1−o(1))·sqrt(k) [Er79d p.73; ErGr80 p.74]. A322293 has exactly 100 known terms, complete below 10^8; largest 267680 (pair 267672/267680 after a long gap from 8742 — thinning consistent with finiteness). Bloom's page: equivalent to problem #430 (Adenwalla observation); Tao has a blog discussion; Tao marks it difficult. Record = any new exceptional k in (10^8, ∞): a segmented lpf-sieve scan 10^8 → 10^11 is GPU-hours (candidate k must have k−1 prime, which prunes hard), but further terms may simply not exist — graded plausible, not open-easy. Exact resolution (finiteness) is a wall.

checked: OEIS A322293 (JSON + b-file b322293.txt, all terms <= 1e8)

entries.quantity: A280992(8) — the next squarefree triangular number that is a product of consecutive primes (equivalently the next n > 715 with C(n,2) a product of consecutive primes); known terms 1, 3, 6, 15, 105, 210, 255255

lower.source: OEIS A280992: exhaustive over the first 5·10^6 triangular numbers (T(5e6) = 12500002500000); Corneth: any further term has a prime factor > prime(2000) = 17389

entries.witness_object: A triangular number T > 255255 equal to a product of consecutive primes p_i·p_{i+1}···p_j (squarefreeness automatic for distinct consecutive primes)
entries.witness_verifier: Check 8T+1 is a perfect square (integer sqrt), factor T, and confirm its prime factors are consecutive primes each to the first power. Big-int arithmetic only, milliseconds. Search-side: enumerate consecutive-prime-window products P < X and test 8P+1 square — no triangular-number scan needed.
entries.notes: Known n with C(n,2) such a product: 4, 6, 15, 21, 715 (terms 6, 15, 105, 210, 255255). Erdős–Graham [ErGr80 p.74] wrote that proving finiteness even for C(n,2) seems hopeless; their guess that it never happens for 3<=k<=n−3 was refuted by Weisenberg: C(7,3)=5·7, C(10,4)=2·3·5·7, C(14,4)=7·11·13, C(15,6)=5·7·11·13. Cheap record available: the exhaustive floor 1.25e13 can be pushed to ~1e24 with only ~4e10 square tests (prime pairs p·p'' with p <= 1e12 dominate; longer windows are far fewer) — GPU-hours for an 11-order-of-magnitude lower-bound record on a(8). But heuristic solution count grows only like log log X (pair case: sum of 1/sqrt(8·p·p'') over consecutive prime pairs), so an actual 8th term is unlikely in reach — witness graded hard. a(8) may not exist at all → exact unknown.

checked: OEIS A280992 (JSON; Shepherd 2017 search bound, Corneth 2017 prime-factor bound)

entries.quantity: A375071(27) — least k >= 1 such that 27·28···(26+k) divides (27+k)···(26+2k) (first uncomputed cell; a(0)–a(26) known, a(26) = 5048891644620)
entries.witness_object: A pair (n=27, k) with 27·28···(26+k) | (27+k)···(26+2k). Unverified candidate: k = 18253129921815 = A375077(13) − 27, from the empirical cross-map A375071(n) = A375077((n−1)/2) − n which holds at every known odd cell (checked n=21,23,25)
entries.witness_verifier: Divisibility ⟺ v_p(C(26+2k, k)) >= v_p(C(26+k, k)) for all primes p (Kummer carry counts). For p > 26 a failing p requires k mod p ∈ (p−27, p), so verification = sieve-scan of k mod p over primes p <= 26+2k (~5e11 primes at k ≈ 1.8e13; a GPU-day). Needs only a prime sieve + modular arithmetic.
entries.notes: Erdős–Straus: does such k exist for every n? (Open; existence for each computed n is witnessed.) Mehta computed n <= 18; OEIS extended to a(26) with active work Mar 2026 (Kesarwani comment; SharkyKesa listed as currently working on erdosproblems.com — coordinate, don't race). The (n,k) → (n+k−1, n) transform ties this to A375077/problem 396: transform preserves validity but NOT minimality (a(7) = 8171 is much smaller than the transform predicts), so the candidate a(27) above is an upper-bound witness at best and must be verified before recording. Exhaustive minimality sweeps at the ~1e13 scale are demonstrated feasible by the A375077 project → exact cell.

checked: OEIS A375077 (JSON; cross-map verified numerically at n=21,23,25)

entries.quantity: Asymptotic constant c in f(n) − 2n ~ c·n/log n, where f(n) = A193429(n) = minimum possible largest factor when n! is written as a product of integers all > n
entries.witness_object: none
entries.witness_verifier: No cheap witness: the open content is the existence of an asymptotic constant, which no finite table certifies. A193429 is computable (b-file reaches n = 168) and extension is cheap, but moves nothing.
entries.notes: Erdős–Guy–Selfridge [EGS82] proved f(n) − 2n ≍ n/log n (both order bounds); the open question is whether f(n) − 2n ~ c·n/log n for a specific constant c. The stub's Lean formalization marks 'research solved' — that plausibly reflects the proven ≍ statement; the constant question is the open residue per erdosproblems.com. a(n) = 0 only for n = 1, 2 (Adams-Watters); sequence appears O(n). Empirical c-estimation from an extended table (b-file n <= 168, Kesarwani) is possible scout work but certifies nothing → not gap-shaped.

entries.quantity: A388302(80) — least spread b_t − b_1 over factorizations 80! = b_1···b_t into t >= 2 distinct increasing integer factors (first uncertified cell; table certified for n = 2..79 with witness tuples)

upper.source: Trivial construction 80! = 2·3···80 (t = n−1, spread n−2), per A388302 comment (D. A. Corneth)

entries.witness_object: A factorization 80! = b_1···b_t into >= 2 distinct increasing integers with spread b_t − b_1 < 78; heuristically factors cluster near (80!)^{1/t} (Liou geometric-mean window)
entries.witness_verifier: Multiply the b_i with big-int arithmetic, compare to 80!, check strict increase. Milliseconds, dependency-free. (Certifying the cell exactly additionally needs an exhaustive sweep over smaller spreads.)
entries.notes: f(n) = A388302(n). Headline open question: is f(n) = 1 infinitely often — f(n)=1 ⟺ n! = m(m+1) ⟺ 4·n!+1 is a perfect square; known only n = 2, 3 (Brocard-adjacent). Berend–Osgood 1992: #{n<=N : f(n)=m} = o(N) for each fixed m; Bui–Pratt–Zaharescu 2023: ≪_m N^{33/34}; Luca 2002: ABC ⇒ f(n) → ∞. Existing table n = 2..79 by Corneth (PARI program + tuple list linked from OEIS); a(n) <= min(A061057(n), n−2). Record: extend the certified table from a(80) onward — candidate discovery via the geometric-mean window heuristic is hours; exhaustive minimality per cell is the honest certification bar. Entry created Sep 2025; no newer bounds found.

checked: OEIS A388302 (JSON; Corneth tuple list n=2..79, Liou heuristic program links)
erdosproblems.com/393 (context only, paraphrased; BeOs92, BPZ23, Lu02 citations)

entries.quantity: Asymptotics of Sum_{n<=x} t_2(n), where t_2(n) = A344005(n) = least m with n | m(m+1)
entries.witness_object: none
entries.witness_verifier: No cheap witness: the open content is a mean-value asymptotic; A344005 itself has an O((log n)^2) exact algorithm (Sloane, Jul 2021) with tables to 10^7 already in OEIS, so no finite computation is a record.
entries.notes: Erdős–Hall 1978 [ErHa78] proved Sum_{n<=x} t_2(n) ≪ x²·logloglog x/loglog x, settling Erdős's o(x²) conjecture; trivial lower bound ≫ x²/log x from t_2(p) = p−1; Erdős–Hall conjecture o(x²/(log x)^c) for every c < log 2 — that conjecture is the open gap and is not witness-movable. Structural facts: t_2(n) <= n−1 with equality iff n is a prime power; <= n/2 − 1 for non-prime-powers; >= (sqrt(4n+1)−1)/2 (Sloane 2021). Secondary discrete asks on the page — behaviour of t_{n−3}(n!), and whether infinitely many n satisfy t_k(n!) < t_{k−1}(n!) − 1 for all 1 <= k < n (known only for n = 10, Erdős–Hall–Selfridge) — could seed a future bounded-search lane (find a second such n) but are not the headline bounded quantity, so this entry stays not_gap_shaped.

checked: OEIS A344005 (JSON; Sloane algorithm comments, tables to 1e7)

entries.quantity: A375077(16) — least k such that k(k−1)···(k−16) divides C(2k,k) (first uncomputed cell; a(1)–a(15) listed, a(15) = 2394789405254721)

lower.source: Monotonicity (the n=15 product divides the n=16 product, so a(n) is nondecreasing) chained to certified-minimal a(13) (Dehorty exhaustive search + Lean 4 Small Prime Barrier sieve, OEIS A375077 comment)

entries.witness_object: An integer k with k(k−1)···(k−16) | C(2k,k); existence guaranteed for every window length (Pomerance 2014: the valid-n set has density 1). Minimal such k is the cell value
entries.witness_verifier: Factor the 17 consecutive integers k−16..k (Pollard rho at ~1e17 scale, trivial); for each prime p dividing the product check v_p(product) <= number of carries when adding k + k in base p (Kummer); explicitly include all primes p < 2·16+3. Seconds, dependency-free beyond big-int + rho.
entries.notes: Flagship machine-shaped cell in this chunk, but ACTIVELY OWNED: minimality of a(8)–a(13) is certified by exhaustive search plus a small-prime sieve whose completeness ('Small Prime Barrier Theorem') is formally verified in Lean 4 + Mathlib (Justin Dehorty, Mar 2026; repo github.com/jdehorty/erdos396 with validation certificates — linked from OEIS, not independently audited here). Certification status of listed a(14), a(15) is not stated in the OEIS comment; presuming them minimal would lift the floor for a(16) to >= 2394789405254721. Expected a(16) ~ 1e16–1e17 by growth pattern → days-to-weeks with the published optimizations. Dehorty and SharkyKesa are listed as currently working on erdosproblems.com/396 — coordinate rather than race; certifying a(14)/a(15) minimality is a complementary sub-record. Solutions transform to problem 389 upper bounds via (n,k) → (n+k−1, n). Context: n+1 | C(2n,n) always (Catalan); Pomerance [Po14] also shows the set of n with n | C(2n,n) has upper density < 1/3.

checked: OEIS A375077 (JSON; Dehorty Mar 2026 certification comment + repo links)
WebSearch cross-check for newer work (none found beyond Mar 2026 state)

entries.quantity: Least n > 7 with n! + 1 a perfect square (Brocard–Ramanujan; conjecturally none — known solutions n = 4, 5, 7 with m = 5, 11, 71)

lower.source: Epstein–Glickman C++ search 2020 (GitHub, unrefereed; cited via Wikipedia). Published floor: 10^9 (Berndt–Galway, Ramanujan J. 2000); intermediate: 10^12 (Matson, unsolvedproblems.org, quadratic-residue/Legendre method) and 4·10^9 (Tim Peters, OEIS A085692 comment)

entries.witness_object: Historic: a pair (n, m) with n > 7 and n! + 1 = m². For the range record: a QNR certificate {(n, p_n)} giving, for every n in (10^15, N], a prime p_n with n!+1 a quadratic non-residue mod p_n
entries.witness_verifier: Claimed solution at moderate n: integer sqrt of n!+1 (feasible to n ~ 1e6-1e7 directly; beyond that, overwhelming multi-prime QR evidence is cheap per prime via n! mod p product-tree computation). Certificate spot-check: recompute n! mod p_n and check Legendre(n!+1, p_n) = −1 for sampled n.
entries.notes: Open since 1876 (Brocard) / 1913 (Ramanujan). Overholt 1993: weak ABC ⇒ finitely many solutions. Naciri 2025 [Na25, per erdosproblems.com]: finiteness if m±1 is k-free (any k >= 2) or a prime power; if 7-free then n = 4, 5, 7 are the only solutions. A085692: the next square, if any, exceeds 10^20000 (Wasserman 2005) and a(4) > (10^12+1)! per Matson's claimed range. FRESHNESS FLAG: the 10^12 and 10^15 tiers are unrefereed code-based claims (Matson PDF; Epstein–Glickman repo) — the safest citable floor is Berndt–Galway 10^9; recorded value uses the strongest claim with provenance flagged. Range extension past 10^15 via GPU QR-sieving is a plausible lane; a 4th solution would be historic. Secondary gap on this problem's other OEIS ref A141399 (k whose k(k+1) prime support is a consecutive run from 2): 28 terms, largest 633555, complete to 5·10^8 (Chandler 2009), and a(29) > 2.29·10^25 if it exists (Resta 2019).

checked: OEIS A085692 (JSON; Berndt–Galway link, Matson link, Peters + Radcliffe + Wasserman comments)
arXiv 2004.09256 inspected and REJECTED as a bound source (Maiti; claims a proof, unreviewed)
erdosproblems.com/398 (context only, paraphrased; Ov93, Na25 citations)

entries.quantity: Growth/normal-order constant of f(n) = A049108(n), the number of iterations of Euler phi needed to send n to 1 (does f(n)/log n converge, etc.)
entries.witness_object: none
entries.witness_verifier: No cheap witness: A049108 is trivially computable to any range (log_3 n ≪ f(n) ≪ log_2 n pins every value between ~log n bounds), and the open content is an asymptotic normal-order statement no finite prefix can move.
entries.notes: Pillai 1929 [Pi29]: log_3 n < f(n) < log_2 n for all large n; Shapiro 1950 [Sh50]: f is essentially multiplicative. Erdős–Granville–Pomerance–Spiro 1990 [EGPS90] answered the problem's first two questions affirmatively CONDITIONAL on a form of the Elliott–Halberstam conjecture — the unconditional statement is the wall. Also expected: for almost all n the largest prime factor of the k-th phi-iterate is n^{o(1)} whenever k → ∞. Discussed in Guy B41. A049108 = A003434 + 1. No bounded machine record identified.

checked: OEIS A049108 (JSON; Pillai-type bounds in formula field)
erdosproblems.com/408 (context only, paraphrased; Pi29, Sh50, EGPS90 citations)

entries.quantity: A229487(n), n = 1..53 — per-cell greatest integer whose iteration x → phi(x)+1 reaches prime(n); every listed maximum is conjectural (max-type record surface for Finucane's problem)

lower.value: per-cell table of 53 conjectured maxima (largest listed: a(53) = 54582 for p = 241; e.g. a(6) = 138 for p = 13, a(1) = 2, a(2) = 6)
lower.source: OEIS A229487 (T. D. Noe; forward search, bound unstated — values explicitly 'conjectured')

entries.witness_object: An integer x > A229487(n) whose phi+1-trajectory ends at prime(n), for any n <= 53 (e.g. any x > 138 reaching 13) — beats a listed cell
entries.witness_verifier: Iterate x → phi(x)+1: strictly decreasing on composites (phi(x) <= x − sqrt(x)), fixes primes, so the trajectory terminates in < x steps (practically ~log x); needs only factorization of iterates, all < x. Milliseconds at x ~ 1e12. Dependency-free.
entries.notes: Finucane's problem: every trajectory of x → phi(x)+1 reaches a prime (trivially, by descent); A039651 counts the steps; Cambie notes F(n) = 1 infinitely often and F(n) = o(n) is trivial — Bloom reads the intended open content as good upper bounds for the iteration count F(n). Two machine lanes on the A229487 surface: (1) forward phi-sieve over all x <= 1e9–1e10 (hours) rederives every basin and surfaces any converger beating a listed cell — Noe's original search bound is unstated, so even a clean replication with an explicit bound upgrades the table's provenance; (2) per-cell CERTIFICATION: enumerate the full inverse-image tree of prime(n) under x → phi(x)+1 (preimages of y are the inverse-totient set of y−1, finite and computable since phi(x) >= sqrt(x/2) forces x <= 2(y−1)²); if the recursion terminates, the conjectured maximum becomes exact — converting a 'conjectured' OEIS cell into a theorem is a legitimate record → exact cell. The page also poses the analogous sigma(n)−1 iteration (does it always reach a prime?), which has no OEIS record surface here.

checked: erdosproblems.com/409 (context only, paraphrased; Cambie/Tao acknowledgments, Guy B41)

entries.quantity: A007497(1501) — next term of the iterated sum-of-divisors trajectory 2, σ(2), σ(σ(2)), … underlying the question whether lim_k σ_k(n)^(1/k) = ∞

lower.source: OEIS A007497 b-file (C. R. Greathouse IV; first 200 terms T. D. Noe)

entries.witness_object: a(1501) = σ(a(1500)) together with the complete prime factorization of a(1500) (prime powers + primality certificates for each prime)
entries.witness_verifier: Re-multiply the claimed prime powers and check the product equals the b-file value of a(1500); check each prime with Pratt/ECPP certificates; compute σ = Π (p^(k+1)-1)/(p-1) in bignum arithmetic and compare to the claimed a(1501). Dependency-free, seconds.
entries.notes: Underlying problem (Erdős–Granville–Pomerance–Spiro 1990, problem (iii) p.169; Guy B9) is explicitly not resolvable by finite computation — the trajectory extension is the machine-record lane, feeding growth heuristics (Cloitre's conjectured (1/2)log n < a(n+1)/a(n) < 2 log n; divisibility patterns checked to n=1000 per OEIS). Factor-chain propagation (σ splits over prime powers of the previous term) keeps most steps in ECM range, but a single hard 150+ digit cofactor (e.g. p+1 for a large prime factor p) can stall the chain — hence plausible, not open-easy. Cohen–te Riele (Exp. Math. 5, 1996) is the classical computational study.

checked: OEIS A007497 + b-file (1500 terms, last term 1393 digits)
Cohen–te Riele Exp. Math. 5 (1996) — cited in OEIS, not read

entries.quantity: Smallest solution n of φ(n)+φ(n+φ(n)) = n whose odd part is outside {1,3,5,7,35,47} — equivalently the smallest exceptional prime p ≡ 7 (mod 8) with φ((3p-1)/4) = (p+1)/2 (the r=2 case of Erdős–Graham's g_{k+r}(n) = 2 g_k(n) for g(n) = n+φ(n))

lower.source: S. Steinerberger, arXiv:2504.08023 (2025); reduction and search bound as summarized in erdosproblems.com/411 remarks

entries.witness_object: a prime p ≡ 7 (mod 8), p > 1e10, satisfying φ((3p-1)/4) = (p+1)/2, supplied with the prime factorization of (3p-1)/4
entries.witness_verifier: Check p prime (BPSW + certificate) and p ≡ 7 mod 8; re-multiply the claimed factorization of (3p-1)/4 and primality-check its factors; compute φ from the factorization; compare with (p+1)/2. Milliseconds given the certificate.
entries.notes: Min-type quantity: a found exceptional prime creates the upper bound and yields a brand-new solution family (disproving Cambie's conjecture that none exist). Heuristically the exceptional set may be empty — tied to whether φ(m) = 2(m+1)/3 has infinitely many solutions — so the cheap-witness hunt is graded hard. The complementary record (pushing the exclusion bound 1e10 → 1e12 with a segmented φ sieve over numbers ≡ 5 mod 6) is a days-scale job but is auditable only by recomputation, not by a succinct certificate. Known solution families (per erdosproblems.com/411, paraphrased): odd part in {1,3,5,7,35,47} times powers of 2; base r=2 solutions n=10 and 94; Weintraub found period-9 and a period-25 identity at n=3114 with ratio 729; Cambie found further sporadic period-4 identities. A383044 b-file has 123 terms; De Vlieger verified the family structure up to 2^24.

entries.quantity: A051572(524) — next term of the iterated-σ trajectory of seed 5; the frontier of the (m,n) = (2,5) instance of the van Wijngaarden/Erdős merging conjecture (∃ i,j with σ_i(m) = σ_j(n))

lower.value: 523 terms known; a(523) has 437 digits; no common value with the 1500 known iterates of seed 2 (intersection of b-files checked empty 2026-07-18)
lower.source: OEIS A051572 b-file (N. J. A. Sloane) + fresh collision check against A007497 b-file done for this entry

entries.witness_object: a(524) = σ(a(523)) with the complete prime factorization of a(523); the grand prize witness for the (2,5) instance would be indices (i,j) with σ_i(2) = σ_j(5) plus the factor-chain certificates of both trajectories
entries.witness_verifier: Same scheme as A007497: re-multiply claimed prime powers to reproduce a(523), primality certificates for each prime, compute σ = Π (p^(k+1)-1)/(p-1), compare. For a merge witness: verify both trajectory chains and the equality of the two terminal values. Dependency-free bignum arithmetic.
entries.notes: Erdős attributed the conjecture to van Wijngaarden (1950s); Erdős–Graham thought nothing provable soon; Selfridge reported numerical evidence suggesting the answer may be NO — i.e. the merge witness may not exist, so the durable machine record is extending both trajectories (A051572 at 523 terms is far behind A007497 at 1500) and re-running the collision check. Bootstrap re-factoring of the 523-step chain from seed 5 is days of ECM work with the usual hard-cofactor risk. Cross-refs: A007497 (seed 2), A257348. See also problems 413 and 414.

checked: OEIS A051572 + b-file (523 terms, last 437 digits)
OEIS A007497 b-file (collision check: empty intersection)

entries.quantity: A005236(10001) — next barrier for ω (an n with m + ω(m) ≤ n for all m < n); current frontier a(10000) = 2054598

lower.source: OEIS A005236 b-file (C. R. Greathouse IV)

entries.witness_object: a claimed next barrier n0 > 2054598 (or a batch of new terms extending the b-file)
entries.witness_verifier: Segmented smallest-prime-factor sieve computing ω(m) for all m < n0; check max_{m<n0} (m + ω(m)) ≤ n0. O(n0 log log n0) time, dependency-free; seconds at 1e7, minutes at 1e9.
entries.notes: Infinitude of ω-barriers is OPEN (Erdős believed yes; Guy B8; Erdős–Graham noted sieve methods not yet strong enough). Recent movement: per erdosproblems.com/413 remarks (page edited 2026-04-17), Lau (2026, arXiv:2604.15042) answered the ε-relaxation (∃ε>0 with infinitely many n s.t. m + εω(m) ≤ n for all m < n) positively and proved a weakened main statement with the condition restricted to m ≤ n − C; we checked only the abstract, not the theorem statements — treat as reported. Erdős (1979) proved barriers for F(n) = Π k_i (product of exponents) have positive density; Selfridge: largest Ω-barrier below 1e5 is 99840. Related: Tao–Teräväinen arXiv:2512.01739.

checked: web search for Lau/Tao–Teräväinen barrier results

entries.quantity: Whether V(2x)/V(x) → 2 and whether an asymptotic formula exists for V(x) = #{n ≤ x : φ(m) = n solvable} (the totient value-counting function)
entries.witness_object: none — the open content is a limit/asymptotic statement; erdosproblems.com marks it not resolvable by finite computation
entries.witness_verifier: none exists; finite tabulations of V (A264810, b-file to 1e4) are trivially recomputable but certify nothing about the limit
entries.notes: Order of magnitude essentially settled by proof, not computation: Pillai (1929) V(x) = o(x); Erdős (1935) V(x) = x(log x)^(-1+o(1)); Maier–Pomerance (1988) V(x) = (x/log x)·e^((C+o(1))(logloglog x)^2); Ford (1998) sharp two-sided ≍ with explicit constants C1, C2, C3 — just short of an asymptotic formula, which is exactly what remains open. Guy B36. No witness lane: excluded from the records hunt.

entries.quantity: lim_{x→∞} V(x)/V'(x), where V(x) = #{totient values ≤ x} and V'(x) = #{φ(m) : 1 ≤ m ≤ x} — does the limit exist, and is it > 1?
entries.witness_object: none — limit-existence question; no finite computation certifies it
entries.witness_verifier: none exists; both counting functions are OEIS-tabulated to 1e4 (A264810, A061070) and empirical ratios are recomputable, but carry no certificate value
entries.notes: Trivially V'(x) ≤ V(x). Erdős (1998) suggested the limit may even be infinite (per erdosproblems.com/417). The formal-conjectures Lean file inverts the ratio to V'(x)/V(x) for boundedness — a technical choice only. Ford's totient machinery (Fo98, see problem 416) is the relevant proof tool; Tao arXiv:2309.02325 is linked from A061070. No witness lane: excluded from the records hunt.

entries.quantity: d*(421) = supremum of asymptotic densities achievable by sequences 1 ≤ d_1 < d_2 < … with all contiguous products Π_{u≤i≤v} d_i distinct (question: is d* = 1?)

lower.source: Selfridge construction, per erdosproblems.com/421 remarks (cf. problem 786)

upper.value: 1 (trivial ceiling; the conjecture is that it is attained)
upper.source: trivial

entries.witness_object: none for the density claim — density is asymptotic, so no finite prefix certifies it; distinctness of all contiguous products IS checkable on finite prefixes (hash the O(N^2) products), which validates the greedy data lane but cannot close the gap
entries.witness_verifier: for prefix data only: recompute all contiguous products of a claimed prefix with bignum arithmetic and check for duplicates; O(N^2) products — feasible to N ~ 1e4-1e5
entries.notes: HOT: erdosproblems.com/421 shows a claimed solution posted in its comments (unreviewed as of 2026-07-18, 43 comments, several users including Bloom and Alexeev mark it tractable) — the scout should re-check upstream status before investing. Greedy sequence A389544 (Sungkawichai, Jan 2026) has 20000 terms with a(20000) = 20163, prefix density ≈ 0.9919 and rising (complement A390848: 1, 6, 12, 16, …), consistent with the conjectured answer yes. Source: ErGr80 p.84.

entries.quantity: W(422) = largest N such that Hofstadter's Q-sequence f (f(1)=f(2)=1, f(n)=f(n-f(n-1))+f(n-f(n-2))) is verified well-defined (both back-indices ≥ 1) for all n ≤ N

lower.source: M. Eric Carr, Jul 2023, comment in OEIS A005185 (earlier record 1e10: Roman Pearce, 2014)

entries.witness_object: an extended computation certifying well-definedness to N' > 3e10 — there is no succinct certificate; the computed prefix itself is the witness
entries.witness_verifier: independent recomputation of the recurrence, checking n - f(n-1) ≥ 1 and n - f(n-2) ≥ 1 at every step; empirically f(n) ≈ n/2 so accesses span ~n/2 back — storage is the binding constraint (~4 bytes/term ⇒ ~120 GB at 3e10); verification cost equals computation cost
entries.notes: The Erdős–Graham/Hofstadter questions — does f miss infinitely many integers, and what is its behavior — are open and explicitly not machine-closable; it is not even known that f is total, which is exactly what the verified range tracks. Advancing 3e10 → 1e11+ is a memory-engineering job (mmap/disk streaming, days), a clean citable record with a 2014→2023 precedent chain in OEIS. Context literature: Pinn (1999/2000) order-and-chaos analyses; Deane–Gentile arXiv:2311.13854 (diluted variant with existence proofs); Guy E31.

entries.quantity: Growth exponent θ = inf{t : a_n ≪ n^(t+o(1))} for the Hofstadter consecutive-sum sequence (a_1=1, a_2=2, a_k = least integer > a_{k-1} expressible as a sum of ≥ 2 consecutive earlier terms)

lower.value: 1 (a_n ≥ n trivially; refined lower bound a_n = n + Ω(log log n))

upper.value: 688/413 + o(1) ≈ 1.6659 (as stated on erdosproblems.com via Cushman's convex-set sumset bound; Tang's own abstract states 4175/2506 + o(1) ≈ 1.6660)
upper.source: Q. Tang, arXiv:2603.09939 (2026), exponent via Cushman [Cu25]; erdosproblems.com/423 remarks

entries.witness_object: none — the exponent moves only by proof (it is coupled to the convex-set sumset exponent c via θ = 1/(c-1); Erdős–Hegyvári's conjectured c = 2 would give a_n ≤ n^(1+o(1))); extending the A005243 b-file is trivial (OEIS keyword easy) and not a record
entries.witness_verifier: none exists for the asymptotic exponent; finite prefixes are recomputable in seconds
entries.notes: MAJOR 2026 movement: the original omission question is now CLOSED — Bolan and Tang independently proved infinitely many integers never appear (a_n − n is nondecreasing and unbounded), per erdosproblems.com/423 (edited 2026-03-23) and Tang arXiv:2603.09939. The hub stub still tags the whole problem open: the asymptotics half genuinely remains open (truth likely a_n = n + o(n)), but the solved half should be recorded on the next hub refresh. Asked by Hofstadter (Erdős says inspired by Ulam); Guy E31; b-file 1000 terms, a(1000) = 1149.

entries.quantity: Lower asymptotic density of the Hofstadter product-minus-one closure {2,3} -> a_i*a_j - 1 (A005244); conjectured positive
entries.witness_object: None: no finite object certifies positive lower density. Counting-function data (b-file, 10000 terms) gives empirical density estimates only.
entries.witness_verifier: No cheap witness exists: density is an asymptotic property; any finite prefix of the sequence is consistent with both answers. Membership of an individual integer IS cheaply verifiable (BFS over products a_i*a_j-1 below the target), but that moves nothing.
entries.notes: erdosproblems.com marks this 'cannot be resolved with a finite computation'. Known structure: no integer == 1 (mod 3) ever appears (residues stay in {0,2} mod 3), so upper density <= 2/3; the original 'almost all integers' phrasing in ErGr80/Guy E31 is trivially false and the intended question (Er77c) is positive lower density. OEIS keyword 'nice,easy' refers to term generation, not the problem. A possible scout instrument (non-record): compute counting function c(x)/x to large x from the closure BFS to watch for density stabilization, but this cannot certify anything.

checked: Guy UPINT section E31 (via OEIS reference)

entries.quantity: A000445(8) = Lambda(8,2) — latest possible first occurrence (limsup over primes p) of a pair of consecutive 8th-power residues mod p; first uncomputed cell of the Lehmer-Lehmer table

lower.source: Don Reble, computation reported on SeqFan mailing list (recorded in OEIS A000445 comment by C. E. Thompson, Jan 2020)

upper.source: Don Reble, same computation (OEIS A000445 comment)

entries.witness_object: A consistency certificate in the Mills/Lehmer style: an assignment of 8th-power-residue characters to a finite set of small primes that (a) is realizable for infinitely many p (finite CRT/Dirichlet check) and (b) forces no consecutive pair of 8th-power residues below some r > 1499876. Companion cheap exhibit: an explicit prime p with first consecutive pair at r (raises the finite-p record and guides the pattern).
entries.witness_verifier: For an explicit prime p and claimed r: for t = 1..r-1 check not both t^((p-1)/8) == 1 and (t+1)^((p-1)/8) == 1 (mod p) — pure modular exponentiation, dependency-free. For the limsup lower bound: verify the residue-character pattern is multiplicatively consistent and blocks all pairs below r (finite combinatorial check over the pattern).
entries.notes: Gap of only 8449 between Reble's bounds has stood since 2019/2020 (OEIS keyword 'more,hard'; freshness re-checked via web search 2026-07-18, nothing newer found). Values Lambda(k,2) for k=2..7: 9, 77, 1224, 7888, 202124, 1649375 (Lehmer-Lehmer 1962 / Bierstedt-Mills 1963 / Dunton 1965 / LLM 1963 / BLL 1964, per OEIS). Note a(8) < a(7). Hildebrand 1991 proved Lambda(k,2) finite for ALL k, so every cell k >= 8 is a legitimate finite target. Remaining fully-open content of #436: is Lambda(k,3) finite for odd k >= 5 (known: Lambda(3,3)=23532, LLMS 1962; = infinity for even k and for all m >= 4 by Lehmer-Lehmer + Graham 1964), plus growth rates. Exact determination of a(8) closed the same way as k<=7 is a bounded (if large) computation — good expert-tooling target.

checked: erdosproblems.com/436 (paraphrased; values Lambda(k,m) cross-checked against OEIS data)
web search for post-2020 improvements (none found)
Hildebrand, Michigan Math. J. 38 (1991) via OEIS link

entries.quantity: A386620(184) = n_184 — smallest k > 368 such that (k-1)(k-2)...(k-184) has no prime factor in (184, 368); first uncomputed cell (b-file complete through n=183)
entries.witness_object: An integer k > 368 such that for each of the 31 primes p in (184,368), k mod p is NOT in {1,...,184} (equivalently k mod p is 0 or in {185 mod p,...,p-1}); any such k upper-bounds the cell, and a sieve exhaustion below k pins the exact value.
entries.witness_verifier: Candidate check is 31 modular reductions (primes 191..367) — trivially dependency-free and instant. Exactness additionally requires certifying no smaller k > 368 works: a residue-sieve scan up to k (heavy: a(183) = 167981058401851841 ~ 1.7e17, so a(184) likely needs a large wheeled sieve, but it is a bounded computation the b-file authors have repeatedly done).
entries.notes: The open PROBLEM is the asymptotic estimate of n_k, which is a wall for machines: van Doorn-Tang (arXiv:2606.19863, June 2026, in OEIS formula section) prove n_k > exp(c (log k)^2/loglog k); trivial upper n_k < e^{O(k)} (PNT / product of primes in (k,2k), Adenwalla observation on erdosproblems.com); Erdos conjectured k^d-beating growth but e^{o(k)}. The RECORD lane is table extension: b-file has exactly 183 terms (Kalogeropoulos 1-70, Chai Wah Wu 71-92, Greathouse 93-183). Because each candidate k is admissible iff k avoids 184 residues mod each of 31 primes, admissible density is prod (p-184)/p over p in (191..367) ~ tiny — terms jump erratically (a(36)=579124 vs a(183)~1.7e17), so budget per new cell is uncertain; grade honest: days of sieve, not hours.

checked: OEIS A386620 (JSON + b-file, confirmed 183 terms, a(183)=167981058401851841)
arXiv:2606.19863 (van Doorn-Tang, existence + statement of lower bound confirmed via arXiv listing)

entries.quantity: limsup_n (f(n) - 2*prime(n)) where f(n) = min_{0<i<n}(prime(n-i)+prime(n+i)) — conjectured infinite (Erdos-Graham / Pomerance); scout proxy record: max_{2<=n<=N} A389677(n)

lower.source: Pomerance, The prime number graph, Math. Comp. 33 (1979) 399-408 (proved limsup >= 2)

entries.witness_object: An index n with f(n) - 2*prime(n) exceeding the current empirical record 58 (attained at n=14357; this chunk's computation over 2<=n<=20000, validated against all published OEIS terms). CAVEAT: a single n is empirical evidence for the conjecture, NOT a proven improvement of the limsup bound — the proven bound (2) moves only via an infinitely-often argument.
entries.witness_verifier: Sieve primes through prime(2n); compute min over 0<i<n of prime(n-i)+prime(n+i) by direct scan (O(n) additions per index; numpy does n<=20000 in seconds). Dependency-free.
entries.notes: Empirical record table computed 2026-07-18 (validated against OEIS A389677 data for n=2..65): value 1 at n=2; 2 at 4; 4 at 21; 10 at 30; 12 at 189; 18 at 217; 24 at 985; 26 at 1847; 32 at 4612; 38 at 9834; 58 at 14357 — steadily growing, consistent with limsup = infinity. OEIS b-files for A389676/A389677 are synthesized (65 terms only), so a b-file extension is a free contribution. Terence Tao flags the problem 'difficult' on erdosproblems.com. The open-easy grade applies to the empirical record hunt; the proven limsup lower bound (=2 since 1979) is a hard analytic target. Companion sequence A389676 = f(n) itself.

entries.quantity: Falsifiable Erdos-Graham inequality: lcm(1..p_{k+1}-1) < p_k * lcm(1..p_k) for all k >= 1; record = largest X with the inequality verified for all k with p_{k+1} <= X

lower.source: this chunk's computation (2026-07-18): exact identity reduces the inequality to 'product of bases of proper prime powers sharing the gap (p_k, p_{k+1}) is < p_k'; all 80070 proper prime powers <= 1e12 enumerated, only 5 multi-power gaps exist — (8,9),(25,27),(121,125),(2187,2197),(32761,32768) — all safe by wide margin; identity validated against direct bignum lcm on 60 random gaps below 1e4; primality via deterministic 12-base Miller-Rabin (valid < 3.3e24)

entries.witness_object: Upper/closing witness: a counterexample k, i.e. a prime gap (p_k, p_{k+1}) containing proper prime powers whose base product >= p_k — essentially two prime squares in one gap (square-cube pairs only give ~p^{5/6} < p_k). Lower/record witness: a rerun of the reduction certificate to larger X (list of all multi-power gaps <= X with their base products).
entries.witness_verifier: Fully dependency-free: enumerate proper prime powers q^j <= X (only ~pi(sqrt(X)) squares dominate), sort, test for a prime strictly between consecutive powers (Miller-Rabin), and for each cohabiting group compare product of bases against prevprime. Single-power gaps are automatically safe for m > 4 since base q <= sqrt(m) < p_k (Bertrand). Verification to 1e12 took ~1 minute in pure Python.
entries.notes: erdosproblems.com marks this FALSIFIABLE (single-k counterexample possible) and 'almost certainly true but proof beyond reach': ruling out two primes-squared in one prime gap is Legendre-conjecture strength. A counterexample needs a prime-free interval of length ~2*sqrt(p_k) — astronomically beyond all known gaps, so the falsification lane is dead; the honest record is the verified range. Cheap extension route to 4e18: all prime gaps below 4e18 are < 1476 (Oliveira e Silva-Herzog-Pardi maximal-gap verification, 2014), which kills double-square cohabitation for q > 737, leaving only a small square-cube/higher coincidence enumeration — an afternoon job for the rungs fleet. No published verified range was found in OEIS or on the problem page; this 1e12 record is ours.

checked: own verified computation to 1e12 (script + identity sanity check, this session)
Oliveira e Silva et al. 2014 maximal prime gap bound (background for the 4e18 extension route, not used in the 1e12 claim)

entries.quantity: Existence of C > 0 with sum of p(n)/n over n in [x, x + C*x^(1/2)*(log x)^2] bounded below by an absolute constant for all large x (p(n) = least prime factor)
entries.witness_object: None: the statement quantifies over all large x with an existential constant C — no finite computation certifies either direction.
entries.witness_verifier: No cheap witness. Numerical exploration (computing the short-interval sums for sampled x, C) can only suggest a plausible C, never certify.
entries.notes: Background fact stated on the problem page: sum over composite n < x of p(n)/n is asymptotically c*x^(1/2)/(log x)^2, so the conjectured interval length matches the mean spacing of the sum's mass (dominated by n = 2*prime). The linked OEIS sequence A032742 (largest proper divisor, i.e. n/p(n)) is auxiliary only — no bounds live there. No partial results found on the problem page (no remarks section beyond the statement) or in OEIS. Not a records-lane target.

entries.quantity: Sub-conjecture record for the f(N)=o(N) question: A167485(N) > 0 (every N is an initial-segment divisor sum of some m) for all N except 2 and 5 — verified through N = 20000

lower.source: OEIS A167485 b-file (Robert Israel, Maple, Sep 2024); zeros scanned this session: only N=2,5 in 0..20000. Israel's a(n)/n plot extends to 1e6 but the underlying values are not published as a b-file, so 20000 is the honest verified figure

entries.witness_object: Lower/record witness: recomputation certificate of a(N) (the minimal m per N) to larger N with no new zeros. Closing witness: a new zero — an N with NO m whose initial divisor sums hit N — which would refute the Goldbach-flavored strengthening noted in OEIS (every even integer > 22 = p + q with 5 < p < q < 5p) and blow up f(N)=o(N).
entries.witness_verifier: Per-N positive check: exhibit m and verify the running sums d_1 < d_1+d_2 < ... of m's sorted divisors hit N (trivial). Zero-certification is a finite case analysis over admissible initial divisor chains summing to N (each chain's realizability as the exact bottom of a divisor lattice is a finite CRT-style check). Batch computation: sieve-style dynamic programming as in Israel's Maple code.
entries.notes: The actual Erdos question — is f(N) = o(N), where f(N) = A167485(N+1)-indexed minimal m — is asymptotic (wall). Data points: liminf a(n)/n = 0 is PROVEN (Israel: a(sigma(n)) <= n and sigma(n)/n unbounded, OEIS comment 2024); but the ratio spikes — max a(n)/n = 11.84 at n=17567 within the b-file (computed this session), so o(N) is far from empirically settled. Companion sequences (Kovac, Aug 2025): A387502 = number of debut sums per n (record indices 2,12,36,144,336,1320,1980,5040,8400,25200,75600 per OEIS comment; average ~0.83 over first 1e5), A387503 = its partial sums; both b-files to 10000. The |D_n \ union D_m| part of #468 is served by A387502 but has no bound-shaped open gap recorded anywhere we found.

checked: OEIS A167485 (JSON + b-file to 20000, zero-scan run this session)
Guy UPINT B2 attribution via OEIS references

entries.quantity: Convergence of sum of 1/n over primitive pseudoperfect numbers (A006036); companion question for A119425 primitives
entries.witness_object: None: convergence of an infinite series over a set with no known parametrization cannot be certified by any finite computation; partial sums to any bound are consistent with both answers.
entries.witness_verifier: No cheap witness. Membership of an individual n in A006036 is cheaply verifiable (subset-sum over proper divisors + primitivity check on each proper divisor), and the b-file (10000 terms, Donovan Johnson) supports partial-sum estimates, but these move nothing.
entries.notes: Origin: Benkoski-Erdos 1974 (Math. Comp. 28, 617-623) and Erdos 1970; also Guy B2. Odd terms exist (945 is the first odd primitive pseudoperfect, in OEIS data; odd terms coincide with odd primitive abundant A006038 as far as computed, Kehowski 2005 comment). FRESHNESS FLAG: erdosproblems.com/468-neighbor page for 469 shows '1 claimed proof for this problem' in its comment metadata (page last edited 2025-10-19, status still OPEN as of 2026-07-18) — the claim is unvetted; the integration lane should re-check the page before treating this entry as stable.
