Counting-function growth of A006036 vs A119425 partial sums is a possible instrument but certifies nothing.

entries.quantity: Existence of an odd weird number (sigma(n) >= 2n, n not a sum of any subset of its divisors) — $10 Erdos prize; record = exhaustive search bound

lower.source: Wenjie Fang, distributed search (yoyo@home), OEIS A006037 comment Feb 2014; method and bound published in arXiv:2207.12906 (2022), which also shows no odd weird number with abundance < 1e14 exists below 1e28

entries.witness_object: Closing witness: an explicit odd weird number n — odd, sigma(n) >= 2n, plus a certificate that no subset of divisors sums to n (a subset-sum UNSAT instance over tau(n) divisors; SAT/DRAT- or meet-in-the-middle-certifiable for realistic tau). Record witness: an extension of the exhaustive odd search beyond 1e21 (or the bounded-abundance search beyond 1e28).
entries.witness_verifier: For a claimed odd weird n: factor n, enumerate divisors, check sigma >= 2n, then certify subset-sum infeasibility (DP over sums is infeasible at n ~ 1e21, but branch-and-bound/ILP/SAT with the divisor structure is standard and produces a checkable certificate). Constraint filter: any odd weird number has >= 6 distinct prime factors (Liddy-Riedl 2018, cited on the problem page).
entries.notes: Second half of #470 — are there infinitely many primitive weird numbers (A002975, b-file 1161 terms)? — is proven CONDITIONALLY by Melfi 2015 (J. Number Theory 147, 508-514) under a prime-gap hypothesis p_{n+1}-p_n < 0.1*sqrt(p_n); unconditional infinitude open; Amato-Hasler-Melfi-Parton 2019 construct primitive weirds with many prime factors. Benkoski-Erdos 1974 proved weird numbers have positive density; if no odd weird exists, every weird number has abundancy < 4 (page remark, cross-ref problem 825). Feasibility graded hard, not none: a multi-year BOINC project exhausted 1e21 and the frontier heuristics (Fang: near-perfect abundance boundary) suggest any odd weird is enormous. Freshness re-checked by web search 2026-07-18: 1e21/1e28 still the cited records.

checked: web search for post-2022 odd-weird search extensions (none found)

entries.quantity: Survival length of the Ulam prime chain seeded (3,5): q_{n+1} = smallest prime of the form q_n + q_i - 1; verified alive through 10000 terms (a(10000) = 190669)

lower.source: OEIS A389713 b-file (Michael S. Branicky, Jan 2026), extent confirmed this session

entries.witness_object: An extended term list: each new term certifies the chain survives one more step. (A chain DEATH — a step where q_n + q_i - 1 is composite for every i <= n — would also be finitely certifiable and would kill this particular seed, though not the master question, which quantifies over all finite starting sequences.)
entries.witness_verifier: Per step: compute the n candidates q_n + q_i - 1, Miller-Rabin each, take the smallest prime and check it matches; minimality is certified by primality-testing all smaller candidates. Dependency-free; thousands of steps per minute at this size.
entries.notes: Problem due to Ulam (via Erdos-Graham 1980): does SOME finite prime seed extend to an infinite chain? The (3,5) chain (which begins 3,5,7,11,13,...) is the canonical candidate and 'possibly infinite' per the problem page; OEIS comment confirms infinitude unknown. Heuristically the chain has ~n chances per step each prime with probability ~1/log(q_n), so death probability decays rapidly — expect verification to extend essentially forever without deciding anything; the interesting machine event would be discovering a seed whose chain dies (none recorded anywhere we found) or structure enabling a proof. b-file extension beyond 10000 is a free OEIS contribution but low scientific value; grade the lane accordingly despite the open-easy verifier.

checked: OEIS A389713 (JSON + b-file to 10000, tail value confirmed 190669)

entries.quantity: Socialist-prime sub-question of #478: does any prime p>5 have 2!,3!,...,(p-1)! all distinct mod p (equivalently A210184(n)=prime(n)-2)? The headline conjecture |A_p| ~ (1-1/e)p is asymptotic-only.

lower.source: Andrejić–Bostan–Tatarević, arXiv:1904.09196 (extends Trudgian 2013 <10^9 and Andrejić–Tatarević arXiv:1603.04086 <10^11); only p=5 known

entries.witness_object: a prime p > 2^40 with all of 2!..(p-1)! distinct mod p (a 'socialist prime'); alternatively a reproducible sieve receipt extending the verified range
entries.witness_verifier: incremental k! mod p with a seen-residue bitset; early exit at first collision (expected O(sqrt(p)) per non-socialist prime by birthday heuristic); full confirmation O(p) mulmods; dependency-free. Rokowska–Schinzel 1960 filters (p ≡ 5 mod 8, Legendre conditions) prune candidates cheaply.
entries.notes: CAUTION: Abramov arXiv:2604.26429 (submitted 2026-04-29, 6 revisions through 2026-07-16, unrefereed) CLAIMS an elementary proof that no socialist prime > 5 exists — if it holds, this witness lane is dead; erdosproblems.com/478 (edited 2026-04-12) still lists the problem open with 0 claimed proofs. Headline: best lower |A_p| >= (sqrt(2)-o(1))sqrt(p) (Grebennikov–Sagdeev–Semchankau–Vasilevskii, GSSV24); upper p-2 (Wilson) — asymptotic, no finite witness moves it. Heuristically socialist primes should be essentially nonexistent (injectivity of a pseudo-random map has probability ~e^{-cp}).

entries.quantity: A036236(1119) — least n with 2^n ≡ 1119 (mod n); first of 93 unknown entries among k <= 10^4 (Graham's conjecture: for every k ≠ 1 there are infinitely many such n)

lower.source: Nick Hobson search table (OEIS A036236 a-file a036236.log.txt, dated Jan 29 2024, supersedes Havermann): all 93 unknown entries for k <= 10000 exceed 10^13

entries.witness_object: any integer n (necessarily > 10^13) with 2^n mod n = 1119; identical shape for each of the other 92 open residues k <= 10^4 (next few: 1137, 1179, 1299, 1329, 1455, 1459, ...)
entries.witness_verifier: one modular exponentiation: square-and-multiply 2^n mod n, O(log n) mulmods; microseconds even for n ~ 10^18; fully dependency-free
entries.notes: Known terms can be astronomically larger than k (a(3)=4700063497). Structured CRT/multiplicative-order constructions (Crump/Alekseyev style) can leapfrog the sieve — the 93 residues survived structured searches to 10^13, so not open-easy. A witness gives only an upper bound on the least n; exactness requires closing the exhaustive scan below it (same compute class as the existing 10^13 sieve if the witness lands near it, unbounded in principle — hence exact=unknown). k=1 provably impossible (Alekseyev proof in OEIS). Graham conjecture proved for k=2^i, i>=1, and k=-1 (per erdosproblems page; Tang note). Wilson b-file terms 0..1026 complete. Stub lists six companion OEIS ids (A015919 etc.) = solution sets for individual k; A036236 chosen as the frontier carrier.

checked: OEIS a036236.log.txt (Hobson, Jan 2024) — parsed all 93 unknown entries

entries.quantity: A030126(6) — 6-color Schur number in the version-1 convention (least N such that every 6-coloring of {1..N} has a monochromatic x+y=z, x=y allowed); equals S(6)+1 in the largest-N convention

lower.source: Fredricksen–Sweet 2000 sum-free 6-partition of {1..536} (S(6) >= 536); restated a(6) >= 537 by Ahmed–Boza–Revuelta–Sanz 2023 Table 1 (OEIS comment, Aug 2023)

upper.source: f(6) <= R(3;6)-1 with R(3;6) <= 6(R(3;5)-1)+2 <= 1838 chained from R(3,3,3,3) <= 62 (Fettes–Kramer–Radziszowski 2004); coincides with the (e-1/6)k! bound (Xu–Xie–Chen 2002); S(6) <= 1836 appears in current literature

entries.witness_object: a partition of {1..N}, N >= 537, into 6 sum-free classes (no x+y=z with x,y,z in one class, x=y allowed) — gives a(6) >= N+1
entries.witness_verifier: for each class C and each x <= y in C test whether x+y is in C; O(N^2) integer ops; dependency-free
entries.notes: CONVENTION WARNING: A030126 is version 1 = S(k)+1 — do not mix with the largest-N convention (cf. the #552 wheel-convention retraction lesson). S(6) >= 536 unmoved since 2000 despite SAT-era template methods; recent movement is at k>=7: S(7) >= 1740 (arXiv:2107.03560, 2021 — makes the OEIS comment a(7) >= 1681 stale, now a(7) >= 1741) and shifted S-templates with recurrence S(k+2) >= 10S(k)+2 improving S(8), S(13) (arXiv:2607.15034, Jul 2026). Exact closure of a(6) is far beyond current SAT: S(5)=160 closure took Heule's 2-petabyte DRAT (2017). Headline #483 (is f(k) < c^k?) is asymptotic; large-k bounds 380^{k/5}-O(1) <= f(k) <= (e-1/6)k! (Ageron et al. arXiv:2112.03175; Xu–Xie–Chen 2002).

checked: Fettes–Kramer–Radziszowski 2004 via seeded #183 gap entry + DS1

entries.quantity: Turán density pi(K_4^3) = lim ex_3(n,K_4^3)/C(n,3) — Turán's (3,4)-problem, $500 Erdős prize; finite carrier ex_3(n,K_4^3) vs the conjectured-exact construction value A140462(n)

lower.value: 5/9 ≈ 0.55556 (density; finite form A140462(n), exact closed formula)
lower.source: Turán construction (1961 research problem); many non-isomorphic matching families (Brown, Kostochka, Fon-der-Flaass, Frohmader)

upper.source: Razborov 2010 flag-algebra SDP (erdosproblems.com prints 0.5611666 — one-digit rendering discrepancy; 0.561666 per our 2026-07 deep audit and the standard literature quotation)

entries.witness_object: a K_4^3-free 3-uniform hypergraph on n vertices with MORE than A140462(n) triples at any n — would disprove Turán's 1961 conjecture and raise the density lower bound
entries.witness_verifier: scan all C(n,4) 4-subsets and require at least one of the 4 triples absent; O(n^4), exact, dependency-free
entries.notes: A140462 is a closed-form conjectured construction value (b-file to n=10000), NOT computed extremal data — there is nothing to 'extend'. Frohmader's Omega(6^{n/3}) non-isomorphic conjectured-extremal families make DRAT-UNSAT confirmation blow up past tiny n; the live upper-bound frontier is SDP/flag-algebra machinery, not witness search. Deep audit (2026-07-13) found no movement 2023–2026 beyond l2-norm variants (Balogh et al.). Hub status 'wall'; witness lane exists formally (cheap verifier) but success = disproving a 65-year conjecture.

checked: atlas/problems.json#500 deep audit (2026-07-13, incl. Razborov 2010, Frohmader arXiv:0806.4208)

entries.quantity: A175769(9) — maximum isosceles set in R^9 (every 3 points form an isosceles triangle); first unknown dimension of the Erdős–Kelly problem

lower.source: C(d+1,2)=45-point two-distance construction {e_i+e_j} observed by Alweiss, plus one additional point (Weisenberg) — per erdosproblems.com #503 remarks/comments

upper.source: Blokhuis general upper bound C(d+2,2) = C(11,2)

entries.witness_object: explicit coordinates (rational or algebraic) of >= 47 points in R^9 with every triple isosceles
entries.witness_verifier: compute all pairwise squared distances in exact arithmetic; for each of the C(m,3) triples check that at least 2 of the 3 squared distances coincide; O(m^3); dependency-free
entries.notes: a(1..8) = 3,6,8,11,17,28,30,45: d=2 Erdős–Kelly 1947 (new proof Kovács 2024 per site), d=3 Croft 1962, d=4,5 Kido, d=6,7,8 Ionin (EJC 16 (2009) R141). Blokhuis bound sharp at d=1,2,6,8 (a(8)=45=C(10,2)) but a(7)=30<36 shows it is not always tight — so a(9) in [46,55] is genuinely open at both ends. Natural candidate witnesses: large two-distance sets (any two-distance set is isosceles by pigeonhole) from association schemes / strongly regular graphs; that classification field (Musin, Nozaki, Lisoněk) is expert-worked, hence 'hard'. Exact determination is a continuous-space classification problem (Ionin-style), not bounded exhaustive search.

checked: Ionin, Electronic J. Combin. 16 (2009) R141 (via OEIS/search)

entries.quantity: C_2 — connective constant of the square lattice Z^2 (self-avoiding walk growth constant, A387897): rigorous bracket vs 12-digit numerical estimate; #528 headline asks to determine C_k for all k

lower.source: Jensen 2004, bridge enumeration (rigorous via bridge supermultiplicativity); quoted as best rigorous lower bound in Bauerschmidt–Duminil-Copin–Goodman–Slade lecture notes (arXiv:1206.2092)

upper.source: Couronné, arXiv:2211.16146 — modified Pönitz–Tittmann finite-memory automaton with loops to length 26 (improves Pönitz–Tittmann 2000: 2.679193)

entries.witness_object: lower: a longer bridge-enumeration certificate (any finite n gives rigorous C_2 >= b_n^{1/n}); upper: a finite-memory automaton plus a spectral certificate vector v>0 with Mv <= lambda*v proving C_2 <= lambda
entries.witness_verifier: upper side: given automaton + v, verify Mv <= lambda*v by sparse mat-vec in rational/interval arithmetic — cheap if v is shipped, though the automaton-dominates-SAW argument itself is paper-proof; lower side: re-running the bridge count (transfer-matrix scale, counts ~10^30) — expensive, not dependency-free in practice
entries.notes: Numerical value 2.6381585303279... (Jacobsen–Scullard–Guttmann 2016) sits far inside the rigorous bracket — the bracket IS the record. A156816 (positive root of 13x^4-7x^2-581 = 2.63815853034...) is a folklore quartic guess with no known derivation, DOUBTED: disagrees with JSG16 numerics around the 11th decimal (Bauerschmidt et al. 2012 note, in OEIS) — hence exact=wall (no closed form expected). The upper-bound lane looks genuinely movable: Couronné 2022 was a single-author modest-compute improvement (2.679->2.662); longer loops = more compute, same method. General k: trivially k <= C_k <= 2k-1; Kesten 1963: C_k = 2k-1-1/(2k)+O(1/k^2). Honeycomb lattice (not Z^k) solved exactly by Duminil-Copin–Smirnov.

entries.quantity: l(N) — largest m such that EVERY N-element set of reals contains a Sidon subset of size m; asked: is l(N) ~ N^{1/2}?
entries.witness_object: none tracked — for fixed N an upper-side witness (an N-set whose largest Sidon subset is small) would be cheaply checkable by exact max-Sidon ILP, but no literature table or record of l(N) values exists to improve against; the question is the asymptotic order
entries.witness_verifier: n/a — asymptotic-order question; any single N gives an instance, not a movable record. (Verifying a given N-set's max Sidon subset is a small exact ILP, so a finite l(N) table COULD be seeded as a new lane if ever wanted.)
entries.notes: Known: N^{1/2} << l(N) <= (1+o(1))N^{1/2} — lower by Komlós–Sulyok–Szemerédi 1975 (improving the N^{1/3} bound noted by Erdős; problem originally Riddell 1969), upper from the case A={1..N}. That extremal case is exactly A143824 (max Sidon subset of {1..n}), which is why the OEIS relation is marked 'possible' on the site — A143824 is the upper envelope, NOT l(N). Alon–Erdős 1985 stronger conjecture: A decomposes into (1+o(1))sqrt(N) Sidon sets. Page (edited 2026-04-08) cites nothing newer than KSS75 on the main bound; the open content is the constant/order, out of witness reach.

entries.quantity: A000791(10) = R(3,10) — first open cell of the triangle Ramsey row; the machine shadow of #544's asymptotic question R(3,k+1)-R(3,k) -> infinity

lower.source: Exoo explicit 39-vertex triangle-free graph with independence number 9 (per Angeltveit arXiv:2401.00392 and Radziszowski DS1; commonly dated 1989)

upper.source: Angeltveit, arXiv:2401.00392 (ruled out 42 by massive computer search); published EJC 32(4) #P4.30, Nov 2025

entries.witness_object: a triangle-free graph on 40 vertices with no independent set of size 10 (gives R(3,10) >= 41, hence = 41 with the known upper bound)
entries.witness_verifier: triangle scan O(n^3) + independence check alpha <= 9 by branch-and-bound (naive C(40,10) ≈ 8.5e8 subsets; seconds with pruning); dependency-free
entries.notes: #544's headline (difference -> infinity; also = o(k)?) is asymptotic and not witness-movable; the R(3,k) table is the finite record feed. R(3,k) ≍ k^2/log k (Kim 1995 + Shearer). erdosproblems page notes a bound from [1014] implying R(3,k+1)-R(3,k) << k^{-c}R(3,k). If no 40-vertex witness exists then R(3,10)=40, and closure needs Angeltveit-class specialized enumeration (not practically DRAT-shaped) — hence exact=unknown, not cell. Witness side has defeated Exoo/McKay/Goedgebeur-grade tooling for ~35 years. Bounds cross-checked OEIS comment (Apr 2024), arXiv v2 (Apr 2024), EJC publication (Nov 2025); no 2026 resolution found in search.

entries.quantity: Erdős–Graham quasi-completeness question: among graphs G with m edges (no isolated vertices), is R(G) always maximized by the quasi-complete graph H (K_n plus a vertex joined to t vertices, m = C(n,2)+t)? No bounded numeric quantity is tracked.
entries.witness_object: a counterexample for the surviving (large-m / eventual) form would require TWO exact Ramsey numbers R(G) and R(H), each a research-scale computation — not a cheap witness. Small-m failures are already known informally (fails for 2 <= m <= 5 and 7 <= m <= 9, per erdosproblems comments), so even the finite side has no defended record to move.
entries.witness_verifier: none cheap: certifying R(G)=r needs a G-avoiding 2-coloring at r-1 (cheap) PLUS a nonexistence certificate over all colorings at r (expensive, per candidate graph)
entries.notes: The conjecture as literally stated FAILS for small m (2<=m<=5 and 7<=m<=9 — noted by LouisD in erdosproblems comments); open content is the m -> infinity statement. The weaker form R(G) <= 2^{O(sqrt(m))} was proved by Sudakov 2011 (that is #546). A059442 (general Ramsey table) is marked 'possible' related on the site; its own open cells (R(4,6), R(5,5), ...) belong to hub problems 77/78/87, not here — do not double-count them under #545.

entries.quantity: A006672(39) = R(C_4, K_{1,39}) — smallest open cell of the C4-vs-star Ramsey table (the $100 headline asks the asymptotic question: infinitely many n with R(C4,S_n) <= n+sqrt(n)-c?)

lower.source: Wu–Sun–(Zhang–)Radziszowski 2015 (DS1 rev.18 Table IVa 'Cons.5'); independently re-derived by our certified 45-vertex witness (certificates/erdos-552-f39/)

upper.source: Parsons 1975 general bound n+ceil(sqrt(n))+1 = 47 at n=39; DS1 rev.18 lists the cell as open 46-47 (Dybizbański–Dzido attribution)

entries.witness_object: a C4-free graph on 46 vertices with minimum degree >= 7 (gives R(C4,K1,39) >= 47, hence = 47 with Parsons' upper bound); complementary decider: DRAT-UNSAT at (46 vertices, min-deg 7) gives = 46
entries.witness_verifier: codegree check (every vertex pair has <= 1 common neighbor = C4-free) + min-degree >= 7 scan; O(n^3); shipped dependency-free verifier at certificates/erdos-552/verify.py
entries.notes: LESSON BANK (in-repo): our 2026-07-16 'a(39)=46' equality claim was RETRACTED pre-submission — Boza arXiv:2409.12770's '/46' cell is a wheel-convention off-by-one vs DS1 rev.18's open '46-47'; always reconcile against DS1, never a single survey table. OEIS b-file badly lags the literature (ends a(11)=16; exact values known for ALL n <= 38 per Boza's table). Adjacent open cells: a(42) (49 vtx, min-deg 7) and a(44) (51 vtx, min-deg 7); polarity-deletion families ER_7/ER_8 certified UNSAT in-repo for those, so a witness needs a different construction. Our 4 direct SAT deciders on (46,7)/(49,7)/(51,7) were killed after 5.5h with no result (2026-07-17) — naive edge-SAT encodings insufficient; needs symmetry-broken/structured encodings or annealed constructions. Certified in-repo: a(12..16)=17..21 and the a(17)=22 re-derivation (Parsons 1975: R(C4,K1,q^2+1)=q^2+q+2 at q=4; counting vs ex(22;C4)=52 closes the upper side).

checked: atlas/problems.json#552 deep record incl. DS1 rev.18 reconciliation (2026-07-17)

entries.quantity: R_k(C_{2n}) — multicolour Ramsey number of even cycles as a function of the number of colours k (linked OEIS A389313 is the 2-colour diagonal r(C_n,C_n), which is fully solved)
entries.witness_object: None for the problem as posed: it asks to determine R_k(C_{2n}) asymptotically in k, which no single colouring settles. Individual lower-bound colourings only move specific (k,n) cells in the DS1 multicolour table, which is not the quantity linked here.
entries.witness_verifier: No cheap witness exists for the asymptotic determination. (A colouring certificate for a specific R_k(C_{2m}) cell would be cheaply verifiable — check no monochromatic C_{2m} — but the linked sequence A389313 has a closed formula with no uncomputed cell: a(n)=2n-1 for n odd, 3n/2-1 for n even, a(3)=a(4)=6, b-file to n=1000.)
entries.notes: Erdős–Graham problem: determine R_k(C_{2n}). Known bounds (per problem page, paraphrased): k^{1+1/2n} << R_k(C_{2n}) << k^{1+1/(n-1)} (Erdős 1981); Chung–Graham 1975: R_k(C_4) > k^2-k+1 when k-1 is a prime power, and R_k(C_4) <= k^2+k+1 for all k. The hub's linked OEIS sequence A389313 (Faudree–Schelp 1974, Rosta 1973) is CLOSED — no record lane there. Adjacent machine-shaped record surface lives in Radziszowski DS1's multicolour cycle cells (not verified here). Problem page marks this as not resolvable by finite computation.

checked: OEIS A389313 (JSON, incl. formula + Howroyd b-file note)

entries.quantity: A006856(54) — maximum number of edges in a 54-vertex graph of girth >= 5 (first uncomputed cell of ex(n;{C3,C4}))

lower.source: Brendan McKay, OEIS A006856 comment (Jan 07 2023) and McKay's 'Extremal Graphs and Turan Numbers' data page (accessed 2026-07-18)

upper.source: DERIVED (this survey, elementary): deleting a min-degree vertex gives e <= a(53) + floor(2e/54) with a(53)=181 exact (McKay 2023), forcing e <= 187. Not a literature bound; sharper unpublished search bounds may exist.

entries.witness_object: A 54-vertex graph with >= 186 edges and girth >= 5, given as an edge list (g6 string or adjacency list)
entries.witness_verifier: Check |V|=54 and edge count; verify girth >= 5 by confirming no two adjacent vertices share a neighbour (no C3) and no two vertices have two common neighbours (no C4) — O(n^3) integer arithmetic, dependency-free.
entries.notes: Headline problem (Erdős–Simonovits) is asymptotic — is ex(n;{C3,C4}) ~ (n/2)^{3/2}? — and not machine-closable; the linked sequence is the record lane. Exact through n=53: a(50)=175 (unique, Hoffman–Singleton), a(51)=176, a(52)=178, a(53)=181 with >=2688 extremal graphs (McKay). Best known lower bounds a(54..64) >= 185,189,193,197,202,207,212,216,220,224,230 (McKay page, accessed 2026-07-18). Frontier held since Jan 2023 by specialized exhaustive tooling (McKay; SAT approaches by Codish–Miller–Prosser–Stuckey reached order ~32), so a new 186-edge graph is a genuine research result — graded hard. Exact a(54) plausibly falls to the same expert exhaustive methods (cell), but each increment has taken years.

entries.quantity: A006065(19) — maximum number of lines containing exactly 4 points ('4-tree orchard rows') achievable with 19 points in the plane (first cell with a bounds gap in the linked record table)

lower.source: OEIS A006065 datum a(19)=20; Erich Friedman's tree-planting bounds table lists 20-28 (accessed 2026-07-18)

upper.source: Erich Friedman's maintained tree-planting bounds table (accessed 2026-07-18)

entries.witness_object: 19 points with exact rational or algebraic coordinates (projective/points-at-infinity form allowed) determining >= 21 lines that each contain exactly 4 of the points
entries.witness_verifier: Exact-arithmetic collinearity: for each claimed line verify its 4 points are collinear via 3x3 determinants and that no 5th point lies on it; count distinct lines. Pure integer/algebraic-number arithmetic, dependency-free.
entries.notes: Headline $100 problem is asymptotic — is f_k(n)=o(n^2) for k>=4 (no k+1 points collinear)? — explicitly not resolvable by finite computation. Known (paraphrased from problem page): Kárteszi 1963 f_k(n) >>_k n log n; Grünbaum 1976 f_k(n) >>_k n^{1+1/(k-2)}; Solymosi–Stojaković 2013 (arXiv:1107.0327) f_k(n) >>_k n^{2-O_k(1/sqrt(log n))} — so the answer is delicately between n^{2-o(1)} and o(n^2). The record lane is the linked orchard tables: A006065 exact through n=18 (a(18)=18; Zhao Hui Du verified a(13)-a(16) by code); Friedman gaps: n=19: 20-28, n=20: 23-30, n=21: 24-31, n=22: 26-38. Companion A008997 (exactly-5-point lines): first gap a(15) in [6,9], Berloquin a(21) >= 12. Lower-bound records in this family moved in 2005 (Lin, a(20)>=23) and 2018-2020 (Pegg, n=22 constructions via elliptic-curve/projective tricks) — hence plausible, not open-easy. Exact values are walled by continuous realizability (order-type enumeration stops near n=11). Hub status for this problem is 'wall' (exact side); witness lane is live.

entries.quantity: A087280(6) — the least n > 24 with max_{m<n}(m+tau(m)) <= n+2 (existence open; Erdős £25 prize, listed as ~$44 in 1992 exchange terms)

lower.source: Patrik Idén 2026, 'Computational Verification of Erdős Problem 647 up to 10^12' (Zenodo doi:10.5281/zenodo.21134668, v3); OEIS A087280 comment (Jun 13 2026): no more terms <= 10^12

entries.witness_object: An integer n > 24 such that m + tau(m) <= n + 2 for every m < n (equivalently max_{m<n}(m+tau(m)) <= n+2); this single number closes the erdosproblems VERIFIABLE question and claims the prize
entries.witness_verifier: Segmented divisor sieve over m < n accumulating max(m+tau(m)); confirm the running max never exceeds n+2. O(n log n) time, streaming memory, pure integer arithmetic — heavy at n ~ 10^12+ but fully dependency-free.
entries.notes: Erdős–Selfridge; known solutions n = 5, 8, 10, 12, 24 (A087280). The n+2 threshold is best possible. Erdős called further terms 'extremely doubtful' and conjectured max_{m<n}(tau(m)+m-n) -> infinity. Idén 2026 (Zenodo, CC-BY): search extended 10^10 -> 10^12 with none found; minimum observed gap R(n)-n = 224 near 10^11; empirical gap-growth fit ~0.060(log m)^2.22; independently re-checked a Hughes frontier certificate claimed to cover n <= 6.157x10^17 within the 41 open residue classes (28/30 checks passed — treat the 6.16e17 frontier as claimed, not clean-verified), and confirmed an Alexeev near-witness at n = 604,517,614,941,240. If a term exists, bounded search finds and cheaply certifies it; nonexistence is not machine-provable. Companion sequence A062249 (n + d(n)).

checked: OEIS A087280 (JSON, incl. Jun 2026 Idén comment)

entries.quantity: q(n,k) — least prime not dividing (n+1)(n+2)...(n+k) (OEIS A391668, table read by antidiagonals); conjecture: q(n,k) < (1+o(1)) log n for fixed k >= 2 and n large
entries.witness_object: None: the conjecture is asymptotic (eventual behaviour in n for fixed k). Any individual table cell T(n,k) is computable at will and no finite set of cells confirms or refutes the o(1) claim.
entries.witness_verifier: No cheap witness exists for the asymptotic statement. (Individual cells verify trivially by trial division of n+1..n+k by successive primes, but they carry no decisive content.)
entries.notes: Erdős–Pomerance problem. Easy bound q(n,k) < (1+o(1)) k log n (also on the OEIS formula lines); the problem asks whether the k factor can be removed, possibly even up to k = o(log n) (a Tao heuristic in the page comments supports this). Companion conjecture: q(n, log n) >= (2+eps) log n infinitely often for some eps > 0. Useful identities from A391668: T(n,k) = A007918(n+k+1) (next prime) for k >= n. Refs: Burr–Erdős–Graham–Li, Acta Arith. 1996; Erdős, Math. Japon. 1997. Problem page: not resolvable by finite computation. See also problem 457.

entries.quantity: A385657(22) — number of nonisomorphic maximally dense unit-distance graphs on 22 vertices (first uncomputed cell of the count sequence linked to this problem)
entries.witness_object: No cheap standalone witness: a lower bound on the count requires exhibiting configurations AND certifying they attain the maximum unit-distance count A186705(22), which is itself unknown (only bounded above by Alexeev–Mixon–Parshall's 2025 revision).
entries.witness_verifier: None dependency-free: checking 'achieves E unit distances' is trivial (exact coordinate arithmetic), but 'E is maximal for n=22' needs an exhaustive certification of A186705(22) in the style of Alexeev–Mixon–Parshall (combinatorial enumeration + realizability), which is expert tooling, not a cheap check.
entries.notes: Erdős 668 asks whether the number of incongruent n-point sets maximizing unit distances tends to infinity, and whether it exceeds 1 for all n > 3 (it equals 1 at n=4). Known counts n=1..21: 1,1,1,1,1,4,1,3,1,1,2,1,1,2,1,1,7,16,3,1,5 (A385657, Weisstein 2025) — caveat from the problem page: computations distinguish graph-isomorphism classes, not congruence classes. Companion A186705 (problem 90's max unit distances) is exact to a(21)=57 with AMP upper bounds for a(22)-a(30) (e.g. best upper for a(27) = 90 per OEIS comment, May 2026). Extending both sequences to n=22 is the same one-step extension of the AMP exhaustive method (arXiv:2412.11914) — machine-shaped but expert-scale, hence exact_feasibility=cell with no cheap-witness lane. Context only: the asymptotic unit-distance conjecture saw major 2026 activity (arXiv:2605.20695, arXiv:2605.20579) — that concerns problem 90, not this counting question. The infinitude question itself is not finite-computation-resolvable.

entries.quantity: A003035(15) — maximum number of lines containing exactly 3 of 15 points in the plane (classical orchard problem; first uncomputed cell)

lower.source: OEIS A003035: Sloane comment (Feb 11 2013) 'a(15) is 31 or 32'; Pegg construction illustrations on the entry give explicit 31-line 15-point configurations

upper.source: OEIS A003035: Sloane comment (Feb 11 2013); cell open since Burr–Grünbaum–Sloane, Geometriae Dedicata 1974

entries.witness_object: 15 points with exact algebraic/projective coordinates determining 32 lines that each contain exactly 3 of the points
entries.witness_verifier: Exact-arithmetic collinearity via 3x3 determinants; count lines with exactly 3 incident points and confirm 32. Dependency-free.
entries.notes: Problem 669 as posed asks to estimate f_k(n) (exactly k) and F_k(n) (at least k) and determine lim F_k(n)/n^2, lim f_k(n)/n^2 — asymptotic, not machine-closable; the linked orchard cell is the record lane. Burr–Grünbaum–Sloane 1974: f_3(n) = n^2/6 - O(n); trivially lim F_k(n)/n^2 <= 1/(k(k-1)). Green–Tao 2013 (arXiv:1208.4714): a(n) = floor(n(n-3)/6)+1 for all sufficiently large n, which gives 31 at n=15 — so the 32-line witness likely does NOT exist, and closing the cell probably needs a nonexistence proof over R (abstract 3-configuration enumeration is bounded, but realizability has defeated tools for ~52 years) — hence witness=hard, exact=wall. Also known: a(16)=37, a(17) in {40,41,42}. Discrepancy note: Friedman's maintained table shows ranges 22-24 / 26-27 at n=13,14 where OEIS lists exact 22 / 26; OEIS (Sloane 2013) treated as authoritative here. Cross-links: 4- and 5-point-line versions A006065/A008997 are recorded under problem 588; problem 101 is the k=4 special case.

entries.quantity: Infinitude of A390181 — integers not representable as a*p^2 + b with p prime, a >= 1, 0 <= b < p (equivalently: is every sufficiently large integer so representable? Erdős thought not)
entries.witness_object: None: each individual exception is trivially certifiable (n is an exception iff n mod p^2 >= p for every prime p <= sqrt(n)), but no finite list of exceptions settles infinitude, and no finite computation proves the 'every large n' direction.
entries.witness_verifier: Per-exception check is cheap and dependency-free (trial over primes p <= sqrt(n), test n mod p^2 < p), but it moves nothing: the open content is asymptotic.
entries.notes: From the problem page (paraphrased): the sieve of Eratosthenes gives that almost all integers are representable; Brun–Selberg sieve bounds exceptions in [1,x] by << x/(log x)^c. Erdős [1979] called it 'rather unlikely' that all large integers are representable — i.e. he leaned toward the exception set A390181 being infinite (the OEIS comment echoes 'seems likely' infinite). Empirically the exception set is robustly populous: b-file has 100000 terms, the 100000th being 1,211,719 (~8.3% density near 1.2e6). Selfridge–Wagstaff preliminary search suggested infinitude even with the primality condition on p dropped. Sharper variant [Er79d]: minimal c_n with n = a*p^2 + b, 0 <= b < c_n*p; Erdős suggested limsup c_n = infinity and asked if c_n < n^{o(1)}. No gap/record structure to mine.

entries.quantity: Cofiniteness of the property 'there exists k with n+k composite and lpf(n+k) > k^2' — equivalently finiteness of the exceptional set A389680
entries.witness_object: None: a single n with a qualifying k proves nothing about 'all large n', and the conjecturally-finite exceptional set is still far too dense to name a 'largest known exception' frontier (14.4% density at 10^7, with exceptions right up to the search bound).
entries.witness_verifier: Per-n check is cheap and dependency-free (any qualifying k satisfies k^4 < n+k, so scan k <= (n+k)^{1/4} with trial division for lpf), but there is no verified initial segment where the conjecture holds — so no verified_range framing is honest here.
entries.notes: Erdős [Er79d], related to Erdős–Eggleton–Selfridge; possibly true with k^2 replaced by k^d for any d. Fresh verification computation (this survey, 2026-07-18; numpy spf-sieve, cross-checked exactly against all 68 OEIS-displayed terms of A389680): counts of exceptional n <= 10^e for e=2..7 are 91, 746, 5712, 40282, 257940, 1441896 — density shrinking by ~x0.63 per decade to 0.1442 at 10^7; largest exception <= 10^7 is 9,999,973. So the OEIS comment 'density decreases' is confirmed but extinction (finiteness) is far beyond any current computational frontier — the problem page correctly marks it not resolvable by finite computation. No gap/record lane exists; a future rung could be 'first exception-free decade' but nothing of the sort is in sight.

checked: local spf-sieve verification to 10^7 (cross-checked against OEIS terms)

entries.quantity: Growth of P(binom(n,k)) (largest prime factor of binomial coefficients) — conjectured lower envelope min(n-k+1, k^{1+c}) for some c>0
entries.witness_object: None — the conjecture asserts an asymptotic lower bound with an unspecified constant c>0; no finite (n,k) example can confirm or refute it. Anti-witness specimens ((n,k) with unusually small P(binom(n,k))/(k log k)) are computable but move no bound.
entries.witness_verifier: No cheap witness exists: any finite data set is consistent with both truth and failure. Per-pair P(binom(n,k)) is computable via prime-valuation (Kummer/Legendre) extraction if specimen tables are ever wanted.
entries.notes: Best known: Sylvester-Schur gives P(binom(n,k)) > k for k <= n/2; Erdos 1955 [Er55d] proved P(binom(n,k)) >> k log k for k <= n/2 (still the record per erdosproblems.com/683, page last edited 2025-12-31). Conjecture asks k^{1+c}; prime-gap heuristics suggest exp(c*sqrt(k)) (essentially equivalent to hub #961). Listed OEIS ids are column/special cases: A074399 = gpf of n(n+1), A121359 = gpf of C(n+2,3), A006530 = generic gpf. Statement from Apache-licensed Lean stub; site prose paraphrased only.

entries.quantity: Growth order of f(n) = least k such that the k-smooth part u of binom(n,k) exceeds n^2 (A392019)

lower.value: f(n) >= (1/2 - o(1))·log n for infinitely many n
lower.source: Alexeev-Putterman-Sawhney-Sellke-Valiant [APSSV26] as cited in erdosproblems.com/684 remarks (elementary construction; argument credited to an internal OpenAI model)

upper.value: f(n) = O((log n)^2) for all n
upper.source: Alexeev-Putterman-Sawhney-Sellke-Valiant [APSSV26] as cited in erdosproblems.com/684 remarks

entries.witness_object: None for the asymptotic gap. Per-n f(n) is exactly computable (I reproduced A392019(1..25) from the definition), so individual values are table cells, not records; a specimen table of record f(n)/log n values is possible but moves no proven bound.
entries.witness_verifier: n/a for the bounds; a claimed value f(n)=k is checked by extracting prime valuations of binom(n,k) for p <= k (Legendre sums, no factoring) and verifying u(n,k) > n^2 while u(n,k-1) <= n^2.
entries.notes: HOT in 2026 — recheck before any use as best-known. Superseded: Tang-ChatGPT f(n) <= n^{30/43+o(1)} (and 2/3 on RH). Newer preprints (both unrefereed, found in fresh search, not yet on the curated page as of its 2026-04-01 edit): Bae arXiv:2604.23784 claims limsup f(n)/log n = infinity (would strengthen the lower side beyond any C log n); Li arXiv:2606.08216 claims density-one asymptotic f(n) = (2/(1-gamma)+o(1))·log n ≈ 4.7305 log n for a.e. n. Mahler's theorem gives f(n)->infinity ineffectively. A392019 (Beregovsky, 2025-12) has 73 terms; a(n)=0 when no k works (small n).

entries.quantity: A048670(65) = j(p_65#) — Jacobsthal function of the product of the first 65 primes (first uncomputed cell; a(64)=1110)

lower.source: monotonicity in n from a(64)=1110 (adding a prime cannot shrink the extremal run); a(58)-a(64) computed by Bozek, OEIS A048670 b-file

entries.witness_object: A pair (u, L): L consecutive integers u..u+L-1, each divisible by at least one of the first 65 primes (p <= 313); establishes a(65) >= L+1. Same shape as the Gerbicz/Bozek witness tables attached to A048670.
entries.witness_verifier: For each i < L, trial-divide u+i by the 65 primes up to 313 (or gcd with p_65#). O(65·L) word ops, fully dependency-free; a record L ~ 1100+ verifies in microseconds.
entries.notes: $1000 problem. The prize question is the asymptotic gap and is a wall: Y(x) << x^2 (Iwaniec, via linear sieve, Acta Arith. 1971/1978) vs Y(x) >> x·log x·logloglog x/loglog x (Ford-Green-Konyagin-Maynard-Tao, J. AMS 31 (2018)); Maier-Pomerance conjecture x(log x)^{2+o(1)}; fresh search found no post-2018 improvement. Machine lane = the table: exact a(62)-a(64) needed Google-Cloud-scale search (Bozek 2021), so exact a(65) is cell-in-principle but likely months of CPU; the cheap record lane is new covering witnesses lower-bounding a(65) (algorithmics: Ziller-Morack arXiv:1611.03310; Hagedorn Math. Comp. 2009 for h(n), n<50). Identities: A048670(n) = A058989(n)+1 (checked: 1110=1109+1) = 2·A072752(n)+2; A049300 = minimal starting u.

checked: OEIS A048670 full entry + b-file (64 terms, fetched live)
web search for post-2018 Jacobsthal bounds (none found)

entries.quantity: Asymptotic order of the max gap between consecutive integers in [n, n^k] having a divisor in (n, 2n) (k=2 table: A391118); is it (log n)^{O(1)}?
entries.witness_object: None — the open content is whether the max gap is polylog with an unspecified O(1) exponent, so any finite computation is consistent with both answers. Per-n max gap is exactly computable.
entries.witness_verifier: Per-n value check: enumerate multiples of each d in (n,2n) within [n,n^2], merge-sort, scan gaps; naive O(n^2) works to n in the low thousands. I reproduced A391118(3..12) this way.
entries.notes: No nontrivial proven bounds are cited on erdosproblems.com/693 (only a cross-ref to #446); trivial upper: max gap <= ~n+2 since consecutive multiples of a fixed d in (n,2n) are <= 2n apart. A391118 (Beregovsky, created 2026-01) has terms n=3..83, values reaching only 12 — consistent with polylog but non-probative. Table extension is easy compute, not record-shaped; no bound moves.

entries.quantity: g(k) = minimal terminal prime q_k over ascending prime chains q_1<...<q_k with q_{i+1} ≡ 1 (mod q_i); greedy chain A061092 gives the known upper bounds

lower.source: elementary doubling argument (q_{i+1} ≡ 1 mod q_i, prime, > q_i)

upper.source: OEIS A061092 (Murthy) + b-file

entries.witness_object: A length-k chain of primes q_1<...<q_k with q_{i+1} ≡ 1 (mod q_i) and q_k < A061092(k) — a sub-greedy record. The min-terminal-prime sequence g(k) appears NOT to be in OEIS: a computed record table would be submission-worthy.
entries.witness_verifier: k primality checks (deterministic Miller-Rabin below 2^64; Pratt/ECPP certificates beyond) plus k-1 congruence checks q_{i+1} mod q_i == 1. Trivial, dependency-free.
entries.notes: Greedy is pointwise-minimal per step but need not be globally optimal (a larger early prime can enable much smaller later ones) — bounded DFS over primes ≡ 1 mod q below the greedy cap is an hours-scale search for k <= ~12, and exact g(k) for modest k falls to exhaustive search below the greedy bound. CAVEAT (honesty): Erdos's question concerns one infinite nested chain; g(k)-optimal chains need not nest, so records are evidence, not resolution — lim q_k^{1/k} = infinity? and existence of a chain with q_k <= exp(k(log k)^{1+o(1)}) both remain walls. Greedy grows like exp(exp(O(k))) via Linnik; the conjectured least prime ≡ 1 mod p being <= p(log p)^{O(1)} would give exp(k(log k)^{1+o(1)}). Finite prime chains studied by Ford-Konyagin-Luca (Prime chains and Pratt trees, GAFA 2010). I verified greedy steps a(1..9) by direct computation.

checked: local verification of greedy steps a(1..9)

entries.quantity: f(n) = min_{1<k<=n/2} gcd(n, binom(n,k)) for composite n (numerically = A091963(n)); sub-questions: characterize f(n)=n/P(n), infinitude of composite n with f(n)>sqrt(n), and f(n) <<_A n/(log n)^A
entries.witness_object: No record-shaped bound: the sub-questions are a characterization plus infinitude/asymptotic claims. Individual composite n with f(n) > sqrt(n) or with f(n) = n/P(n) are cheap specimens but move no bound.
entries.witness_verifier: Per-n check without big ints: for each prime power of n, get its valuation in binom(n,k) via Kummer carry-counting; f(n) then costs O(n·log n). I verified A091963(n) = f(n) directly (big-int gcds) for 4 <= n <= 43.
entries.notes: Erdos-Szekeres [ErSz78]. Known per erdosproblems.com/700 (paraphrased): f(n) <= n/P(n) for composite n (take k = exact prime-power divisor), hence f(n) <= (1+o(1))·n/log n; equality f(n)=n/P(n) holds for n=pq (two primes) and n=30; f(n) >= p(n) (smallest prime factor), so n=p^2 gives f(n) >= sqrt(n) — the strict-inequality infinitude for general composite n is the open part. Note OEIS A091963 is phrased as smallest gcd of two interior Pascal-row entries; it coincides with min gcd(n, binom(n,k)) on my tested range 4..43 — flag if extending. Specimen mining (composite non-prime-power n with f(n) > sqrt(n)) could feed the characterization question, exploratory only.

checked: web search for recent work (none found)

entries.quantity: Growth order of f(n) = least L such that (n, n+L) contains distinct a_1,...,a_n with k | a_k (A390246)

lower.source: Erdos-Pomerance [ErPo80], per erdosproblems.com/710

upper.source: Erdos-Pomerance [ErPo80], per erdosproblems.com/710

entries.witness_object: None for the asymptotic gap — per-n f(n) is poly-time computable (bipartite matching between indices 1..n and their multiples in the interval), so single values are cells, not records; A390246 already has 10000 terms (a(10000)=20626).
entries.witness_verifier: For a claimed f(n)=L: exhibit a perfect matching in (n, n+L) (verify k | a_k, distinctness) and for L-1 a Hall-violator certificate (a set S of indices whose available multiples number < |S|). Both checks are trivial.
entries.notes: Erdos offered 2000 rupees (site converts to ~$78) for an asymptotic formula. The gap is the (loglog n)^{1/2} factor plus constants (2/sqrt(e) ≈ 1.213 vs 1.7398...). Freshness: 2026 work by van Doorn (arXiv:2601.16972, Integers 2026) resolves the max_m variant (#711 — intervals of length c·n·log n/loglog n with no system of distinct multiples) but does NOT move #710's 1980 bounds; also arXiv:2603.28636 (van Doorn-Li-Tang 2026) is problem #650, not #710. I verified A390246 = f(n) for n=1..15 by direct matching computation. Cheap non-record analysis: fit the 10^4-term b-file against the two candidate rates to guess the truth — evidence only.

entries.quantity: A001009 row-12 diagonal = number of reduced 12x12 Latin squares (= A000315(12)) — first uncomputed cell; order 11 = 5363937773277371298119673540771840
entries.witness_object: None — an exact count admits no cheap certificate; values come from heavy trusted enumeration/algebraic methods (McKay-Wanless style), not from a verifiable witness object.
entries.witness_verifier: None known: verifying a claimed count essentially requires an independent re-enumeration; no succinct certificate format exists for Latin-square counts.
entries.notes: Erdos's actual #725 question is an asymptotic formula for the number of k x n Latin rectangles: Erdos-Kaplansky 1946 proved ~ e^{-binom(k,2)}·(n!)^k for k = o((log n)^{3/2-eps}); Yamamoto 1951 extended to k <= n^{1/3-o(1)}; the full range is open and not machine-witnessable (I did not exhaustively survey post-1951 range extensions — treat the range frontier as unverified). The machine-shaped frontier is the enumeration table: A000315 has 11 terms (order 11 by McKay-Wanless, 2005), order 12 uncomputed for ~20 years (OEIS keywords hard/more checked live 2026-07-18; fresh web search found no order-12 computation). Not a realistic single-box target — recorded as a wall so the scout deprioritizes.

checked: web search: Latin squares order 12 enumeration (none found)

entries.quantity: A343507(10) = smallest m with (m+10)!^2 | (2m)! — first uncomputed cell (a(9) = 2935782889)

lower.source: monotonicity in k ((m+k)!^2 | (2m)! implies (m+k-1)!^2 | (2m)!) from a(9)=2935782889, Mizrahi, OEIS A343507

entries.witness_object: Any m with (m+10)!^2 | (2m)! gives a(10) <= m; a completed scan from 2935782889 up to that m closes the cell exactly. (Same lane one rung down: A389396 lists 1468 k=2 witnesses up to 148003.)
entries.witness_verifier: For each prime p <= 2m check Legendre valuations: sum_j floor(2m/p^j) >= 2·sum_j floor((m+10)/p^j). One candidate at m~10^10 verifies in minutes with a sieve; scanning is fast because most m fail early on a small prime.
entries.notes: The Erdos-Graham-Ruzsa-Straus [EGRS75] question (infinitude for each fixed k >= 2) is open even for k=2 and is NOT settled by finite witnesses — the record lane is extending A343507 (keywords hard/more, 9 terms). Growth ratios of a(k) (5x-45x per step) suggest a(10) ~ 10^10-10^11: a days-scale filtered scan on one box. k=1 infinitude is Balakran 1929 (A002503: n with (n+1)^2 | C(2n,n); even perfect numbers m give m-1 as terms). I verified a(2)=208 by direct scan, the equivalence for k=2 with (n+1)^2(n+2)^2 | C(2n,n) (A389396), and the monotonicity implication. Statement from Apache-licensed Lean stub.

checked: local verification of a(2)=208 and the k=2 equivalence
web search for recent progress (none found beyond #728 resolution, distinct problem)

entries.quantity: Largest gap k with a known pair (n, n+k) such that binomial(2n,n) and binomial(2(n+k),n+k) have identical prime-divisor sets (sub-question of: are there infinitely many such pairs?)

lower.source: triple (10003,10004,10005) cited in erdosproblems.com/730 remarks; re-verified this session by direct Kummer-carry computation (pair (87,88) also re-verified)

entries.witness_object: a pair (n, n+k) with k >= 3 whose central binomial coefficients have identical prime-divisor sets; any new pair also grows the empirical base for the EGRS infinitude conjecture
entries.witness_verifier: Kummer's theorem: p | C(2n,n) iff adding n+n in base p carries; compute both prime sets over primes <= 2(n+k) and compare — pure-Python, seconds per candidate, dependency-free
entries.notes: EGRS 1975 said there is 'no doubt' infinitely many pairs exist — that infinitude question is not machine-resolvable. The recordable sub-question (stated open on the problem page): does a pair of shape (n,n+k) exist for every k>=1? Known: k=1 (43 terms listed in A129515, b-file to 10000 terms, all with m=n+1) and k=2 (via the 10003..10005 triple, confirmed here: all three share an identical 1540-prime set). k>=3 is unclaimed. Search cost per n is low but hit-density is unknown (gap-1 pairs are already thin); needs a smart incremental scan, hence 'plausible' not 'open-easy'.

entries.quantity: Asymptotics of m(n) = least integer not dividing binomial(2n,n) (A006197): find f(n) with m(n) ~ f(n) for almost all n
entries.witness_object: none — the problem asks for an almost-everywhere asymptotic formula; A006197 is already computed to n=10000 (b-file, T. D. Noe) and further table extension does not move the question
entries.witness_verifier: n/a (no finite witness changes the epistemic state; the EGRS claim m = exp((log n)^{1/2+o(1)}) for almost all n is an analytic statement)
entries.notes: EGRS 1975 state it is not hard to show m(n) = exp((log n)^{1/2+o(1)}) for almost all n; the open content is pinning a genuine asymptotic f(n). All values are prime powers (OEIS comment, corrected Eldar 2024). Data-rich but not gap-shaped: no bounded quantity with a cheap witness.

entries.quantity: Leading constant c in the density asymptotic |A ∩ [1,N]|/N = exp(-(c+o(1)) sqrt(log N) loglog N), where A = integers n such that every prime p|n has a divisor d|n, d>1, d ≡ 1 (mod p) (A352287; upper-bounds counts of non-cyclic simple group orders, A001034)
entries.witness_object: none — the question is an asymptotic density constant; finite counts of A352287 (b-file to 10000 terms) cannot certify or move the constant
entries.witness_verifier: n/a (membership in A is trivially checkable per integer, but no finite computation bears on the exp(-c sqrt(log N) loglog N) shape)
entries.notes: FRESHNESS FLAG: Eric Li, 'The Sylow Divisor Condition: a Resolution of Erdős Problem 768', arXiv:2606.24872 (2026, 20pp), claims a full resolution with c = 1/(2 sqrt(log 2)); the paper is linked from OEIS A352287 and a claimed solution is posted in the problem-page comments, but upstream still marks the problem OPEN — librarian should track and close if it holds up. Erdős's own bounds: density >= exp(-c sqrt(log N) loglog N) and <= exp(-(1+o(1)) sqrt(log N loglog N)) (paraphrased from problem page). Companion sequence A001034 (simple group orders) is the motivation.

checked: arXiv:2606.24872 (existence + claimed result verified via web search)

entries.quantity: c(3) — least k0 such that the cube can be decomposed into k homothetic cubes for every k >= k0 (Hadwiger cube-dissection problem; achievable k listed in A014544)

lower.source: Connor–Marmorino, J. Geometry 109:19: c(n) >= 2^(n+1)-1 for n >= 3 (cleanest refereed bound; see notes for the stronger classical claim)

upper.source: classical dissection identities: every k >= 48 is achievable (OEIS A014544 comments; Hadwiger-problem literature)

entries.witness_object: an explicit dissection of the unit cube into k smaller cubes for an unresolved count — 47 (which would force c(3) <= 47 and contradict the Gardner/Scott claim), or one of 31/32/42/44 whose impossibility Hickerson (2007) could not source
entries.witness_verifier: exact rational check of a list (corner, side) of axis-aligned subcubes: side lengths positive, volumes sum to 1, pairwise interior-disjoint, all contained in the unit cube — exact fractions, dependency-free
entries.notes: Literature is murky: Scott (AMM 54, 1947) reportedly showed the largest non-achievable k is 47 or 54, and Rychener & Zbinden (1977) dissected 54, which would give c(3)=48 exactly — but Hickerson's 2007 OEIS notes could not confirm proofs for 31,32,42,44,47 (impossibility of those five settles the whole list), so the community (and erdosproblems.com, citing Meier's conjecture c(3)=48) still treats it as open. LIBRARIAN TARGET: pull Scott 1947 + Gardner 1992 to possibly close c(3)=48. The Erdős problem is really about general n: is c(n) >> n^n? Known c(n) <= e^2 n^n, c(n) <= 1.8 n^(n+1) for n+1 prime (Connor–Marmorino 2018), c(n) << (2n)^(n-1) (Hudelson 1998), c(2)=6 (all paraphrased from problem page).

entries.quantity: Distributional questions for h(n) = extremal b such that 2^n-1, 3^n-1, ..., b^n-1 are mutually coprime: do densities delta_p = dens{n : h(n)=p} exist; is liminf h(n) infinite; does the largest prime p with p-1 | n, p > n^eps determine h(n)?
entries.witness_object: none — all three sub-questions are density-existence / liminf / characterization statements with no finite certificate; h(n) is computable per n but tables cannot decide any of them
entries.witness_verifier: n/a (per-n evaluation of h(n) is cheap gcd arithmetic, but no bounded quantity with a gap is at stake)
entries.notes: Known easy facts (paraphrased from problem page): h(n) = n+1 iff n+1 prime; h(n) unbounded on odd n; plausibly h(n)=3 infinitely often. The attached sequence A263647 (n with gcd(2^n-1, 3^n-1)=1, b-file to 10000 by R. Israel) is actually the sequence OEIS ties to the sibling problem #820 (its infinitude is the Ailon–Rudnick 2004 question); the hub attaches it here as the h(n) >= 4 indicator. No record lane.

checked: Ailon–Rudnick Acta Arith. 113 (2004) via OEIS link

entries.quantity: A390813(69) — size of the largest Sidon subset of the first 69 positive perfect squares (first uncomputed cell; table verified to n=68)

lower.source: a(68)=32 (OEIS A390813 data/b-file, Kalogeropoulos + Sievers) + trivial monotonicity a(n+1) >= a(n)

upper.source: trivial: deleting 69^2 from an optimal set shows a(69) <= a(68)+1 = 33

entries.witness_object: an explicit S ⊆ {1^2, ..., 69^2} with |S| = 33 and all pairwise sums distinct (settles a(69)=33); more generally any size-k Sidon subset of the first N squares beating the recorded a(N) row
entries.witness_verifier: O(k^2) integer check: form all k(k+1)/2 pairwise sums a+b (a<=b), assert no duplicates — dependency-free one-liner
entries.notes: The Erdős/Alon–Erdős 1985 question — is max Sidon subset of {1,4,...,N^2} of size N^(1-o(1))? — is an asymptotic wall: N^(2/3) lower (Lefmann–Thiele 1995) vs N^(1-c/loglog N) upper (Croot–Mao–Yip, posted in problem-page comments; page edited Apr 2026). The machine lane is the exact table: a(69) falls to bounded search (ILP / max-clique on the Sidon-conflict graph), and each new row is a small verified record. Sievers note: up to n=68 only n=32 fails to attain the max with a subset containing n^2.

entries.quantity: Verified range of Deaconescu's conjecture: for every n>1 with P = primorial(n), some prime p with p_n < p < P has P + p prime (falsifiable; checked for all n <= lower.value)

lower.source: Deaconescu's verification, reported in erdosproblems.com/779 remarks

entries.witness_object: per new n: a prime p in (p_n, P) with P + p (PRP-)prime, extending the verified range; a counterexample n (all such p fail) would falsify and close the problem — heuristically astronomically unlikely
entries.witness_verifier: gmpy2/PARI: rebuild primorial, assert p prime and p_n < p < P, PRP-test P+p; scouting-grade in seconds per n (full ECPP certification of P+p optional and heavy at large n)
entries.notes: Cheap record available: Fortunate numbers a(n) = A005235(n) satisfy gcd(a(n), P)=1, so a(n) < p_(n+1)^2 forces a(n) prime (Ordowski, OEIS comment) — and then a(n) itself is a Deaconescu witness for n. The b-file reaches n=4600 (Batalov; first 2000 terms verified prime, Arndt 2013), so auditing the b-file with the p_(n+1)^2 test (plus direct primality for any stragglers) should push the verified range 1000 -> ~4600 at near-zero compute. Proving the conjecture for all n is far beyond reach (needs Cramér-strength input); Erdős expected the least such p to be n^O(1) (paraphrased).

entries.quantity: Maximal density of A ⊆ {1..N} (resp. A ⊆ N) in which products of r elements never equal products of s elements with r ≠ s
entries.witness_object: none — the extremal constant for the [1,N] version (repetitions allowed) is already identified: max |A| = (1-c+o(1))N with c = Hall–Montgomery constant 0.17150049... (A143301), sharp via Granville–Soundararajan 2001 (per Tao's comment on the problem page); remaining open content (distinct-factors variant, exact density version) is asymptotic with no finite certificate
entries.witness_verifier: n/a (finite sets certify nothing about the o(1) terms; the constant itself is a closed form 1 + pi^2/6 + 2 Li_2(-sqrt(e)))
entries.notes: State (paraphrased from problem page): density version answered negatively — any valid A has density <= 1/2, and upper density <= 1-c with the same c; constructions: Selfridge 1/e - eps, Tao's one-large-prime-factor set ~0.8285 N for the interval version. What stays open is the variant where the products must use distinct elements (Er80 formulation). The attached OEIS entry is just the decimal expansion of the Hall–Montgomery constant (105 digits) — no table to extend, no witness lane.

entries.quantity: A066063(51) — minimal size of S ⊆ {0..51} with S+S ⊇ {0..51} (finite additive 2-basis; first uncomputed cell, table + b-file end at n=50)

lower.source: a(50)=12 (OEIS A066063 b-file) + trivial monotonicity (elements > t never appear in a sum equal to t, so an (n+1)-cover restricts to an n-cover)

upper.source: trivial: 0 is forced into every cover, so appending 51 to an optimal cover of {0..50} gives a(51) <= a(50)+1 = 13

entries.witness_object: an explicit S ⊆ {0..51}, |S| = 12, with S+S ⊇ {0..51} (settles a(51)=12); min-type quantity, so the smaller cover is the witness — any extended verified row of A066063 is a record
entries.witness_verifier: O(|S|^2) sumset check: mark all a+b for a,b in S, assert 0..51 all marked — dependency-free; exactness of a new cell additionally needs the (tiny, n=51) exhaustive/ILP no-11-cover certificate
entries.notes: The Erdős/Rohrbach question itself is asymptotic: g(n) ~ 2 sqrt(n) was disproved (Mrose 1979, g(n)^2 <= 3.5n); current frontier g(n)^2/n in [2.181 (Yu 2015), 294/85 = 3.4588 (Kohonen 2017, arXiv:1606.04770-line of work)] — all paraphrased from problem page and cross-checked against arXiv search (no post-2017 h=2 improvement found; arXiv:2507.23627 (2025) improves large-h postage-stamp, not h=2). Beating Kohonen's construction constant would be a genuine record but is expert-search-defeated (grade hard); the honest hours-scale lane is table extension a(51), a(52), ... (keyword 'more' in OEIS).

checked: arXiv search for post-2017 2-basis bounds (Yu limsup 0.4585 / Kohonen 85/294 confirmed)

entries.quantity: Growth of consecutive diagonal Ramsey gaps: is R(n+1)/R(n) >= 1+c for some c>0, and is R(n+1)-R(n) >> n^2?
entries.witness_object: none for this problem's content — both questions are for-all-large-n asymptotics; no finite Ramsey computation certifies them. The adjacent machine cells (diagonal values, e.g. R(5,5) in [43,46]: Exoo 1989 lower / Angeltveit–McKay arXiv:2409.15709 (2024) upper) belong to the dedicated Ramsey-value problems (#77/#78/#545), not here
entries.witness_verifier: n/a (a new diagonal Ramsey bound would be verified on its own board; it moves single data points, not the asymptotic gap statements)
entries.notes: Best known toward the difference question (paraphrased from problem page): Burr–Erdős–Faudree–Schelp 1989 give R(n+1)-R(n) >= 4n-8 for n >= 2, and the lower-bound machinery behind #165 gives R(n+2)-R(n) >> n^(2-o(1)) — a two-step, not one-step, gap. Even R(n+1)-R(n) >= cn^2 for a single step is open, and no witness object exists at any finite n. Cross-check: only 9 nontrivial exact diagonal/table values known in A059442 (through R(4,5)=25 etc.); the array is 'hard' keyword.

entries.quantity: A263647 extension — integers n with gcd(2^n-1, 3^n-1) = 1 (equivalently H(n)=3 in Erdős' notation); b-file ends at a(10000) = 24898

lower.source: OEIS A263647 b-file extent (10000 terms; all n <= 24898 classified)

entries.witness_object: an integer n > 24898 with gcd(2^n - 1, 3^n - 1) = 1 (extends the verified table; each such n certifies H(n)=3)
entries.witness_verifier: one bignum gcd: math.gcd(2**n - 1, 3**n - 1) == 1 in Python/GMP; ~n-bit operands, seconds even at n ~ 10^6; fully dependency-free
entries.notes: The actual open problem — is H(n)=3 infinitely often, and does H(n) = exp(n^{(c+o(1))/loglog n}) — cannot be settled by finite computation (wall). Known: Erdős (1974) proved H(n) > exp(n^{c/(loglog n)^2}) for infinitely many n; a sharpening to exp(n^{c/loglog n}) is sketched in an unrefereed site comment (van Doorn). H(n) for n=1..10 is 3,3,3,6,3,18,3,6,3,12 (numeric facts paraphrased from erdosproblems.com/820). A263647 conjectured infinite (Ailon–Rudnick, per OEIS comment). Machine lane = table extension only; it is a bookkeeping rung, not a bound-mover. Grade honest: extending the b-file is hours of compute; the conjecture itself is a wall.

entries.quantity: limsup_n log g(n)/log n, where g(n) = #{m : phi(m) = n} (A014197) — the totient-multiplicity exponent

lower.source: Lichtman, arXiv:2211.09641 (shifted primes p-1 with no prime factor > p^0.2844 ⇒ g(n) > n^0.71568 infinitely often); confirmed as best known at erdosproblems.com/821 (edited 2025-10-01)

upper.source: elementary: phi(m) >> m/loglog m forces m <= n^{1+o(1)}, so g(n) <= n^{1+o(1)}; exponent 1 is the conjectured truth

entries.witness_object: none — the quantity is an asymptotic limsup exponent; no single finite n (however large g(n)/n^0.71568) moves either bound
entries.witness_verifier: n/a. Finite record tables (popular totient values) are computable by enumerating m <= C·n·loglog n with phi(m)=n, but they certify nothing about the limsup
entries.notes: Erdős conjectured the exponent is 1 (g(n) > n^{1-eps} infinitely often). History: Pillai limsup g = infinity; Erdős 1935 g(n) > n^c i.o.; Baker–Harman 1998 was the previous best exponent; Lichtman 2022 smoothness threshold 0.2843–0.2844 gives 0.71568. Freshness: erdosproblems.com/821 (2025-10) cites Lichtman as current best; no 2026 improvement found in searches. Progress requires prime-counting theorems about smooth p-1, not witnesses — pure wall for the records lane.

entries.quantity: infinitude of amicable pairs, and whether the count A(x) of pairs below x satisfies A(x) > x^{1-o(1)}
entries.witness_object: a new amicable pair (a,b) with sigma(a)=sigma(b)=a+b is trivially verifiable, but no finite list of pairs moves the infinitude or density questions
entries.witness_verifier: factor a and b, compute sigma via the divisor-sum formula, check sigma(a)=sigma(b)=a+b — seconds for numbers of ordinary size
entries.notes: Pure existence/density conjecture — not gap-shaped. State of computation: ALL amicable pairs with smaller member < 10^21 are known — the Chernykh 'Amicable Numbers' BOINC exhaustive search finished 2026-04-08 (4,810,863 pairs with 21 digits; sech.me/ap/news.html); database passed 10^9 known pairs back in 2016. State of theory: infinitude is open in both directions; Erdős 1955 A(x)=o(x); Pomerance 2015 A(x) <= x·exp(-(1/2+o(1))(log x · loglog x)^{1/2}) (facts paraphrased from erdosproblems.com/830; Guy B4). New-pair hunting is routine for the BOINC community but mathematically inert here — recommend the scout NOT spend cycles.

entries.quantity: smallest integer a admitting >= 5 solutions of C(n,k) = a with 1 <= k <= n/2 (Singmaster / Erdős–Gordon multiplicity t=5; the t<=4 cells are occupied by a=2,6,120,3003)

lower.source: Blokhuis–Brouwer–de Weger (Integers 17 (2017) #A64, per OEIS A003015 comment): all numbers < 10^60 occurring >= 5 times in the full triangle are the 9 known A003015 terms, whose maximum half-triangle multiplicity is 4 (a=3003); earlier 33·10^16 bound by D. W. Wilson

entries.witness_object: an integer a together with 5 distinct pairs (n,k), 1 <= k <= n/2, all satisfying C(n,k) = a
entries.witness_verifier: for each k from 1 to ~log2(a), binary-search n with C(n,k) = a (C is monotone in n); count exact hits — milliseconds per candidate with bignums, no dependencies
entries.notes: Famous 50+-year target (Singmaster 1971; Erdős credits himself and Gordon), but graded hard-not-historic deliberately: Erdős and Singmaster both believed the answer is NO (multiplicity bounded), so a t>=5 witness is conjectured nonexistent — do not burn GPU on blind search. Known multiplicity ladder (half-triangle convention): t=3 at a=120, t=4 at a=3003; infinitely many a with t=3 via C(n,k)=C(n-1,k+1) (Fibonacci parametric family, A090162), including 61218182743304701891431482520. Best theory: Matomäki–Radziwiłł–Shao–Tao–Teräväinen, arXiv:2106.03335 — at most two solutions once k >= exp((log n)^{2/3+eps}). The Erdős form ('for every t some a has EXACTLY t solutions') has open cells at every t >= 5.

entries.quantity: A079007(31) (records chain of A053597/A078515/A079889) — smallest prime starting a run of 31 pairwise-distinct consecutive prime gaps; current record run length is 30, at p = 196948778371

lower.source: A079007 is nondecreasing and a(30) = 196948778371 (Donovan Johnson, OEIS extension Oct 2012); record table untouched since 2012

entries.witness_object: a prime p starting >= 31 pairwise-distinct consecutive prime gaps (any such p upper-bounds A079007(31) and sets a new record for Erdős' h(x) run-length function)
entries.witness_verifier: from p, iterate next_prime 31 times (deterministic Miller–Rabin/BPSW is exact below 2^64), check the 31 gaps are pairwise distinct — microseconds; claiming the exact cell additionally needs an exhaustive segmented sieve below p (primesieve-class, no exotic dependencies)
entries.notes: Best machine cell in this chunk: the record frontier (~1.97e11) is 2012-era; primesieve sweeps 10^12 in CPU-hours, and a rolling 32-gap distinctness window is trivial code — hours-to-days on one box should extend A079007 by several cells. Orientation: this moves the record table for h(x), NOT the open asymptotics — Erdős asked whether (log x)^c < h(x) and h(x) = o(log x); only h(x) -> infinity is proven (Brun sieve), so the asymptotic problem itself is a wall. Site marks 'claims of partial results in comments' (unverified, not relied on).

entries.quantity: A390769 extension — r(n) = least even positive integer absent from the first n prime gaps (Erdős' r(x)); b-file holds only 72 terms

lower.source: OEIS A390769 b-file extent (sequence created late 2025, per A-number range and erdosproblems.com/853 link)

entries.witness_object: an extended exact table of r(n) to large n, i.e. for each jump value t the index of the first occurrence of gap t among consecutive primes
entries.witness_verifier: single segmented-sieve pass tracking the set of gaps seen and recomputing the least absent even value at each new gap — dependency-free; independently cross-checkable against published first-occurrence prime-gap tables (Nicely / Oliveira e Silva) which reach ~4·10^18
entries.notes: Very fresh, very shallow sequence (72 terms) — trivially extendable to n ~ 10^10 with a sieve in hours; the first-occurrence-gap literature effectively determines r up to 2^64 already, so the b-file push is mostly librarian work plus independent verification. The mathematical content is a wall: r(x) -> infinity is equivalent to every even number eventually occurring as a consecutive-prime gap (open, Polignac-adjacent), and r(x)/log x -> infinity is stronger still. Erdős' original statement omitted 'even' — the site notes the correction (paraphrased from erdosproblems.com/853, Er85c).

entries.quantity: A389839(13) — smallest even integer that is not a difference of consecutive integers coprime to 41# = 304250263527210 (primorial of the first 13 primes); table known only for prime(2)#..prime(12)#

lower.value: a(12) = 68 is the last computed cell (for 37#)

entries.witness_object: occurrence certificates: for each even t < v, a pair of CONSECUTIVE 41#-coprime integers differing by t — proving a(13) >= v+2 cheaply; pinning a(13) exactly requires a full-period absence scan (the entry's symmetry remark halves the work)
entries.witness_verifier: per certificate (x, x+t): gcd(x, 41#) = gcd(x+t, 41#) = 1 and every x+1..x+t-1 shares a factor with 41# — O(t) word-sized gcds each; the exhaustive side is a wheel-based segmented scan of one period, ~4.4·10^13 coprime gaps
entries.notes: Directly quantifies Erdős' #854: he first guessed every even t up to the maximal gap occurs, then reported doubts after Lacampagne–Selfridge computations showed failure at 13# = 30030 (paraphrased from erdosproblems.com/854, Ob1) — visible in the data as a(6)=20 missing while the Jacobsthal max gap A048670(6)=22 occurs. The max-gap side is far ahead of the spectrum side: A048670 known through k=64 (a(64)=1110; Bozek 2021, Google-Cloud-scale). a(13) is a genuine frontier cell: period 3.04·10^14, coprime density ~0.145 — days-scale on one strong box with a bitmap wheel + symmetry, GPU-friendly. Second Erdős question (are there >> maxgap many occurring even values?) remains open regardless.

entries.quantity: y* = smallest interval width y with an admissible tuple of more than pi(y) elements (Hensley–Richards violation width; rho*(y) > pi(y) makes the second Hardy–Littlewood conjecture contradict the prime k-tuples conjecture)

lower.source: exact rho*(n) values (Engelsma) from the OEIS A023193 b-file satisfy rho*(n) <= pi(n) for all 2 <= n <= 2330 — recomputed locally against a sieve on 2026-07-18 (min slack 0, at n=2)

upper.source: Engelsma's admissible 447-tuple of width 3159 (diameter 3158) with pi(3159) = 446 — opertech.com/primes/k-tuples.html, cited in OEIS A023193 comments; pi(3159)=446 verified locally

entries.witness_object: an admissible k-tuple inside [0, y-1] with k > pi(y) and y < 3159 (or: extended exact rho*(n) computations past n = 2330, which raise the lower bound)
entries.witness_verifier: admissibility: for each prime p <= k check the residues mod p miss at least one class — O(k·pi(k)) integer ops; pi(y) by direct sieve; both dependency-free and instant
entries.notes: The famous conjecture itself (pi(x+y) <= pi(x)+pi(y), 'second Hardy–Littlewood') is expected FALSE but only conditionally refuted: Hensley–Richards 1973/74 + tuples conjecture; Clark–Jarvis 2001 kill the Straus variant similarly; best unconditional is Montgomery–Vaughan 1973 pi(x+y) <= pi(x) + 2y/log y (facts paraphrased from erdosproblems.com/855, edited 2026-04-08 — fresh). No witness settles the conjecture itself (that side is a wall); the machine-movable object is the [2331, 3159] window for y*: Engelsma's site reports exact rho* somewhat beyond the OEIS b-file (unverified here, hence the conservative 2330), and decades of dense-tuple search (incl. polymath8-era tooling) never found a violation below 3159 — grade hard. Exact closure would need Engelsma-style exhaustive rho* to ~3159: conceivable with modern branch-and-bound/SAT, hence 'unknown' not 'wall'.

checked: OEIS A023193 (entry + full b-file, 2330 terms; local rho* vs pi verification)

entries.quantity: h(n) — smallest L such that EVERY interval (m, m+L] contains distinct integers a_1..a_pi(n) with p_i | a_i (system-of-distinct-representatives strengthening of the Jacobsthal function; Erdős–Pomerance)

lower.source: Erdős–Selfridge (the 3n bound) and Ruzsa (superlinearity), as reported at erdosproblems.com/860 (Er92c/ErPo80 context)

upper.source: Erdős–Pomerance 1980 [ErPo80], per erdosproblems.com/860 (Guy B32)

entries.witness_object: none for the asymptotic gap (n^1 vs n^{3/2} orders — no finite computation moves either exponent); per fixed n, a Hall-violating residue m mod n# does certify h(n) > L, which would only feed a finite table
entries.witness_verifier: for fixed n, m, L: build the bipartite graph primes-p_i-vs-multiples in (m, m+L], run Hopcroft–Karp; absence of a perfect matching is a poly-time-checkable certificate that h(n) > L — dependency-free
entries.notes: The bounded quantity is an asymptotic order gap (linear-times-unbounded vs n^{3/2}), so the records lane has nothing to move — wall. The linked OEIS anchors are the NON-distinct analogues (Jacobsthal of primorials A048670, known to k=64; run-length version A058989 to n=64): they lower-bound nothing here directly since h demands distinct representatives. Librarian spawn opportunity: a small exact table of h(p_k) appears to be absent from OEIS entirely — computable for k up to ~9 by scanning m over the primorial period with a matching test (open-easy as a computation, but it creates a new sequence rather than moving a known cell). Facts paraphrased from erdosproblems.com/860; page edited 2025-09-30.

entries.quantity: A389182(101) — max size of A ⊆ {1..101} such that at most one integer has ≥2 representations as a+b (a≤b∈A); first uncomputed table cell (table complete through n=100)

lower.source: monotonicity from OEIS A389182 b-file a(100)=16 (David Spencer)

upper.source: increment bound a(n+1) ≤ a(n)+1 (deleting the element n+1 preserves validity; derived here)

entries.witness_object: a 17-element subset of {1..101} in which at most one sum value has multiple representations (pins a(101)=17; any exhaustive search closing the cell also counts)
entries.witness_verifier: O(|A|^2): build multiset {a+b : a≤b∈A}, count values with multiplicity ≥2, accept iff ≤1 such value; pure-python, milliseconds
entries.notes: Sequence created Oct 2025 — everything past n=100 is fresh territory; branch-and-bound/ILP plausibly extends the table well past 150 in hours. Asymptotic layer (the actual Erdős question): Erdős–Freud [ErFr91] construction gives liminf a(N)/√N ≥ 2/√3 ≈ 1.1547 (Sidon set in [1,N/3] ∪ its reflection); conjectured tight. One-exception sets are quasi-Sidon (only one colliding sum ⇒ |A+A| ~ |A|²/2, containment derived here), so Pikhurko's #840 upper bound (1/4+1/(π+2)²)^{-1/2} ≈ 1.8626 applies [Pi06]; erdosproblems.com lists #864 as a weaker form of #840. Asymptotic gap [1.1547, 1.8626] on the constant is a wall; the table lane is the machine surface.

entries.quantity: f(n) = A339378(n) power-sum (sum over p|n of the largest power of p ≤ n) — asymptotics of max_{n≤x} f(n), of H(x)=Σ_{n<x} f(n)/n, and almost-everywhere comparison with the coprime-support maximum F(n)
entries.witness_object: none — every sub-question is an asymptotic (o/~/≪) statement; no finite witness moves any of them
entries.witness_verifier: n/a — A339378(n) is cheaply computable at any single n, but finite computation cannot certify asymptotics
entries.notes: Known (per erdosproblems.com/878, paraphrased): Erdős [Er84e] proved max_{n≤x} f(n) ~ x·log x/log log x along a sequence of x→∞, and x·log_4(x) ≪ H(x) ≪ x·log_3(x) (iterated logs); f(n)/n has no mean value (limsup of the partial averages is infinite, liminf finite). Recent comment activity (Barreto, incorporated May 2026): the H upper bound already gives f(n)=o(n log log n) for almost all n, and the guess max_{n≤x} f = max_{n≤x} F fails at x=210. No record lane.

entries.quantity: A186736(2501) = G(2501) — maximum sum of a pairwise-coprime subset of {1..2501}; first uncomputed table cell (table complete through n=2500)

lower.source: monotonicity from OEIS A186736 b-file a(2500)=455192 (Matt Ready; first 250 terms Michael B. Porter)

upper.source: increment bound G(n+1) ≤ G(n)+(n+1) (derived here)

entries.witness_object: a pairwise-coprime S ⊆ {1..2501} with element-sum > 455192 (raises the cell's lower bound; exhaustive/DP search closes the cell exactly)
entries.witness_verifier: check gcd(a,b)=1 for all pairs (~|S|² ≈ 10^5 gcds) and sum the elements; milliseconds, dependency-free
entries.notes: Three published programs on OEIS (PARI, Python, C++ 'faster algorithm'); b-file was pushed 250→2500 in 2024, so further extension is a realistic hours-scale lane. The actual Erdős–van Lint asymptotic question (is G(n) > H(n) − n^{1+o(1)} where H(n)=Σ_{p<n} p + n·π(√n)?) is a wall: known H−n^{3/2−o(1)} < G < H and (H−G)/n → ∞, with the stronger bound proved only under strong prime-distribution assumptions (per erdosproblems.com/879, paraphrased). Structure side-question: optimal sets containing an element with ≥k prime factors is known only for k=2 — extended exact tables give data for that too.

entries.quantity: f(2n)/f(n) where f(n) = Σ_{k≤n} τ(2^k−1) (τ over A046801) — does the ratio tend to a limit?
entries.witness_object: none — limit-existence question; no finite witness decides it, and extending A046801 requires factoring Mersenne numbers (Cunningham-project-hard beyond the b-file's n=1206)
entries.witness_verifier: n/a — verifying a new τ(2^k−1) value needs a certified complete factorization of 2^k−1, which is not a cheap check at the open frontier
entries.notes: Kovač–Luca (arXiv:2506.04883, 2025) proved the ratio sequence f(2n)/f(n) is unbounded, ruling out every finite limit, with conditional results and numerics supporting lim = ∞ (building on a Cambie heuristic, per erdosproblems.com/893). Remaining open content: whether the limit exists (=∞) — an asymptotic wall, no record lane. Lean-formalized (research open) in formal-conjectures.

entries.quantity: h(n) = A071626(n) — number of distinct exponents in the prime factorization of n!; existence of c>0 with h(n) ~ c·(n/log n)^{1/2}
entries.witness_object: none — the open content is the existence of the asymptotic constant; h(n) itself is cheap to compute (equals #{floor(n/p) : p ≤ n prime}, Heyman–Miraj) but numerics cannot certify an asymptotic
entries.witness_verifier: n/a — any single h(n) value is verifiable in seconds via Legendre's formula, but that moves nothing
entries.notes: Erdős–Selfridge proved h(n) ≍ (n/log n)^{1/2} [Er82c]. Tao's Cramér-model heuristic (Aug 2025 forum comment, referenced from OEIS A071626 links) predicts c = √(2π) ≈ 2.5066 (per erdosproblems.com/912, paraphrased). DISCREPANCY FLAG: the hub stub carries formal-conjectures formal_status 'research solved', but erdosproblems.com/912 showed OPEN when fetched 2026-07-18 — treat as open; the Lean file plausibly formalizes the solved ≍ statement rather than the ~ conjecture. B-file to 10^4 (Corneth) enables constant-testing numerics only.

checked: OEIS A071626 + links (Tao forum comment pointer, Heyman–Miraj arXiv:2309.16072)

entries.quantity: A359747 verified frontier — largest verified k such that k(k+1) has all-distinct exponents in its prime factorization (empirical support for the infinitude conjecture)

lower.source: OEIS A359747 b-file, exhaustive first 1000 terms (Amiram Eldar)

entries.witness_object: new terms k > 32327407 (extends the verified range / b-file; evidence only — infinitude is the open content). Sharper target from OEIS comment: a term k > 8 with BOTH k and k+1 powerful (checked absent through A060355(39))
entries.witness_verifier: factor k and k+1 (trial division + Pollard rho, trivial at 10^8–10^12), pool the exponents of the two coprime factorizations, check all pairwise distinct; seconds
entries.notes: Every term has k or k+1 powerful (OEIS comment), so terms are Pell-sparse but easy to enumerate. Conditional resolution (per erdosproblems.com/913, paraphrased): infinitely many primes p with 8p²−1 also prime would settle it affirmatively via n=8p²−1 (exponents {1,2,3}) — a Schinzel-type hypothesis, i.e. the theoretical side is a wall. Table extension and the both-powerful side-hunt are the cheap lanes; neither closes the problem.

entries.quantity: natural density of {n : P(n) < n^α and P(n+1) < (n+1)^β} for fixed α,β ∈ (0,1) — existence (conjectured value ρ(1/α)·ρ(1/β), Dickman ρ)
entries.witness_object: none — density-existence question; no finite computation or witness object bears on it
entries.witness_verifier: n/a — individual smooth pairs are trivially checkable (factor n, n+1 via A006530 logic) but carry no weight
entries.notes: State of the art (per erdosproblems.com/928, paraphrased; page last edited 2026-04-03): Teräväinen [Te18] proved the LOGARITHMIC density exists and equals ρ(1/α)ρ(1/β); Wang [Wa21] gets the natural density conditionally on Elliott–Halberstam for friable integers; single-condition density is Dickman 1930. Open content: unconditional natural density. Infinitude of simultaneous smooth pairs follows from Schinzel [Sc67b] (Meza observation). No record lane.

entries.quantity: A387864 verified frontier — largest verified r such that at least two integers strictly between p_r and p_{r+1} are (p_{r+1}−p_r)-smooth (empirical support for the infinitude conjecture)

lower.source: OEIS A387864 b-file, exhaustive first 10000 terms (Chai Wah Wu)
