entries.witness_object: new terms r > 6386830: exhibit the prime pair (p_r, p_{r+1}) and ≥2 gap integers with complete factorizations into primes < p_{r+1}−p_r (extends the verified range; evidence only — infinitude is the open content)
entries.witness_verifier: compute p_r, p_{r+1} (sieve; p_r ≈ 1.2e8 at the frontier); for each n in the gap, trial-divide by primes below the gap length and confirm full factorization; seconds, dependency-free
entries.notes: Erdős [Er76d] believed it true but with density-0 r (he proved density 0 even for one such n); empirically abundant regardless — 10^4 terms below 6.4e6. Tao is among those flagging the problem 'difficult' on the page (fetched 2026-07-18). Lean-formalized (research open). Sequence created Sep 2025 (Radcliffe); frontier extension is a cheap standing lane but cannot close the problem.

entries.quantity: E_2 = limsup_n log_n Q_2(n(n+1)(n+2)) — powerful-part exponent over 3 consecutive integers (ℓ=2 case of #935; Q_2 = A057521 powerful part)

lower.source: Pell-family construction (van Doorn; also given in [Fe26]) makes Q_2(n(n+1)(n+2))/n² unbounded, per erdosproblems.com/935; Mahler already gives limsup ratio ≥ 1 for every ℓ

upper.source: trivial: Q_2(n(n+1)(n+2)) ≤ n(n+1)(n+2) ~ n³ (derived here)

entries.witness_object: an explicit infinite family (polynomial identity / Pell-type recurrence) certifying Q_2(n(n+1)(n+2)) ≥ n^{2+δ} for fixed δ>0 infinitely often — this would refute Erdős's conjecture E_2 = 2. Single large-exponent n's are cheap evidence but do not move the limsup
entries.witness_verifier: factor n, n+1, n+2 (ECM at moderate sizes; for a parametric family verify the identity symbolically), take the product of prime powers with exponent ≥ 2, compare log Q_2/log n against 2+δ
entries.notes: Erdős's main question asserts E_2 = 2 (Q_2 < n^{2+ε} eventually for every ε), which he called very difficult; ABC implies the weaker Q_2 = o(n^{ℓ+1}) (question 3) per [Fe26] (all per erdosproblems.com/935, paraphrased; page last edited 2026-02-08). Question 2 (limsup Q_2/n² = ∞ for ℓ≥2) is RESOLVED affirmatively via the x²−8y²=1 Pell family (van Doorn / [Fe26], essentially #367). For ℓ=1 the exponent question is closed trivially: Q_2(n(n+1)) ≤ n(n+1) and consecutive powerful pairs (A060355) attain it, so E_1 = 2 exactly (derived here); A389244 tabulates the ℓ=1 values. Second linked sequence: A389244.

entries.quantity: Brocard sub-case search frontier: largest N such that no n in (7, N] has n!+1 a perfect square (A146968 = {4,5,7}); the erdosproblems statement asks whether 2^n±1 and n!±1 are powerful only finitely often

lower.source: Matson quadratic-residue search (unsolvedproblems.org S73.pdf), reported in OEIS A085692 comment by Radcliffe; rigorous published records: 1e9 Berndt–Galway 2000, 4e9 Tim Peters 2006

entries.witness_object: either (a) an extended exhaustive-scan claim 'no n ≤ N with n!+1 square' for N > 1e12, or (b) a 4th n with n!+1 a perfect square (sporadic-solution shock outcome)
entries.witness_verifier: (b) verifies in seconds: incremental r_p = n! mod p over ~30 primes, quadratic-residue filter, then exact integer-sqrt of n!+1. (a) has no cheap certificate — verification = independent re-run of the residue sieve (trust-the-log record)
entries.notes: Range extension is sequential-per-prime but parallel-across-primes: ~1e12 mulmods per prime chain = hours on one box. Only the square sub-case is machine-frontier; testing n!±1 or 2^n±1 for POWERFUL requires full factorization (no cheap search; 2^n±1 factor state = Cunningham tables). Known powerful value: 2^3+1=9. Conditional results: Cushing–Pascoe 2016 (abc ⇒ finitely many powerful values near n!), CrowdMath 2020 (abc ⇒ same for 2^n±1), per erdosproblems #936 remarks. Wasserman 2005: no square n!+1 values < 10^20000. Matson's 1e12 is a claim reported in OEIS, not a refereed publication — treat as soft frontier; 4e9 (Peters) is the conservative rigorous line.

entries.quantity: 3-term arithmetic progressions of CONSECUTIVE powerful numbers n_k, n_{k+1}, n_{k+2} (A001694): complete table up to search limit; largest known example

lower.value: 1e12 (search limit; exactly 10 triples exist with all terms ≤ 1e12, largest = (697592448400, 697593096200, 697593744000), gap 647800)
lower.source: local exhaustive computation this session: all a^2*b^3 ≤ 1e12 generated (2158391 powerful numbers), sorted, consecutive-triple equal-gap scan

entries.witness_object: a triple (x,y,z) of powerful numbers with y−x = z−y and no powerful number strictly between x and z, with x > 697592448400 (extends the largest-known table) or a raised exhaustive search limit
entries.witness_verifier: powerfulness: factor each of the 3 numbers (Pollard rho, ms) and check min prime-exponent ≥ 2; consecutiveness: enumerate all a^2*b^3 in [x,z] (O(sqrt(z)) work) and confirm none lies strictly between; fully dependency-free
entries.notes: The 10 triples ≤ 1e12: (1728,1764,1800), (6912,7056,7200), (729000,729316,729632), (1458000,1458632,1459264), (2916000,2917264,2918528), (11664000,11669056,11674112), (149022674775,...), (260102040004,...), (348796224200,...), (697592448400,...). NOT in OEIS as of 2026-07-18 (searched 697592448400 and initial terms — no hits): candidate new sequence + submission. Scaling a triple by a square preserves AP+powerful but consecutiveness must be re-verified, so infinite families are not automatic. Erdős's finiteness question itself cannot fall to computation (abc-flavored). Context: Chan, J. Int. Seq. 26 (2023) 23.1.1 (APs among powerful numbers, not-necessarily-consecutive); Chan arXiv:2503.21485 (n,n+1,n+2 variant, cf. Erdős #364). Extending the exhaustive limit to 1e14–1e16 is minutes-to-hours (O(sqrt(X)) powerful numbers).

checked: local exhaustive enumeration ≤ 1e12 (this session, code in scan above)
OEIS search for triple values (no hits)

entries.quantity: A048892(19) — smallest start of 19 consecutive integers with pairwise distinct numbers of divisors (first uncomputed cell; F(a(n)) ≥ n feeds the Erdős–Mirsky F(x) ≤ (log x)^{O(1)} question)

lower.source: = A048892(18) (Martin Ehrenstein, OEIS ext Aug 2023) + monotonicity: any 19-run start is an 18-run start, so a(19) ≥ a(18)

entries.witness_object: an integer n such that tau(n), tau(n+1), ..., tau(n+18) are pairwise distinct (any such n upper-bounds a(19); no 19-run is currently known)
entries.witness_verifier: factor 19 integers (~1e14–1e17 scale, Pollard rho ms each), compute divisor counts, check 19 distinct values — seconds, dependency-free
entries.notes: a(16)=3.47e11, a(17)=2.62e12, a(18)=2.40e14 — extrapolation suggests a(19) ~ 1e16, i.e. Ehrenstein-class dedicated sieving (weeks), hence graded hard despite the trivially cheap verifier. Underlying asymptotics (Erdős–Mirsky 1952): (log x)^{1/2}/loglog x ≪ F(x) ≪ exp(O((log x)^{1/2}/loglog x)); erdosproblems #945 credits Beker with upper F(x) ≪ exp(O((log x)^{1/3+o(1)})) — primary source NOT located on arXiv (2026-07-18 search), treat as site-remark attribution; Cambie: Cramér ⇒ F(x) ≪ (log x)^2. Exact cell falls to bounded exhaustive scan + cheap verify exactly as a(17), a(18) did.

checked: arXiv search for Beker upper bound (not located)

entries.quantity: error term E(x) = #{(i,j): a_i + a_j ≤ x} − x for Rosen's greedy sequence (A390642); Erdős asks if E(x) = O(x^{1/4+o(1)})
entries.witness_object: none that moves a bound — the question is the asymptotic order of E(x); no finite computation certifies an exponent
entries.witness_verifier: n/a; exploratory computation is cheap (the greedy rule is elementary — extend A390642 to ~1e6 terms, x ~ 1e12, plot E(x) and fit the exponent) but proves nothing
entries.notes: By construction the solution count is ≥ x; Erdős and Rosen could not even prove it is ≤ (1+o(1))x (per erdosproblems #954 remarks). OEIS A390642 is brand new (created 2025-12-01, 55 terms, no b-file) with the comment-conjecture a(n) ~ (pi/8) n^2. No literature bounds found. Scout value: numeric exponent-fit at scale is a cheap exploratory (non-record) task; extending the OEIS sequence + b-file is a trivial concrete contribution.

entries.quantity: A213253(269) — first cell beyond Najman's computed range n=1..268; f(n) = minimal L such that every L consecutive integers > n contain one with a prime factor > n (equivalently 1 + longest run of n-smooth integers > n)

lower.source: = A213253(268) (b-file from Najman, arXiv:1108.3710 / Arch. Math. 97 (2011)) + monotonicity of f

upper.source: Sylvester–Schur theorem: f(k) ≤ k (Sylvester 1892; Erdős 1934 proof)

entries.witness_object: a run of L ≥ 16 consecutive integers, all > 269 and all 269-smooth (gives A213253(269) ≥ L+1)
entries.witness_verifier: trial-divide each of the L integers by the 57 primes ≤ 269 — microseconds, dependency-free
entries.notes: b-file (268 terms) unchanged since it was entered from Najman's paper; no extension located (2026-07 search). The EXACT value needs a nonexistence proof over all integers — Najman's bounded Lehmer/Pell (Størmer-type) machinery achieved this for n ≤ 268 but its scaling past there is unclear to me, hence exact_feasibility unknown rather than cell. Asymptotics: f(k) < 3k/log k (Erdős 1955); f(k) ≪ (logloglog k/loglog k)·k/log k (Jutila 1974; Ramachandra–Shorey 1973); conjectured (log k)^{O(1)}, heuristically (log k)^2 (Najman remark in A213253). Site marks this essentially equivalent to erdosproblems #683. Adjacent fresh work: van Doorn–Tang arXiv:2606.19863 (June 2026) treats runs avoiding primes in (k,2k) — related family, not this quantity.

checked: web search for b-file extensions (none found)

entries.quantity: A327909(1000) — smallest start of a run of 1000 consecutive integers each having a prime factor > 1000 (first cell beyond the 999-term b-file; per-n table underlying Erdős's k(n))

lower.source: = A327909(999) (b-file, David Spencer ext of Thomas Garrison's 369 terms) + monotonicity: the first 999 integers of any valid 1000-run form a valid 999-run

entries.witness_object: a start s with each of s, s+1, ..., s+999 having a prime factor > 1000; for the exact cell, the least such s certified by exhaustive scan below it
entries.witness_verifier: for each of the 1000 integers, divide out all primes ≤ 1000 and check the cofactor exceeds 1 — milliseconds, dependency-free
entries.notes: a(999) ≈ 2.24e10 and the sequence grows slowly (long constant stretches), so a segmented 1000-smooth sieve to ~1e11 (≈ 2e11 division ops in C) settles a(1000) in hours on one box — among the easiest genuine cells in this chunk. Asymptotics for Erdős's k(n): log k(n) ≫ sqrt(log n loglog n) (Erdős 1976, per erdosproblems #962); constant improved to (1/sqrt(2) − o(1)) attributed to Tang in site remarks (2026) — primary arXiv source NOT located, treat as site attribution; upper: k(n) ≤ (1+o(1))sqrt(n) (Tao, site comment); Erdős reported exp(−(log n)^c)·sqrt(n). Extending the table gives data for the log k(n) = (log n)^{1/2+o(1)} question. b-file year unrecorded (entry rev 2025-10-28), hence year null.

checked: arXiv search for Tang bound (not located; site remark only)

entries.quantity: lower density of {n : p_n/n < p_{n+1}/(n+1)} (the index set behind A387591) — conjectured positive
entries.witness_object: none — a positive-density existence question; finite counts cannot certify positive lower density
entries.witness_verifier: n/a; empirical density to n ~ 1e9 is a cheap sieve computation (A387591 b-file already has 10000 terms) but is exploratory only
entries.notes: Erdős–Prachar 1961 [ErPr61] proved the COMPLEMENT {u_n > u_{n+1}} has positive (lower) density and that sum over p_n < x of |u_{n+1} − u_n| ≍ (log x)^2 (per erdosproblems #968 remarks). Erdős also asked for infinitely many monotone triples u_n < u_{n+1} < u_{n+2} and the reverse. Tao has flagged the problem 'looks difficult' on the site (2026). A387591 is new (Sep 2025). No bounded record quantity identified.

entries.quantity: exponent theta = inf{t : E(x) ≪ x^t} of the error term E(x) = Q(x) − (6/pi^2)x in squarefree counting (A013928 is Q's counting function)

lower.source: Evelyn–Linfoot: E(x) = Omega(x^{1/4})

upper.value: 1/2 (elementary; best unconditional is x^{1/2−o(1)}-type savings, Walfisz; on RH: 11/35 + o(1), Liu)
upper.source: Walfisz 1963 (unconditional); Liu 2016 (conditional on RH), per erdosproblems #969 remarks

entries.witness_object: none — an asymptotic exponent has no cheap certificate; numeric extreme-value tables of |E(x)|/x^{1/4} (cf. Cohen–Dress–El Marraki: |E(x)| < 0.02767 sqrt(x) for x ≥ 438653) are exploratory only
entries.witness_verifier: n/a for the exponent; E(x) itself is exactly computable in O(sqrt(x)) via the Moebius sum, so data generation is trivial — but no finite computation moves theta
entries.notes: E(x) ≪ x^{1/4+o(1)} would IMPLY RH (per erdosproblems #969), and even under RH the true order is unknown — a definitional analytic wall for the records lane. Believed true order: x^{1/4}. PNT already gives o(x^{1/2}). Schnirelmann-density curiosity in A013928: min a(n)/n = 53/88 at n=176 (Rogers 1964).

checked: OEIS A013928 (rev 2026-07-15; Cohen–Dress–El Marraki and Walfisz refs)

entries.quantity: A048670(65) — Jacobsthal function g of the product of the first 65 primes (first uncomputed primorial cell; the lower-bound engine for Erdős's h(k) and the h(k) ≪ k^2 question)

lower.source: = A048670(64) (Andrzej Bozek, computation supported by Google Cloud) + monotonicity: any run sharing factors with p_64# also shares factors with p_65#

entries.witness_object: an explicit u (or CRT residue certificate) such that each of u+1, ..., u+L shares a prime factor ≤ p_65 = 313 with the primorial, with L+1 > best known lower bound for a(65)
entries.witness_verifier: L gcd computations against p_65#, or per-offset covering-prime list — trivially checkable, dependency-free
entries.notes: Exact a(62)–a(64) required Google-Cloud-scale search (Bozek 2021); expert tooling is Ziller–Morack arXiv:1611.03310 + Ziller arXiv:1903.11973 — a(65) exact is bounded-exhaustive but heavy; good greedy/covering lower-bound runs are a hours-to-days heuristic-search game. CAVEAT for the Erdős quantity: Hajdu–Saradha (Math. Comp. 81, 2012) disproved Jacobsthal's conjecture that primorials maximize — at k=24 some 24-prime-factor n has g = 236 > a(24) = 234 — so A048670 lower-bounds h(k) but is not h(k) itself. Asymptotics: Iwaniec 1978 h(k) ≪ (k log k)^2; FGKMT (arXiv:1412.5029, JAMS 2018) g(p_k#) ≫ k log k logloglog k/loglog k; Pintz-type refinement with constant 2e^gamma in A048670 comments; Maier–Pomerance heuristic suggests a(n) = n(log n)^{3+o(1)}, i.e. Jacobsthal's k^2 conjecture as stated in #970 is expected FALSE at the (log k) level while h(k) ≪ k^{1+o(1)}... note carefully: heuristic n(log n)^3 is consistent with h(k) ≪ k^2. Stub lists A048669 (g(n) itself, easy, b-file 1e4); the record cell lives in A048670.

checked: OEIS A048670 + b-file (64 terms; a(64)=1110; Bozek ext 2021)

entries.quantity: existence of c > 0 with #{a mod d : p(a,d) > (1+c) phi(d) log d} ≫ phi(d) for ALL large d, where p(a,d) = least prime ≡ a (mod d) (A226521 tabulates p(a,d))
entries.witness_object: none — a for-all-large-d positive-proportion statement; per-d numeric tables verify nothing about the tail of d
entries.witness_verifier: n/a; for any fixed d, computing all p(a,d) and the count exceeding (1+c) phi(d) log d is a cheap sieve (A226521 triangle), exploratory only
entries.notes: Erdős [Er49c] proved the statement for an infinite sequence of d, and complementarily that for any eps > 0, p(a,d) < eps phi(d) log d holds for ≫_eps phi(d) residues (per erdosproblems #971 remarks). The heuristic mean of p(a,d)/(phi(d) log d) is ~1; the question is a uniform fat-upper-tail claim. No bounded record quantity: numeric d-sweeps (say all d ≤ 1e6 with several c values) would be cheap supporting data only.

entries.quantity: existence of the asymptotic constant c(f) in Sum_{n<=X} tau(f(n)) ~ c*X*log X for irreducible f in Z[x] of degree >= 3
entries.witness_object: none — the question is whether an asymptotic exists for every irreducible f; no finite computation or object certifies or improves it
entries.witness_verifier: none exists; any finite partial-sum table (A147807 b-file has 10^4 terms for the solved quadratic n^2+1 case) is consistent with both outcomes
entries.notes: Order of magnitude is settled: Sum tau(f(n)) >> X log X (van der Corput 1939) and << X log X (Erdős 1952, elementary), so only the existence of the limit constant is open, and only for deg f >= 3. All irreducible quadratics are solved (Hooley 1963; McKee 1995/1997/1999 give the constants), e.g. Sum_{n<=x} tau(n^2+1) = (3/pi) x log x + O(x). A147807 tracks the SOLVED quadratic case (partial sums of tau(n^2+1)/2, c = 3/(2*pi) per Eldar's 2023 OEIS comment) — it is a sanity harness, not a frontier. No bounded quantity with a movable record exists here.

checked: OEIS A147807 (JSON API, incl. Eldar 2023 asymptotic comment)

entries.quantity: f(n)/n where f(n) = largest m such that every convex n-gon has a vertex with >= m distinct distances to the other vertices (Erdős 1946 conjecture: f(n) = floor(n/2))

lower.source: Nivasch–Pach–Pinchasi–Zerbib, J. Comput. Geom. 4(1):1–12 (arXiv:1207.1266)

upper.source: regular n-gon construction (Erdős, conjectured exact)

entries.witness_object: a convex n-gon with exact rational (or algebraic) coordinates in which EVERY vertex determines fewer than floor(n/2) distinct distances — this would disprove the Erdős conjecture and lower the upper bound below the regular polygon
entries.witness_verifier: dependency-free: convexity via O(n) exact cross-product signs; per vertex, sort n-1 squared distances in exact arithmetic and count distinct values; total O(n^2 log n)
entries.notes: Lower-bound chain (proof-only, not witnessable): ceil(n/3) Moser 1952 -> floor(n/3+1) Erdős–Fishburn 1994 -> ceil((13n-6)/36) Dumitrescu 2006 -> (13/36 + 1/22701)n - O(1) NPPZ 2013, still current per Sheffer's distinct-distances survey (arXiv:1406.1949) and the problem page (last ed. Oct 2025). The stronger 1946 variant (a vertex with no 3 equidistant co-vertices) is FALSE, so naive attack routes are closed. Witness lane is disproof-shaped: conjecture is widely believed true, so grade hard; but the verifier is trivial, making any claimed counterexample cheap to audit. A004526 = floor(n/2) is just the target formula, not data.

entries.quantity: largest N such that every prime 3 <= p <= N has a prime primitive root q < p (verified range of the conjecture)

lower.source: self-verified 2026-07-18 from OEIS A002233 b-file (T. D. Noe, first 10^4 primes): max least prime primitive root over primes 3..104729 is 107, always < p

entries.witness_object: extension certificate: for each prime p in (current N, new N], a prime q < p together with the prime factorization of p-1 and the checks q^((p-1)/r) != 1 (mod p) for every prime r | p-1
entries.witness_verifier: per prime, verify the factorization of p-1 by multiplication plus primality of its factors, then k modular exponentiations; compact, dependency-free, spot-checkable certificates (only exceptional primes with large least prime primitive root need storing — max is 107 below 10^5)
entries.notes: Edge convention: for p=2 no prime q<2 exists; read for p >= 3 (Guy UPINT F9 phrases it for large p; the stub's Lean form uses q <= p). Literature frontier is well beyond my certified floor: Paszkiewicz–Schinzel (Math. Comp. 71:1307–1321, 2002) tabulated least prime primitive roots and Paszkiewicz (Math. Comp. 78:1193–1195, 2009) computed least primitive roots for ALL p < 10^12 (per Moree's Artin survey arXiv:math/0412262, sect. on g(p)=h(p)); I could not pin the exact certified range of the PRIME-primitive-root tables, so lower is the conservative self-verified 104729 — a librarian pass on P–S 2002 should lift it toward ~10^12. A meaningful new record means N > 10^12 (~3.6e10 primes to process): days-scale but standard, hence plausible not open-easy. Theory side: Heath-Brown 1986 — at least one of {2,3,5} is a primitive root for infinitely many p; full statement is Artin-conjecture-hard, no finite resolution.

checked: OEIS A002233 + b-file (10^4 terms, self-verified against sympy primes)
Moree, Artin's primitive root conjecture survey arXiv:math/0412262 (pp. 50 extract)

entries.quantity: infinitude of {n : phi(n) = phi(n+1)} (the set tabulated by A001274)
entries.witness_object: no finite witness can certify infinitude; an individual solution n is trivially checkable (two totient evaluations) but moves nothing on the headline question
entries.witness_verifier: n/a for the headline; for any claimed solution: factor n and n+1, compute phi of each, compare — seconds
entries.notes: Search frontier is FRESH: 42999 solutions below 10^15, exhaustive (Jud McCranie, OEIS comment dated Jan 27 2026); b-file has 42499 terms. Only count-type theory known: #{n<=x} <= x/exp((log x)^{1/3}) (Erdős–Pomerance–Sárközy 1987); reciprocal sum in (1.4325, 7.8358) (Kinlaw–Kobayashi–Pomerance, Acta Arith. 196:69–92, 2020); heuristic a(n) ~ C n^3 log n (Ordowski, OEIS). Adjacent record-shaped cheap-witness targets for the scout (verified by a few factorizations each): (i) a SECOND consecutive pair — only (5186,5187) gives phi(n)=phi(n+1)=phi(n+2) below 10^15 (Lal–Gillard/Firoozbakht question); (ii) a third counterexample to the 'all composite terms are 2 or 3 mod 6' pattern (known exceptions 3044760173455 and 127872660090175). Neither resolves #1003 but both are publishable OEIS-level records.

checked: OEIS A001274 (JSON, incl. McCranie Jan-2026 10^15 comment)
Kinlaw–Kobayashi–Pomerance, Acta Arith. 196 (2020) via OEIS links

entries.quantity: A386893(101) — first OEIS-uncomputed value of f(n), the Mayer–Erdős run-length: largest m such that any two Farey fractions of order n at index distance <= m are similarly ordered (table currently ends at n=100)

lower.source: f(n) >= (n/12)(1 - 4/n^(1/3)) — van Doorn, arXiv:2509.00121 (via OEIS formula section)

upper.source: f(n) < n/4 + 5 for all n >= 4 — van Doorn, arXiv:2509.00121 (via OEIS formula section)

entries.witness_object: for claimed value v: the violating pair of Farey fractions at index distance v+1 (with (a_k - a_l)(b_k - b_l) < 0) plus a full sliding-window scan of F_n confirming no violation at distance <= v
entries.witness_verifier: generate F_n by the mediant/next-term recurrence (integer-only), scan windowed pairs for the similarly-ordered predicate; O(|F_n| * f(n)) with |F_n| ~ 3n^2/pi^2. Verifier validated in this mining pass: reproduces a(4)..a(60) from the b-file exactly and computes a(101) in milliseconds
entries.notes: MINER RESULT: a(101) = 27, computed and double-checked here, matching van Doorn's conjectured exact formula f(n) = floor(n/4) + d, d = (1,2,2,4) for n = (0,1,2,3) mod 4, n >= 92. The real lane is bulk: extend the table to n ~ 10^4-10^5 hunting a counterexample to the exact formula (any mismatch is publishable and would kill the conjectured c = 1/4 form), plus a free OEIS b-file extension beyond n=100. Headline asymptotic gap (proof-only): (1/12 - o(1))n <= f(n) <= n/4 + O(1) (van Doorn 2025; history: Mayer 1942 f -> infinity, Erdős 1943 f >> n). CAUTION: the problem page (accessed 2026-07-18) flags a claimed solution posted in comments, not yet incorporated — librarian should re-check status before major effort.

checked: own computation: verifier reproduces a(4..60); a(101)=27

entries.quantity: m(38) — minimum number of edges of a pancyclic graph on 38 vertices; first open cell after Griffin's exact table (hub quantity h(n) = m(n) - n)

lower.source: 4 chords admit at most 2^5 - 1 = 31 cycles (Shi, Discrete Math. 133, 1994) < 36 required lengths, so >= 5 chords, i.e. m(38) >= 38 + 5; argument as in Griffin, arXiv:1312.0274

upper.source: m(37) = 42 exact (Griffin, arXiv:1312.0274, Table 1) plus m(n+1) <= m(n) + 2 (Griffin, Prop. 2)

entries.witness_object: a pancyclic graph on 38 vertices with 43 edges (Hamiltonian cycle + 5 chords), shipped with one explicit cycle per length 3..38
entries.witness_verifier: positive side is solver-free: check each of the 36 cycle certificates is a valid cycle of the stated length against the edge list, O(n) per cycle. Nonexistence side (m(38)=44): exhaustive 5-chord enumeration over chord-endpoint configurations up to dihedral symmetry (~10^8 configs), each checked via Shi's at-most-2-cycles-per-chord-subset enumeration (<= 63 cycles per graph)
entries.notes: A genuine two-value cell: m(38) is 43 or 44, decided either way by a bounded search (Griffin ran the analogous 5-chord exhaustion to 31 vertices on 2013 hardware; his Figure-1 construction with 5 chords tops out at exactly n=37, which is why the table stops there). Griffin 2013 gives m(n) exactly for all n <= 37: m(n) = n+5 for 25 <= n <= 37, agreeing with George–Marr–Wallis. OEIS A105206 is STALE — it ends at a(22) = 26 while exact values to n=37 have been published since 2013; the b-file extension n=23..37 (values 27,28,30,31,...,42) is a free micro-contribution. The hub's asymptotic question (is h(n) >= log_2 n + log_* n - O(1)? Bondy 1971 window log_2(n-1) - 1 <= h(n) <= log_2 n + log_* n + O(1)) is a wall, but every table cell is machine-decidable and each new cell probes which side of the window m(n) tracks. Sanity: h(38) in {5,6} vs log_2(37) - 1 ~ 4.2.

checked: arXiv:1312.0274 full PDF (Griffin, Table 1 read directly, m(20..37) transcribed)
OEIS A105206 + b-file (20 terms, matches Griffin for n<=22)
web search: no post-2013 extension of m(n) found (2025 papers are edge-pancyclic, a different quantity)

entries.quantity: divergence of R(k)/(k*2^(k/2)) as k -> infinity (Erdős: $100 for proof, $1000 for disproof)
entries.witness_object: none — divergence of a limit cannot be certified by finitely many colorings; explicit lower-bound colorings for specific R(k) belong to the record lanes of problems 77/986, not here
entries.witness_verifier: none exists
entries.notes: Hub status: wall — correctly so. Known window: R(k) >= (1+o(1))(sqrt(2)/e) k 2^(k/2) (Spencer 1975, still the best lower-order constant; Erdős–Szekeres 1935 gave k*2^(k/2) << R(k) <= binom(2k-1, k-1)) vs upper R(k) <= 3.7992^(k+o(k)) (Gupta–Ndiaye–Norin–Wei arXiv:2407.19026, 2024, optimizing Campos–Griffiths–Morris–Sahasrabudhe arXiv:2303.09521's (4-eps)^k breakthrough). Any lower bound of the form k*2^(k/2)*omega(k) with omega -> infinity resolves the problem — 50 years without improving even the constant. The (sqrt 2)^k-vs-3.8^k chasm is the wall; nothing witness-shaped lives here.

entries.quantity: liminf R(k+1,k)/R(k,k) — Erdős–Sós: is it > 1 + c for some absolute c > 0?
entries.witness_object: none for the limit — finite Ramsey data cannot certify an asymptotic ratio; specific off-diagonal R(k+1,k) record constructions belong to problems 986/544/1014 lanes
entries.witness_verifier: none exists
entries.notes: Known: R(k+1,k) - R(k,k) >= k - 2 trivially; best is the ADDITIVE bound R(k+1,k) - R(k,k) >= 2k - 5 (Burr–Erdős–Faudree–Schelp 1989) — hopelessly far from the multiplicative 1 + c; per the problem page (last ed. 2026-03-23) even R(k+1,k) - R(k,k) > k^c for some c > 1 is unknown. Small-k data (from A059442/A000791, consistent but probative of nothing): R(4,3)=9 vs R(3,3)=6; R(5,4)=25 vs R(4,4)=18; R(5,5) in [43,46] vs R(5,4)=25. Both known asymptotic tools (Spencer-type lower bounds, CGMS/GNNW upper bounds) are too coarse to separate R(k+1,k) from R(k,k) multiplicatively.

checked: OEIS A000791 (JSON, incl. Angeltveit R(3,10)<=41 note)

entries.quantity: Number of known unitary perfect numbers (terms of A002827: k with usigma(k) = 2k)

lower.source: Wall 1975 (Canad. Math. Bull. 18, 115-122, fifth unitary perfect number); OEIS A002827 lists exactly five terms; erdosproblems.com/1052 confirms five known (accessed 2026-07-18)

entries.witness_object: A sixth unitary perfect number: an even integer k, distinct from 6, 60, 90, 87360, 146361946186458562560000, with usigma(k) = 2k where usigma(k) = prod(p^a + 1) over the prime-power factorization of k
entries.witness_verifier: Factor k (or accept claimed factorization + primality certificates), compute usigma(k) = prod(p^a+1), check equality with 2k. Pure bigint arithmetic. Sanity-tested locally 2026-07-18: all five known terms pass.
entries.notes: $10 prize (Carlitz/Erdos/Subbarao per Guy B3); Subbarao offered 10 cents per new example. No odd unitary perfect numbers exist (proven; all UPNs even). Constraints on a sixth: Frei 1978 - if not divisible by 3, then divisible by 2^m with m >= 144, >= 144 distinct odd prime factors, and > 10^440. Goto 2007: a UPN with omega(m)=k satisfies m < 2^(2^k). Fifth term found 1975; 50 years without a sixth. Freshness: web search 2026-07-18 found no new discovery. Upstream marks the finiteness question as not resolvable by finite computation.

checked: local sympy verification of usigma(k)=2k for all five known terms

entries.quantity: Largest k for which a k-perfect number (sigma(n) = kn) is known

lower.source: Flammenkamp Multiply Perfect Numbers database (2 known abundancy-11 MPNs among 5932 known MPNs, snapshot 2022-12-31, page updated 2023-01-12); erdosproblems.com/1053 remark confirms largest attained k = 11 (accessed 2026-07-18)

entries.witness_object: A 12-perfect number: n with sigma(n) = 12n, presented via its complete prime factorization (expected to have >= 142 distinct prime divisors if even)
entries.witness_verifier: Given n = prod p_i^{e_i}: verify primality of each p_i (certificates), compute sigma(n) = prod((p_i^{e_i+1}-1)/(p_i-1)), check sigma(n) = 12n. Dependency-free bigint arithmetic; verification is cheap even at thousands of digits.
entries.notes: The actual Erdos question (Guy B2): for k-perfect n, must k = o(log log n)? Asymptotic wall - but the record ladder is the largest attained abundancy, stuck at k=11 since ~2001. RH-conditional bound (A007539 comment, Greathouse): least n-fold perfect > exp(exp(n/e^gamma)), so a 12-perfect has >= ~10^366. Guy also reports the suggestion that only finitely many k-perfect numbers with k >= 3 may exist. Related table A007539 (least n-fold perfect) has b-file through n=8 (a(8) ~ 8.27e132); minimality of the largest entries rests on census completeness, which Flammenkamp calls extremely probable only for abundancy <= 7 - treat a(8) as least-known. Freshness: search 2026-07-18 found no 12-perfect discovery; PrimeGrid-adjacent efforts ongoing.

checked: Flammenkamp MPN page wwwhomes.uni-bielefeld.de/achim/mpn.html (abundancy census table)

entries.quantity: Asymptotics of f(n) = A167485(n) = least m such that n equals the sum of the k smallest divisors of m: is f(n) = o(n) (everywhere / almost everywhere)? Is limsup f(n)/n infinite?
entries.witness_object: none - all three open parts (formal-conjectures 1054.lean parts i-iii) are asymptotic statements not settled by any finite certificate
entries.witness_verifier: n/a. A soft evidence lane exists: record values of f(n)/n (largest observed ~4.5 at n=59, f=265, within OEIS data; each value certifiable by checking every m' < f(n) fails, O(n * f(n)) divisor-sum work) would support limsup = infinity, but no finite record bounds anything.
entries.notes: Guy B2. Proven: f undefined exactly at n=2 and n=5 among small n (Lean textbook theorems in formal-conjectures 1054.lean); OEIS comment conjectures 2 and 5 are the only zeros, which would follow from a strengthened Goldbach statement (every even N > 22 is p+q with 5 < p < q < 5p). Robert Israel: liminf f(n)/n = 0 via f(sigma(n)) <= n. Computed range: b-file to n=20000 (Israel), f(n)/n plotted to 10^6 (OEIS link) - extending these is easy but moves no bound.

checked: OEIS A167485 (JSON API, comments incl. Israel 2024 additions)
formal-conjectures FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/1054.lean (Apache-2.0, fetched from GitHub raw)
Guy UPiNT B2 (via OEIS reference line)

entries.quantity: A005113(20) - least prime in class 20 of the Erdos-Selfridge classification (class via largest-class prime factor of p+1)

lower.source: = 2*a(19) - 1 with a(19) = 65994286610033 (OEIS A005113 data); inequality a(r+1) >= 2*a(r) - 1 proven in A005113 comment (M. F. Hasler)

upper.source: M. F. Hasler, OEIS A005113 comment (Apr 2007); = 2q-1 with q = 382138355312827; p and q verified prime locally 2026-07-18 (sympy); the class-19 status of q rests on Hasler's 2007 computation, not independently re-verified

entries.witness_object: A prime p < 764276710625653 with a full recursion tree certifying class exactly 20: the factorization of p+1, the class of each odd prime factor (max must be 19, attained), recursing down to class-1 leaves (q+1 having only prime factors 2 and 3)
entries.witness_verifier: Recursively factor q+1 for every prime q in the tree (all <= 7.7e14: trial division + Pollard rho, milliseconds each); class(q) = 1 + max class over odd prime factors of q+1; base case: q+1 is 3-smooth. Entire certificate replays in seconds in plain Python.
entries.notes: Underlying open problem (Guy A18, Erdos 1977): are there infinitely many primes in each class, and does p_r^(1/r) tend to infinity (Erdos) or stay bounded (Selfridge)? Known: number of class-r primes <= n is n^(o(1)). Structure for search: any class-20 prime p < 7.6e14 has p+1 = t*q with q a class-19 prime (q >= a(19) = 6.6e13) and even t <= 11, so the bottleneck is enumerating class-19 primes below 3.8e14 - recursive, days not hours. Data ends at a(19) (19 terms, keyword more, no b-file); both the a(20) cell and tightening [1.32e14, 7.64e14] are records. Freshness caveat: bounds are 2007 OEIS comments; no newer source located.

checked: OEIS A005113 (JSON API; data through a(19); Hasler 2007 comments)
local sympy primality checks of 764276710625653 and 382138355312827; arithmetic check 2*a(19)-1

entries.quantity: A060427(15) - least prime p admitting 15 consecutive intervals of integers each with product congruent to 1 mod p (also: existence frontier k=15 of the for-all-k conjecture)
entries.witness_object: A prime p and 16 indices b_0 < b_1 < ... < b_15 < p with all b_i! congruent mod p - equivalently 15 consecutive intervals (b_{i-1}, b_i] each of product 1 mod p. Any such p upper-bounds a(15) AND settles existence for k=15.
entries.witness_verifier: Compute b_i! mod p by sequential modular multiplication (O(p) word operations) or multiply out each interval; check the 16 residues are equal. Dependency-free; the interpretation was sanity-checked locally against the published k=2 (p=11) witness.
entries.notes: Guy A15. Existence known: k=2 (Erdos, 1979 letter: intervals 3-4, 5-7 mod 11), k=3 (Makowski 1983 mod 17) per erdosproblems 1056 remarks - but OEIS A060427 already extends verified existence to k=14 (a(14) = 10428007), far beyond what the problem page records. Search = prefix-factorial residue multiplicity: a(n) is the least prime with some residue hit >= n+1 times by j! mod p. a(13)=2374649, a(14)=10428007 (ratio ~4.4) suggest a(15) within 10^8-10^9; O(p) per prime makes the exhaustive scan ~10^15-10^16 ops - GPU-days, exact value falls to the same bounded scan. Empirically a(n) is nondecreasing but no lower bound for a(15) is proven, so lower is left null. Noll-Simmons generalization: arbitrarily many equal factorials mod p. erdosproblems page has 9 comments including claimed partial results - check before attacking.

checked: local check: factorial-residue multiplicity interpretation reproduces the mod-11 k=2 witness

entries.quantity: A006931(40) - least Carmichael number with exactly 40 prime factors

lower.source: trivial: a Carmichael number is odd and squarefree, so one with 40 distinct prime factors is at least the product of the 40 smallest odd primes (3*5*...*179, ~1.49e70; computed locally 2026-07-18)

entries.witness_object: A squarefree N = p_1*...*p_40 (40 distinct primes) satisfying Korselt's criterion p_i - 1 | N - 1 for all i, smaller than any previously tabulated 40-factor Carmichael number (explicit upper witnesses exist in Alford-Grantham-Hayman-Shallue 2014 data covering every factor count 3..19565220, but no specific value was verified here, so upper is left null)
entries.witness_verifier: Given the factor list: primality of each p_i, distinctness, product equals N, and (N-1) mod (p_i - 1) = 0 for all i. Bigint-only, milliseconds.
entries.notes: The linked Erdos problem 1057 is the counting asymptotic C(x) = x^(1-o(1)) for Carmichael numbers - an analytic wall, not witness-movable. Bounds per erdosproblems 1057 remarks (page edited 2026-01-16): C(x) > x^0.3389 (Lichtman 2022), improving x^0.33336704 (Harman 2008) and x^(2/7) (Alford-Granville-Pomerance 1994); C(x) < x*exp(-c log x * logloglog x / loglog x) (Erdos 1956), which Pomerance 1989 heuristically matches. The OEIS cell a(40) is the adjacent computable record only. Exact values a(36)-a(39) were computed by Shallue-Webster (arXiv:2506.09903, 2025-26; a(39) ~ 2.475e81 in b-file, found by Butler undergrads Johnson & Webster, added to OEIS Jan 2026) - the lane is ACTIVE; read that paper before spending compute, a(40) may already be in progress or listed there.

checked: arXiv:2506.09903 (Shallue-Webster, cited via OEIS links; not read in full)
local computation: product of 40 smallest odd primes

entries.quantity: Infinitude of Erdos primes: primes p with p - k! composite for every k with 1 <= k! < p (A064152)
entries.witness_object: none for the infinitude question - individual Erdos primes are plentiful (b-file has 10000 terms; 5901409 below 10^9 per OEIS comment census) and finding more settles nothing
entries.witness_verifier: n/a for the open question. Single-prime membership IS cheaply checkable (for each k with k! < p, test p - k! for compositeness via BPSW), but it bounds no quantity.
entries.notes: Guy A2 (3rd ed., p. 11). Heuristic count #E(x) ~ (1/8) x / log x (OEIS comment, fitted to census through 10^9) strongly suggests infinitude, i.e. positive relative density among primes - which is exactly why new examples carry no information. Erdos's suggested easier variant (per erdosproblems 1059 remark): show infinitude of n in (l!, (l+1)!] whose prime factors all exceed l and with n - k! composite for 1 <= k <= l; still not witness-shaped.

entries.quantity: A212490(6) - least m such that k*sigma(k) = m has exactly 6 solutions (the record ladder for f(n) = A327153, the count Erdos asked to bound)

lower.source: Michel Marcus & David A. Corneth, OEIS A212490 comment; independently consistent with a local exhaustive sieve over k <= 10^7 (complete for m <= 10^14) finding max multiplicity 5, and exactly reproducing a(5) = 1584858562560 with k = {624960, 640080, 696384, 708660, 713232}

upper.source: Daniel Suteu, OEIS A212490 comment (constructed from products of perfect numbers times powers of 2)

entries.witness_object: An integer m < 7089671638182002688000 with six listed k_1..k_6 each satisfying k_i*sigma(k_i) = m, plus an exhaustiveness certificate (sieve of all k <= sqrt(m)) showing no seventh solution - 'at least 6' alone still records if paired with the smallest-known-exactly-6 bookkeeping
entries.witness_verifier: At-least-6: factor each k_i, compute sigma, multiply - trivial. Exactly-6: bounded sieve of k*sigma(k) for k <= sqrt(m) (~8.4e10 at the current upper bound; blocked divisor sieve + disk-bucketed collision count, heavy but bounded and replayable).
entries.notes: Erdos problem 1060 proper (Guy B11) asks whether f(n) = #{k : k*sigma(k) = n} is <= n^(o(1/loglog n)), maybe (log n)^O(1) - analytic wall. The machine lane is A212490: known a(1)..a(5) = 6, 336, 333312, 5418319872, 1584858562560 (each ~ products of perfect numbers times 2-powers); every new cell is also evidence that f is unbounded, itself unproven. Adjacent cells are open too, some with SMALLER upper bounds than a(6): Suteu gives a(7) <= 106345074572730040320 (~1.06e20) and bounds for a(8), a(9); Corneth: conditional pushes to 1.1e17 / 1.8e20 for the lower side; Suteu conjecture a(n) <= 2^(n-1) * prod_{k<=n} A000396(k). Corneth's Pari program is linked in the OEIS entry. exact_feasibility graded cell on the strength of the bounded sqrt(m) sieve, but flag: if a(6) sits near the upper bound the collision sort is ~670GB of keys - big-box territory, not laptop.

checked: local exhaustive numpy sieve k <= 10^7 (2026-07-18): reproduced a(4) = 5418319872 and a(5) = 1584858562560; max multiplicity 5 for m <= 10^14

entries.quantity: S(x) = number of ordered pairs (a,b) with a+b <= x and sigma(a) + sigma(b) = sigma(a+b): is S(x) ~ c*x for some c > 0?
entries.witness_object: none - asymptotic counting question; finite S(x) values (computable by partial-summing A110177, b-file to n = 16384) bound nothing
entries.witness_verifier: n/a
entries.notes: IMPORTANT FRESHNESS FLAG: a June 2026 preprint claims to RESOLVE this problem negatively - Eric Li (Trinity College, Cambridge), 'A resolution of Erdos Problem 1061 on the sum-of-divisors function', arXiv:2606.25849 (submitted 2026-06-24), proving S(x)/(x (log x)^R) -> infinity for every R > 0, refuting the ~c*x guess. The preprint is linked from OEIS A110177 but is NOT peer-reviewed, and erdosproblems.com/1061 still shows OPEN as of 2026-07-18 (comment activity pending). Recorded as open per upstream, but the scout should spend NO compute here and watch for the upstream status flip; if Li's proof stands, mark closed. Guy B15.

entries.quantity: A038372(45) - largest subset of {1,...,45} in which no element divides two other distinct elements (fork-free sets)

lower.source: = a(44) = 30 (OEIS A038372 data, 44 terms); monotone since any valid subset for n is valid for n+1

upper.source: = a(44) + 1: deleting 45 from an optimal subset of [1..45] leaves a valid subset of [1..44], so a(45) <= a(44) + 1

entries.witness_object: A subset S of {1..45} with |S| = 31 such that no a in S divides two distinct other elements of S (pins a(45) = 31); if the exhaustive/ILP search proves none exists, a(45) = 30 - either way the cell closes
entries.witness_verifier: For each a in S count multiples of a among S \ {a}; require count <= 1. O(|S|^2) integer divisibility checks, dependency-free, instant.
entries.notes: a(45) is boxed into {30, 31}; a 45-variable ILP/SAT solves it in seconds, and the whole table (data stops at n=44, no b-file) extends by dozens of exact cells in hours - among the cheapest genuine table records in the hub. The underlying Erdos problem (Guy B24): how large is f(n), and is lim f(n)/n irrational? Interval (n/3, n] gives f(n) >= ceil(2n/3); Lebensold 1976: 0.6725 n <= f(n) <= 0.6736 n for large n (stated as a theorem in formal-conjectures 1062.lean and on the problem page) - tightening THAT density gap is a separate, analytic/LP-flavored lane; new exact cells sharpen the empirical density toward the irrationality question. erdosproblems 1062 has 13 comments with claimed partial results - check before attacking the density constants.

formal-conjectures FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/1062.lean (Apache-2.0; lower-bound and Lebensold theorems)

entries.quantity: A389360(60) = n_60 — least n >= 120 such that n-i divides binomial(n,60) for all but exactly one i in 0<=i<60 (Erdős–Selfridge quantity n_k at the first uncomputed k)

lower.source: definition (n_k >= 2k by problem statement / Lean formalization domain)

upper.source: witness m = 60*lcm(2..59) (Cambie-type construction noted on erdosproblems.com/1063); machine-verified this session: binomial(m,60) divisible by m-i for all i=1..59 and not by m (exactly one exception, i=0)

entries.witness_object: an integer m in [120, 5.82e26] such that m-i | binomial(m,60) for exactly 59 of the 60 indices i in {0..59}; any smaller qualifying m improves the record toward the exact a(60)
entries.witness_verifier: dependency-free Python: compute binomial(m,60) exactly (60 big-int multiplications), trial-divide by m-i for i=0..59, accept iff exactly one non-divisor; milliseconds even for m ~ 1e27
entries.notes: b-file complete and gap-free for k=2..59 (Chai Wah Wu, Oct 2025) — a(60) is the live frontier. Values are erratic: a(57)=218175259280 (~2.2e11), a(59)=4967281530 (~5.0e9); an exhaustive scan to ~1e11-1e12 with modular tricks is days-scale on one box, so exact a(60) is a bounded search (same method as the existing terms). Theoretical: Erdős–Selfridge observed at least one exception index always exists for n>=2k (proof in Monier via [Mo85]); Monier bound n_k <= k! improved by Cambie to k*lcm(2..k-1) <= e^{(1+o(1))k}. Guy UPINT problem B31. OEIS entry created Oct 2025 (Cambie), so freshness risk is low.

entries.quantity: A(x) = number of composite u <= x with u | n!+1 for some n — conjecture A(x) <= x^{o(1)}
entries.witness_object: none for the conjecture — x^{o(1)} is asymptotic with no finite threshold; new terms of A256519 (a composite u with witness exponent n such that u | n!+1) are cheaply verifiable but move no bound
entries.witness_verifier: n/a for the conjecture; per-term check is trivial (compute (u-k)! mod u by modular product, confirm == -1)
entries.notes: Erdős question from discussions with Hardy and Subbarao (Guy UPINT A2). Enumeration state (verified from b-file): 719 composites known, largest a(719) = 99795337, i.e. A(~1e8) = 719 — consistent with very slow (x^~0.36 at this range) growth but of course probing nothing asymptotic. Wilson's theorem makes the prime analogue trivial, which is why only composite u count. Extending the enumeration is open-easy compute (sieve over u, scan n < u) but is data-gathering, not a record. entries.quantity: A186704(14) — minimum number of distinct distances determined by 14 points in the plane (first uncomputed cell of the linked sequence; the hub problem itself asks for f_d(n) asymptotics in fixed dimension d >= 3)

lower.source: max planar j-distance set sizes are 1,3,5,7,9,12,13 for j=0..6 (Erdős–Fishburn, Discrete Math. 160 (1996) for j<=5; Shinohara 2008 uniqueness at j=5; X. Wei, 'A Proof of Erdős-Fishburn's Conjecture for g(6)=13', Electron. J. Combin. 19(4)#P38, 2012), all <= 13, so any 14-point set determines >= 7 distances

upper.source: explicit 14-point triangular-lattice patch with exactly 7 distinct distances, machine-verified this session in exact Eisenstein-integer arithmetic (squared norms a^2+ab+b^2)

entries.witness_object: n-point planar configuration with few distinct distances (rational/Eisenstein coordinates); for the OEIS-extension record: the verified 14-point (also 15- and 16-point) lattice patches with exactly 7 distances
entries.witness_verifier: exact integer arithmetic: represent points as Eisenstein/lattice coordinates, compute all C(n,2) squared distances as integers, count distinct values; no floating point, stdlib only
entries.notes: CROSSREF CLOSURE AVAILABLE: a(14)=a(15)=a(16)=7 all follow from Wei 2012 (+ EF96/Shinohara08 for j<=5) plus lattice constructions verified this session — OEIS still ends at a(13)=6, so extending A186704 (and A131628, whose a(6)=13 already encodes Wei) is a free librarian win in the #241/#552 pattern. First genuinely open cell: a(17) ∈ {7,8} — verified 17-point patch gives <= 8; equals 7 iff a 17-point 7-distance set exists, i.e. iff g(7) >= 17; best known 7-distance construction has 16 points and the classification of maximum planar 7-distance sets is announced as near-term work (arXiv:2510.09800, Oct 2025) — that cell is 'hard' (EF-conjecture-era case analysis). CAVEAT: hub problem 1083 is the d>=3 generalisation (asymptotics: Erdős 1946 n^{1/d} << f_d(n) << n^{2/d}; Aronov–Pach–Sharir–Tardos 2004; Solymosi–Vu 2008 f_3 >> n^{3/5} after Guth–Katz; conjecture f_d(n) = n^{2/d - o(1)} open) — that part is a wall with no cheap witness; the OEIS link is marked 'possible' and is the planar (problem 89) table.

entries.quantity: contact-number problem f_d(n) = max pairs at distance exactly 1 among n points pairwise >= 1 apart; linked sequence A045945 = 3n(3n+1) is the closed-form planar record values
entries.witness_object: none against the linked ledger — A045945 is a closed-form (keyword: easy) sequence with no uncomputed cell; the planar case of the problem is fully closed: Harborth 1974 proved f_2(n) = floor(3n - sqrt(12n-3)) (matching Erdős's triangular-lattice speculation f_2(3n^2+3n+1) = 9n^2+3n)
entries.witness_verifier: n/a for the linked sequence; a d=3 lane witness (n unit-sphere centers with many touching pairs) would verify by exact/interval arithmetic: all pairwise distances >= 1, count of == 1 pairs — but no OEIS ledger for that exists here
entries.notes: Open content is d >= 3 asymptotics, a constants gap not a table: Erdős claimed c_1,c_2 > 0 with 6n - c_1 n^{2/3} < f_3(n) < 6n - c_2 n^{2/3}; best proven upper bound f_3(n) < 6n - 0.926 n^{2/3} for n >= 2 (Bezdek–Reid 2013); general d: (d-o(1))n <= f_d(n) <= 2^{O(d)} n (lattice lower bound, kissing-number upper bound); survey: Bezdek–Khan 2018 (contact numbers for sphere packings). A potential future records lane: exact maximum contact numbers c(n) for small n in R^3 (sticky-sphere clusters) — would need a new ledger, the hub's linked sequence does not cover it (link marked 'possible'). See also hub #223 for the diameter-1 analogue.

checked: OEIS A045945 (JSON entry, closed form, b-file 0..1000)
erdosproblems.com/1084 (context only, paraphrased: Harborth [Ha74b], Bezdek–Reid [BeRe13], Bezdek–Khan [BeKh18])

entries.quantity: A186705(22) = u(22) — maximum number of unit-distance pairs among 22 points in the plane (first open cell of the linked planar table; hub problem is the general-d unit-distance count)

lower.source: known constructions, catalogued as previously-known lower bounds in Alexeev–Mixon–Parshall, arXiv:2412.11914, Theorem 1(b) (constructions lineage: Schade 1993; Ágoston–Pálvölgyi; Engel et al. 2024 Moser-ring search)

upper.source: Alexeev–Mixon–Parshall, 'The Erdős unit distance problem for small point sets', arXiv:2412.11914, Theorem 1(b) (2025 revision; previous best upper bound was 72)

entries.witness_object: a 22-point planar configuration realizing 61 unit distances (would close the cell at 61): graph6 code of the 61-edge graph plus an exact algebraic embedding of the vertices
entries.witness_verifier: certified arithmetic: given exact algebraic (or high-precision interval) coordinates, check each claimed unit pair has squared distance exactly 1 and count 61 such pairs among C(22,2); interval arithmetic with algebraic certification suffices, no proprietary tools
entries.notes: One-edge gap: 60 <= u(22) <= 61 (AMP Thm 1(b)); AMP's pipeline (forbidden-subgraph graph generation + custom algebraic embedder) obtained exact u(n) for all n <= 21 (u(16..21) = 41,43,46,50,54,57, matching OEIS data) and full enumerations of densest graphs, but left 22..30 as gaps: u(23) in [64,66], u(24) in [68,72], u(25) in [72,78], u(26) in [76,84], u(27) in [81,90] (lower = Hamming(3,3) graph), u(28) in [85,96], u(29) in [89,103], u(30) in [93,110]. Deciding u(22) is finite via their method but exceeded their 2024-25 budget — grade the exact cell as heavy expert compute. FRESHNESS: OEIS entry edited May 2026 confirms AMP is still current for small n; the 2026 asymptotic developments (disproof-of-unit-distance-conjecture remarks arXiv:2605.20695; Sawin explicit lower bound arXiv:2605.20579) concern large-n growth, not these cells. CAVEAT: hub 1085 is the general-d problem — even d >= 4 is essentially closed (Brass 1997 d=4 exact; Swanepoel 2009 even d >= 6 exact; Erdős–Pach 1990 odd d >= 5 up to constants); the open content is d=2 (this table + asymptotics) and d=3 (n^{4/3}loglog n << f_3 << n^{3/2}beta(n)); OEIS link marked 'possible' (it is problem 90's planar table).

arXiv:2412.11914 full text (Theorem 1 tables read directly)

entries.quantity: A003458(378) = g(378) — Erdős–Selfridge function: least n > 379 such that the least prime factor of binomial(n,378) exceeds 378 (first cell beyond the verified literature frontier k=377)
entries.witness_object: any integer n > 379 with lpf(binomial(n,378)) > 378 upper-bounds g(378); smaller qualifying n = better record; the exact value additionally needs the sieve certificate that no smaller n qualifies
entries.witness_verifier: Kummer's theorem, dependency-free: p | binomial(n,k) iff adding k and n-k in base p produces a carry; so for each of the 74 primes p <= 378 check every base-p digit of 378 is <= the corresponding digit of n (no carries). Pure integer arithmetic, instant even for n ~ 1e31
entries.notes: FRESHNESS CATCH (verified in primary source): the OEIS b-file stops at k=375 (a(375)=12863999653788432184381680413559) but the Sorenson–Sorenson–Webster arXiv v3 addendum (arXiv:1907.08559v3, June 2021, supplementing their ANTS XIV paper, Open Book Ser. 4 (2020) 23) reports g(376)=7778804220120654420924631668091 (one week wall time) and g(377)=5973303871796437264595936954237 (~two weeks) — so a(378) is the true frontier and adding 376-377 to OEIS is a free librarian win. Expected magnitude ~1e31; SSW's sublinear-in-g(k) sieve is the tool (CRT/knapsack-constructed admissible residues + wheel), so exact a(378) is weeks-scale expert compute, and producing a non-minimal upper-bound witness via their residue construction is much cheaper. Asymptotics on the hub problem: k^{1+c} < g(k) <= exp((1+o(1))k) (Ecklund–Erdős–Selfridge 1974); record lower bound g(k) >> exp(c(log k)^2) (Konyagin 1999); ELS 1993 conjecture direction exp(ck/log k); SSW heuristic log g(k) ≍ k/log k; EES conjectured g(k) < lcm(1..k) for large k. See also hub #451, #1094.

entries.quantity: g(k) = max over squarefree n with omega(n)=k of tau_perp(n), where tau_perp counts coprime consecutive-divisor pairs — exponential growth base

lower.source: Erdős–Simonovits (see Erdős [Er81h], via erdosproblems.com/1100 remarks, paraphrased)

upper.source: Erdős–Simonovits (see Erdős [Er81h], via erdosproblems.com/1100 remarks, paraphrased)

entries.witness_object: for a concrete k: a set of k primes whose squarefree product n achieves a large tau_perp(n) — builds a finite-k record table (no OEIS ledger for g(k) exists yet; creating one is the actionable lane) and large-k families would press the asymptotic base
entries.witness_verifier: dependency-free: enumerate all 2^k divisors as prime-subset bitmasks with exact integer products, sort by value, count consecutive pairs whose bitmasks are disjoint (AND == 0). Exact and trivial for k <= 25
entries.notes: The bounds are asymptotic bases with unspecified o(1)/c — recorded as expressions, not numerics; nobody has published a finite-k g(k) table as far as checked. Finite-k values are suprema over infinitely many prime choices but depend only on the realizable order type of the 2^k subset-log-sums, so bounded search per k is plausible for small k (hyperplane-arrangement enumeration); grading exact as unknown. Other parts of hub 1100 are asymptotic and not witnessable: tau_perp(n)/omega(n) -> infinity for almost all n; tau_perp(n) < exp((log n)^{o(1)}); Erdős–Hall 1978 proved max_{n<x} tau_perp(n) > exp((loglog x)^{2-eps}). Trivial: tau_perp(n) >= omega(n), with equality infinitely often. OEIS link A325864 is marked 'possible' and counts sum-distinct subsets of {1..n} (b-file 0..150) — related machinery, not a g(k) ledger. Page flags unincorporated partial-result claims in comments — check before claiming novelty.

entries.quantity: Growth rate of an infinite sequence A of integers such that every element of A+A is squarefree (how slowly can a_j grow?)
entries.witness_object: No finite witness: any finite initial segment says nothing about the required infinite squarefree-sum property or the asymptotic growth rate, so no cheaply checkable object can move the known bounds.
entries.witness_verifier: None exists — the quantity is an asymptotic growth exponent for an infinite sequence; only proofs move it.
entries.notes: Asymptotic-only problem (erdosproblems.com marks it not resolvable by finite computation). Known: every such sequence satisfies a_j > 0.24·j^(4/3) for all j, and a squarefree-termed example exists with a_j < exp(5j/log j) for large j (van Doorn–Tao, arXiv:2512.01087, 2025); Konyagin (Izv. RAN 2004) had earlier a_j >> j^(15/11-o(1)). The finite analogue is problem #1109 (same OEIS A392164), which IS machine-actionable — see that entry. Upper bounds for f(N) in #1109 imply lower bounds here.

entries.quantity: A292528(6) — minimum number of vertices in a triangle-free graph with chromatic number 6 (first unknown cell of the sequence 1,2,5,11,22,?)

lower.source: Goedgebeur, On minimal triangle-free 6-chromatic graphs, J. Graph Theory (arXiv:1707.07581); OEIS A292528 comment

upper.source: Goedgebeur, same paper — explicit triangle-free 6-chromatic graphs on 40 vertices (House of Graphs meta-directory)

entries.witness_object: Adjacency list of a graph on n <= 39 vertices that is triangle-free and has no proper 5-coloring (min-type quantity: a smaller example lowers the upper bound).
entries.witness_verifier: Triangle-freeness: check all O(n^3) triples (trivial). Chromatic number >= 6: encode 5-colorability as SAT over 5n vars and get UNSAT (seconds at n<=40 with any solver; DRAT-certifiable). Fully dependency-free alternative: exhaustive 5-coloring backtracking, still fast at this size.
entries.notes: Bounds 32 <= a(6) <= 40 have stood since 2017; OEIS entry is actively curated (edits Jan 2026 by Greathouse added asymptotics without touching them), and 2026 web check found nothing newer. Witness graded hard because the 33..39-vertex window was already attacked by the domain expert with dedicated triangle-free generators (triangle-free Ramsey-style enumeration); a lucky heuristic/tabu search hit is conceivable but ungraded optimism is banned. Lower-bound side (33) needs exhaustive nonexistence — a Sigma2-type constraint (for-all colorings), not naturally DRAT. Known values: a(4)=11 unique (Chvátal 1974, Grötzsch graph), a(5)=22 with exactly 80 extremal graphs (Jensen–Royle 1995). Asymptotics for the problem's f(n) (max chromatic number of triangle-free graph on n vertices): between (1-o(1)) and (2+o(1)) times sqrt(n/log n) (upper: Davies–Illingworth arXiv:2107.12288; lower: Hefty–Horn–King–Pfender 2025, per erdosproblems.com/1104). Mycielski recursion gives a(n) <= 2a(n-1)+1, so a(6) settles the next rung of a whole ladder.

checked: arXiv:1707.07581 / J. Graph Theory 2020 (web search confirm)

entries.quantity: Conjecture F(n) > n for all n > 115, where F(n) = A194259(n) = number of distinct primes dividing p(1)·p(2)···p(n) (p = partition function); A194260(n) = F(n) - n

lower.source: OEIS A194260 b-file (Heinz first 2000 terms, Resta to 10000); positivity of a(n) for 115 < n <= 10000 re-verified from the b-file by this miner on 2026-07-18 (0 violations)

entries.witness_object: Extension of the exact F(n) table past n = 10000: for each new n, the complete prime factorization of p(n) (list of primes with exponents). Publishing the factorizations is a succinct, independently checkable certificate that extends the verified range of F(n) > n.
entries.witness_verifier: Recompute p(n) exactly (Euler pentagonal recurrence, exact bigint); check the claimed primes multiply (with exponents) to p(n); BPSW/ECPP-test each claimed prime; union with previous primes and count. All steps dependency-free except a bigint library.
entries.notes: Problem asks (a) does F(n) -> infinity (answered: yes, follows from Tijdeman-type results as noted by Schinzel; details in Erdős–Ivić 1990) and (b) is F(n) > n for large n — open. Proven: F(n) >> log n (Schinzel–Wirsing, Proc. Indian Acad. Sci. 1987); every prime divides some p(n) (Ono 2000). Empirically F(n) - n is positive for all 115 < n <= 10000 and reaches 18312 at n = 10000 (b-file, re-verified this session). Extension graded plausible, not open-easy: p(10000) has ~106 digits and each new term needs a full factorization (ECM + GNFS-class effort on stubborn cofactors), days per stubborn term. Asymptotic question itself is a wall — erdosproblems.com marks it not resolvable by finite computation.

entries.quantity: A392342 — integers not the sum of at most 4 cubefull numbers (the r=3 case of: every large integer is a sum of at most r+1 r-powerful numbers). Conjecturally finite with largest term 2039; record = verified search bound

lower.source: OEIS A392342 comment + linked Python program (David Cleaver): no terms beyond 2039 below 10^9

entries.witness_object: Either (i) a pushed exhaustive bound: certificate that every n in (10^9, X] is a sum of <= 4 cubefull numbers (streamable per-n 4-tuples, spot-checkable), or (ii) sensationally, a new non-representable term n > 10^9.
entries.witness_verifier: For a claimed new term n: enumerate cubefull numbers <= n (only O(n^(1/3)) of them, ~1000 at 10^9), form all pair sums (~10^6), hash, and check no pair-of-pairs hits n — minutes, dependency-free. For a range push: verify claimed 4-tuples sum to n and each part is cubefull (min prime exponent >= 3).
entries.notes: Erdős–Ivić (Oberwolfach 1986). r=2 case SOLVED: Heath-Brown 1988 proved every large integer is a sum of at most 3 powerful numbers; A056828 (non-sums of <= 3 powerful) is conjecturally complete at {7,15,23,87,111,119} (Mollin–Walsh 1986), checked to 4x10^7 (Jobling 2001). Open for every r >= 3. r=3 sieve frontier is 10^9 (Cleaver, Feb 2026) — pushing to 10^11+ is a straightforward table-of-cubefull + 4-sum sieve, hours-days on one box, and each new decade strengthens the finiteness evidence. Fresh sibling lane: the r=4 case A392343 (non-sums of <= 5 four-full numbers) has NO recorded finiteness frontier yet — computing its presumed-final term and a verified bound would be a first, not an improvement. The full all-large-n statement is a wall (not finitely decidable).

entries.quantity: A115645(27) — next powerful number (min prime exponent >= 2) expressible as a sum of distinct factorials; 26 terms known, largest = 1401602635449 = 1183893^2

lower.source: OEIS A115645 comment (Amiram Eldar): a(27) > 10^18 if it exists

entries.witness_object: Either a new term — a subset S of distinct factorials with sum(S) > 10^18 powerful — or a pushed exhaustive bound (all subset sums of {1!..k!} up to X checked non-powerful).
entries.witness_verifier: Sum the claimed factorials (exact bigint); fully factor the sum (64–90 bit range: Pollard rho, trivial; up to ~10^25: ECM, still cheap); check every prime exponent >= 2. Dependency-free with any bigint + rho implementation.
entries.notes: Erdős at Oberwolfach 1988, motivated by a question of Mahler. Range push is genuinely cheap: all subset sums of {1!..21!} number only 2^21 ≈ 2·10^6 with max ~5·10^19, and subsets of {1!..25!} are 3.4·10^7 with max ~1.6·10^25 — factoring that many <=84-bit numbers is days on one box, so 10^18 -> 10^24 is within reach (grade open-easy for the verified-range record; an actual 27th term is conjecturally unlikely). The squares-only subcase A025494 is already far deeper: 16 squares known (largest 1401602635449), and any further square must exceed 48! ≈ 1.24·10^61 (Schoenfield 2006, via A014597) — squares need no factorization, hence the deeper range. Theory: Brindza–Erdős 1991 proved for fixed r that powerful sums of r factorials have bounded largest factorial. Convention warning: A115645 treats 0!,1! as equal; A051761 (perfect powers variant, 21 terms) treats them as distinct. Finiteness question itself is a wall.

entries.quantity: A392165(40) — smallest N with f(N) >= 40, where f(N) = A392164(N) = size of the largest S ⊆ {1..N} such that every element of S+S (pairwise sums, including 2a) is squarefree. Table exact through N = 1103 (f(1103) = 39)

lower.value: 39 (f exactly computed through N = 1103; 39th record index = 1103)
lower.source: OEIS A392164 + A392165 (Beregovsky Jan 2026; A392165 comment by Chai Wah Wu)

entries.witness_object: A subset S ⊆ {1..N} with |S| = 40 and every pairwise sum a+a' (including a=a') squarefree — certifies f(N) >= 40 and brackets A392165(40); the exact record index additionally needs exhaustive max-clique confirmation for smaller N.
entries.witness_verifier: For each of the ~820 pairs (a,a') with a <= a' in S, trial-factor a+a' <= 2N to sqrt(2N) and check no square divisor. Milliseconds, fully dependency-free.
entries.notes: Erdős–Sárközy 1987 (Acta Math. Hungar.): log N << f(N) << N^(3/4) log N; best known asymptotics are Konyagin 2004: loglog N (log N)^2 << f(N) << N^(11/15+o(1)) (OEIS formula lines + erdosproblems.com/1109; van Doorn–Tao arXiv:2512.01087 revisits the problem family). Erdős–Sárközy guessed the lower bound is nearer the truth; the problem asks if f(N) <= N^o(1). Machine lane: build the compatibility graph on {1..N} (vertex a admissible iff 2a squarefree; edge iff a+a' squarefree) and run an exact max-clique solver (cliquer-class) — N in the low thousands is hours of compute, extending both A392164 (only 98 terms listed, no b-file) and A392165 (39 record indices, keyword 'more'). A392165 record indices are all odd squarefree (subsequence of A056911, per Wu). This finite table is also the machine surrogate for infinite-version problem #1103.

entries.quantity: Existence of a Sierpiński number (odd k with k·2^n+1 composite for all n >= 1) that has NO finite covering set of primes
entries.witness_object: No cheap witness: 'k is Sierpiński' is itself a universally quantified statement (all n), and 'no finite covering set exists' is a second universal quantifier over covering systems — neither side is finitely checkable for a single candidate.
entries.witness_verifier: None exists. Even the leading candidate cannot be certified: its Sierpiński property is proved via algebraic (Aurifeuillian) factorization plus a partial covering, and the absence of a covering set is only conjectural.
entries.notes: Erdős–Graham 1980 (p.27), also Guy UPINT F13. Sierpiński 1960: infinitely many Sierpiński numbers exist (positive density, via covering systems); smallest is believed to be 78557 (Selfridge). Concrete evidence for YES: m = 734110615000775^4 is a proven Sierpiński number (Izotov, Fibonacci Q. 1995; detailed by Filaseta–Finch–Kozek 2008) whose compositeness for n ≡ 2 mod 4 comes from an Aurifeuillian factorization rather than a covering prime — believed to have no finite covering set. Filaseta–Finch–Kozek revised conjecture: every Sierpiński number is a perfect power or has a finite covering set. A negative answer would imply infinitely many Fermat primes. Adjacent (different-problem) machine lane, noted for the scout: proving 78557 smallest = Seventeen-or-Bust residue; a Proth prime k·2^n+1 for a remaining k is a cheaply verifiable world-class witness, but it belongs to the 'smallest Sierpiński number' question, not #1113.

checked: OEIS A076336 (JSON, incl. Nielsen/Hasler comments on covering-set-free examples)

entries.quantity: Collatz (3x+1) conjecture — every positive integer reaches 1; machine-facing quantity: the exhaustively verified convergence bound

lower.source: Barina convergence-verification project (pcbarina.fit.vutbr.cz, log entry 2025-01-15: all n < 2^71 verified); published rung: Barina, Improved verification limit for the convergence of the Collatz conjecture, J. Supercomputing (2025); earlier 2^68 in J. Supercomputing 77:2681–2688 (2021)

entries.witness_object: No succinct witness: extending the verified range produces no independently checkable certificate — re-verification costs the same supercomputer effort as the original run. (Side records — path/delay records like A006877/A008908 maxima — ARE cheaply verifiable per-n but bound nothing about the conjecture.)
entries.witness_verifier: None for the range record (recomputation only). For a claimed path/delay record n: iterate the map and count steps — trivial, but out of scope of the conjecture's truth.
entries.notes: Hub status: wall — correctly so. Not originally Erdős's problem (Collatz, pre-1952); the $500 figure traces to a 1983 Erdős–Graham conversation reported by Lagarias, not a formal prize offer; Erdős called such problems ones mathematics 'may not be ready for' (Guy UPINT E16 / Lagarias 2016 notes, paraphrased from erdosproblems.com/1135). Theory rungs: at least N^0.84 of integers below N eventually reach 1 (Krasikov–Lagarias 2002); almost-all logarithmic-density convergence (Tao 2019, not re-verified this session — flagged as context not a recorded bound). GPU verification throughput exists (Barina's total CPU->GPU speedup reported at ~1335x), but a fleet-scale re-run to claim a new record without novel algorithmic leverage is not a rational use of the farm. Recommend the scout treat this as a monitor-only board.

checked: Springer J. Supercomputing 2025 article (existence confirmed via search; paywalled)

entries.quantity: Does max_{n<x} d_n·d_{n-1} / (max_{n<x} d_n)^2 -> 0 as x -> infinity, where d_n = p_{n+1} - p_n (are two consecutive record-sized prime gaps asymptotically forbidden)?
entries.witness_object: No witness moves the quantity: it is a limit as x -> infinity, and any finite prime-gap configuration (however striking a pair of consecutive large gaps) is consistent with both answers. No nontrivial proven bound on the ratio was found to record.
entries.witness_verifier: None for the limit. (A claimed record product of consecutive gaps d_n·d_{n-1} is itself cheaply verifiable — certify primality of p_{n-1}, p_n, p_{n+1}, p_{n+2} and compositeness of everything between — but it bounds nothing here.)
entries.notes: From Vaughan's problem collection attribution [Va99, 1.2] per erdosproblems.com/1137 (page carries no partial results — the remarks section is empty, which is itself signal: nothing to be scooped by). Data context: A005250 (record prime gaps) b-file now has 85 maximal gaps, largest listed 1854 (b-file extended by Kehrig, fetched and read this session — exhaustive frontier has moved past the long-standing 2^64 era); A083550 = d_n·d_{n-1} has a 10000-term b-file. Cramér-type heuristics make consecutive near-maximal gaps doubly exponentially rare, favoring YES, but nothing is proven. A candidate scout lane — tabulating record values of A083550 and their positions from extended gap tables — is cheap and publishable as OEIS data, but honestly bounds nothing about this problem; graded not-gap-shaped.

checked: OEIS A005250 (JSON + b005250.txt downloaded, 85 terms, last 1854)

entries.quantity: A039669 — integers n > 2 with n - 2^k prime for every k >= 1 with 2^k < n; known terms {4, 7, 15, 21, 45, 75, 105}, conjecturally complete (Erdős). Record = verified completeness bound

lower.source: OEIS A039669 comment (Max Alekseyev): no other terms below 2^120; earlier published bound 2^44 (Mientka–Weitzenkamp 1969, per erdosproblems.com/1142)

entries.witness_object: Either (i) a new term: an integer n > 2^120 with n - 2^k prime for all 2^k < n — a bombshell, cheaply verifiable; or (ii) a pushed completeness bound with a compressed sieve certificate (covering residue classes n mod m each killed by a fixed prime p | n - 2^k, plus per-exception composite factors).
entries.witness_verifier: For a claimed term n: at most log2(n) ≈ 120+ primality checks of n - 2^k (BPSW screen + ECPP certificates) — minutes, dependency-free. For a range certificate: check each covering class algebraically (p | n - 2^k for n ≡ r mod m) and verify the finite exception list's factors.
entries.notes: Erdős 1950 (Summa Bras. Math.); Guy UPINT A19; [Va99, 1.7]. Erdős conjectured the seven known terms are all (OEIS comment), so the new-term witness likely doesn't exist; the honest record is the completeness bound, and 2^120 (2011, unpublished-in-journal OEIS comment by Alekseyev) already reflects clever covering/sieve work — beating it is graded hard, not because verification is hard but because the low-hanging sieve range is spent. Vaughan 1973 proved the count of such n <= N is at most N·exp(-c·(logloglog N / loglog N)·log N). Erdős's stronger related conjecture (see problem #236): the number of k with n - 2^k prime is o(log n). Curiosity preserved from OEIS: Mientka–Weitzenkamp's 1969 claim of 9 terms below 20000 presumably counted 1 and 2 (Israel 2015 comment). Infinitude/completeness question itself is a wall.

entries.quantity: Verification frontier m_max for the conjecture: if n <= 2^m then g(n) <= g(2^m), where g(n) = A000001(n) = number of groups of order n (attributed variously to Erdos and Graham Higman; Question 22.16 in Blackburn-Neumann-Venkataraman 2007)

lower.source: Conjecture verified for all n <= 2^m with m <= 11: recomputed this session from the OEIS A000001 b-file (Small Groups Library, n <= 2047; max on each range [1,2^m] attained at 2^m for m <= 10; a(1024)=49487367289 per Burrell, Comm. Algebra 2021, doi:10.1080/00927872.2021.2006680) plus g(2048) > 1774274116992170 (exact count of order-2048 groups of exponent-2 class 2, Eick-O'Brien, reported in Conway-Dietrich-O'Brien, Math. Intelligencer 2008), which exceeds max_{n<2048} g(n) = g(1024) ~ 4.95e10

entries.witness_object: None cheap. A counterexample would be an order n <= 2^m with g(n) > g(2^m); but exact values of g beyond the enumerated range (n <= 2047) — including g(2048) itself — require full isomorphism-class enumerations (multi-year expert computations in GAP/Magma), and there is no succinct certificate for a claimed group count.
entries.witness_verifier: None exists: verifying a claimed g(n) value means re-running a p-group descendant/enumeration computation at Small-Groups-Library scale; not dependency-free, not cheap, no known short certificate for either direction.
entries.notes: erdosproblems.com/1160 marks this open and 'cannot be resolved with a finite computation'. Known partial result (paraphrased from the page's remarks): Pantelidakis [Pa03, 2003] proved the conjecture when n is odd and m >= 3619. BNV07 Question 22.18 proposes the stronger conjecture sum_{n<2^m} g(n) <= g(2^m) for all sufficiently large m (perhaps m >= 7); from the b-file, sum_{n<2^m} g(n) <= g(2^m) FAILS for small m (e.g. m=4: sum=35 > 14) so 'sufficiently large' is essential there. Extending the verified range to m=12 requires g(4096) — far past the enumeration frontier (g(2048) already unknown; only the class-2 lower bound is known). Scout action: none realistic; watch for new Small Groups Library / gnu(n) computations (Konovalov's crowdsourced gnu database on GitHub tracks n < 50000).

entries.quantity: A008407(343) = H(343) — minimal diameter a_k - a_1 of an admissible 343-tuple (Elliott-Erdos A(k), Erdos 1980 p.108); k=343 is the first k where the minimal diameter is unconfirmed (exhaustively confirmed through k=342)

lower.source: Rigorous lower bound H(343) >= 2334 recorded on the Polymath8 wiki page 'Finding narrow admissible tuples' (used to strengthen their partitioning bounds; derived from Engelsma's exhaustive computations). Weaker but independently checkable floor: Engelsma's exhaustive enumeration of ALL maximum-density patterns through interval width 2331 (opertech.com, 2009) rules out diameter <= 2330, and mod-2 admissibility forces even diameter, giving >= 2332.

upper.source: Engelsma's explicit admissible 343-tuple of width 2341 (diameter 2340), opertech.com k-tuples table (last update Sep 2009; archived snapshot verified). Still the record: Sutherland's merged narrow-admissible-tuples database (math.mit.edu/~primegaps) lists k=343 -> 2340, Engelsma, not marked optimal, and the OEIS A008407 comment (Kehrig, Mar 2025) confirms terms past a(342)=2328 are best-known only.

entries.witness_object: 343 integers 0 = b_1 < b_2 < ... < b_343 = D with D <= 2338 (D even), such that for every prime p <= 343 at least one residue class mod p contains none of the b_i (admissibility; only p <= k need checking)
entries.witness_verifier: ~15-line dependency-free Python: sieve primes p <= 343; for each, compute {b_i mod p} and assert its size <= p-1; assert 343 distinct sorted values, b_1=0, and b_343 <= 2338. Runs in milliseconds. New-record protocol: submit tuple to Sutherland's database + correct A008407.
entries.notes: The Erdos/Elliott problem proper is asymptotic and not finitely resolvable: known (1/2+o(1))k log k <= A(k) <= (1+o(1))k log k (lower Elliott 1965, rediscovered by Polymath8; upper attributed to Davenport, via the k smallest primes > k; refinements Hensley-Richards 1973); the open question is whether A(k) ~ k log k. This entry is its machine-actionable shadow cell. Only slack values {2334, 2336, 2338} remain (diameter is even). Feasibility calibration: Engelsma's cell-by-cell exhaustive assembler search (years) set the table, and the 2013-14 Polymath8 tuple toolchain (greedy/Schinzel/ILP; improved Engelsma at ~1927 values of k) did NOT improve k=343 — so an improving witness is graded hard, though a focused modern SAT/ILP attack on widths 2335-2339 has never been tried specifically. Nonexistence side is pigeonhole-like (cardinality 343-of-~2339 + per-prime coverage), so DRAT certificates may blow up; exact closure realistically means Engelsma-style exhaustion just past his 2009 frontier. Related sequences: A023193 = rho*(n) (max admissible tuple in interval of length n; Engelsma: rho*(3159) >= 447 > pi(3159) = 446, so the prime k-tuples conjecture contradicts pi(x+y) <= pi(x)+pi(y) — see problem 855); A135311 = greedy admissible sequence (Erdos also asks its growth; terms 1..10000 in OEIS); B(k) = min-mean variant with expected B(k) ~ (1/2)k log k.

entries.quantity: A193838(15) — smallest k such that 15 points of the k x k lattice grid have pairwise distinct mutual distances (the Erdos-Guy 1970 finite grid shadow of problem 1208's asymptotic quantity F_d(n)); first uncomputed cell, a(14)=18 exact

lower.source: A271490(17) = 13 < 15 (Cariboni, OEIS A271490 extension, Jul 2022): no 15-point distinct-distance subset fits in a 17x17 grid; equivalently monotonicity from the exact value A193838(14) = 18 (Cariboni 2022)

upper.source: Explicit 15-point distinct-distance configuration in a 20x20 grid (Cariboni, OEIS A193838 comment, Jul 2022; improves the Al Zimmermann Point Packing contest-derived bound of 21 reported by Kamenetsky, Apr 2021)

entries.witness_object: 15 lattice points (x_i, y_i), 0 <= x_i, y_i <= k-1 with k in {18, 19}, whose C(15,2) = 105 squared distances (integers) are pairwise distinct
entries.witness_verifier: 5-line dependency-free Python: compute the 105 integer squared distances, assert len(set(...)) == 105 and all coordinates within the grid. Instant. A k=19 witness sets A193838(15) <= 19; a k=18 witness also lifts A271490(18) to >= 15.
entries.notes: Erdos problem 1208 proper (Er57b; Er80 p.110) asks for the asymptotics of F_d(n) = largest subset with pairwise distinct distances guaranteed in ANY n points in R^d — not finitely resolvable. Known (paraphrased from erdosproblems.com/1208 remarks): n^{1/3}/(log n)^{1/3} << F_2(n) << n^{1/2}/(log n)^{1/4} (lower Charalambides 2013 [Ch13]; upper from the integer grid, which has << n/sqrt(log n) distinct distances); for d >= 3, Thiele 1995 gave F_d(n) >> n^{1/(3d-2)}, improved by Conlon-Fox-Gasarch-Harris-Ulrich-Zbarsky 2015; F_1(n) ~ n^{1/2} (Komlos-Sulyok-Szemeredi 1975, see problem 530). Grid version (this OEIS pair): Erdos-Guy 1970 showed the max distinct-distance subset of the d-dim grid with n points is >= n^{2/(3d)-o(1)} and <= n^{1/d}; Lefmann-Thiele 1995 improved the lower bound to >> n^{2/(3d)}. Cell history: a(13)=16 Dobbelaere 2020, a(14)=18 Cariboni 2022 — exact values fell to exhaustive DFS with distance-set pruning, so the a(15) decision at k=19 is the next ring of the same bounded search (witness may not exist if a(15)=20). Known upper-bound ladder beyond: a(16) <= 22, a(17) <= 24, a(18) <= 27 (Cariboni 2022); a(19..26) <= 33,36,37,40,43,46,49 (Kamenetsky 2021, Zimmermann contest). Dual cell A271490(18) >= 14 (from the a(14)=18 witness), exact value open; A271490 entry carries keyword 'changed' with a Jul 2026 edit but terms still end at a(17)=13 (b-file checked this session).
